topics.title: Desire

glossaryTerms.definition: The fundamental, innate drive in human beings for something more, stemming from a sense of incompleteness. It is the engine of human action and the starting point for the search for meaning and God.
The philosophical and theological concept that human beings are not self-sufficient. We have a built-in sense of being fragmented or incomplete, which gives rise to our desires.
The dual nature of desire, which can be either creative and life-giving ('nourishing') or destructive and harmful ('killing'), often compared to the properties of fire.
A strong, intense form of desire. While a powerful motivator, it becomes dangerous when driven by the ego rather than by a life-giving purpose.

In this context, the part of the self concerned with self-preservation, validation, and control. An unchecked ego can distort pure desires, turning them into selfish or harmful pursuits ('ego projects').

A state of inner restlessness, dissatisfaction, or unease with one's life. Theologically, this is seen not as a negative state but as a positive spiritual signpost, pointing toward a deeper, unfulfilled desire for God or ultimate meaning.

A good desire, such as love, that has been warped into a harmful form. Examples include obsession, where the other person is treated as an object, and overdependence, where one's identity is lost in the other.

A Christian spiritual approach, founded by St. Ignatius of Loyola, which holds that our deepest, most authentic desires are pointers to God's will for us. It is based on the belief that God actively desires a relationship with humanity.
The foundational premise of the course that theological reflection begins with concrete human experience, specifically the universal experience of desire and longing for meaning.
The dichotomy illustrating desire's ambivalence. Creative desire leads to growth, connection, and life ('nourishes'), while destructive desire leads to obsession, harm, and fragmentation ('kills').

entries.content: I felt pressured into pursuing medicine even though deep down I didn't really want to. The idea of pursuing medicine wasn't my own.

I'm afraid of what the biggest things in the past to become a doctor it takes at least 10 years from pre med to medical school residency to board exams almost 10 years of intense studying and that's still before actual appointment.
I don't want to still be financially dependent on my parents at that stage. I don't want that she carrying me and it should be kind of encouraged.
There are the stories we always hear about my residents busy birthdays, choosing hospitality over family events, being unable to be physically present while in challenges in their lives.

So one day I finally asked my parents why [they wanted me to pursue medicine]. It was because we wanted to have a stable community career even after you pass which is true without a doubt being in healthcare is one of the most stable in demand.

A student reflects on taking their boisterous, joyful family home for granted, realizing only after entering college and seeing friends and family move away that the simple moments of laughter and conversation are what make life worth living.

What makes my life worth living are those simple moments... It is not always about the individual milestones that make my life moving, but rather any small pieces of joy which I improve everyday living.
The noise that once overwhelmed me now feels precious because it reminds me that I was not alone.
I realized that what I truly long for the future is not success in the traditional sense, but permanent joy.
You can kiss your family and friends goodbye and go miles to see you. At the same time you carry them with you... Because you've not just lived in a world, but a world that's true.
If change is inevitable and distance is unavoidable, what gives life meaning? Making our effort to make room for joy again.
The whole point is to find joy in whatever configuration of life we find ourselves in.
Our desires always remind us of our existential lack or our existential incompleteness. They remind us of how fragmented we really are.
Passion, driven by desire, can be egocentric. It becomes dangerous when our ego is the ultimate reason for what we do.
The command to 'love your neighbor as yourself' implies you must first truly love yourself. Otherwise, love for a neighbor can become an ego project or people-pleasing to prove you are lovable.
I am not sure if this is a retreat from responsible engagement or a search for a transcendence of that macabre nightmare described in Archibald MacLeish’s poem “The End of the World.”

So let's begin with a tough question.

entries.content: Then, at age twenty-six, I came home one night after work and turned on the television set. After graduation, I had taken a job with General Electric but was beginning to grow dissatisfied with the work. After six years of working late at night and on the weekends, I had also started to develop stress-related stomach problems and was wondering how much more I could take.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: On television that night was a documentary about Thomas Merton, a man who had turned his back on a dissolute life to enter a Trappist monastery in the early 1940s. Something about the expression on his face spoke to me: his countenance radiated a peace that to me seemed unknown, or at least forgotten. The show was so interesting that the next day I purchased and began reading Merton's autobiography, The Seven Storey Mountain. Gradually, I discovered within myself a desire to do something similar to what Thomas Merton had done; maybe not join a monastery (since I'm too talkative) but somehow lead a more contemplative, more religious, life.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

topics.review:
### The Human Starting Point: Unpacking Desire

**The Big Idea: Why Desire Matters**

In the landscape of theology, our journey of understanding God and ourselves begins not with abstract doctrines or ancient texts, but with a deeply personal and universal human experience: desire. Desire is the fundamental engine of human life, the force that propels us out of bed in the morning and shapes the trajectory of our existence. It is the felt awareness of an inner incompleteness, a persistent longing for something more. This is why it serves as the “Human Starting Point” for our theological inquiry. To understand our desires is to begin to understand who we are, what gives our lives meaning, and what we ultimately seek. Our desires, in their purest and even their most conflicted forms, are the clues that can lead us toward the transcendent, toward God.

**The Universal Experience of Desire**

At our core, we are “desiring beings.” The absence of desire is often linked to a depressive state, a lack of drive that signifies a kind of spiritual or psychological stagnation. Desire is the animating force that moves us beyond our comfort zones, motivating us to learn, to create, to connect, and to grow. The source material provides a rich tapestry of these desires, ranging from the grand to the mundane: the student who feels parental pressure to pursue a stable medical career despite their own lack of desire; the longing for the simple, joyful chaos of a family home after moving away for college; the list of personal goals including “Baki strength,” mastering a video game, learning Japanese, and achieving a successful career.

These examples reveal a crucial truth: we desire what we feel we lack. As the notes state, “We want what we can't have / don't have in full / used to have.” The student pressured into medicine desires autonomy and a life not defined by a decade of intense study and financial dependence. The student away from home desires the lost intimacy and connection of family life, realizing that “what makes my life worth living are those simple moments.” Our desires are constant reminders of our “existential lack” or “existential incompleteness.” They show us that we are not self-sufficient beings, but are fundamentally oriented towards something or someone outside of ourselves for our fulfillment.

**The Ambivalent Nature of Desire: A Double-Edged Sword**

A central theme in this topic is the profound ambivalence of desire. It is not inherently good or bad; its moral quality depends on its object and its orientation. The lectures use the powerful analogy of **fire**. Like fire, desire is a potent force that can be either creative or destructive. It can provide warmth, light, and energy for cooking and creation—it can be life-giving. This is desire channeled into passion that “nourishes,” moving us toward love, community, and self-actualization. However, fire can also rage out of control, consuming and destroying everything in its path. This is desire that “kills,” leading to harm, isolation, and fragmentation.

We are, as the notes suggest, “people of conflicting desires.” The same desire for love can lead to a healthy, life-giving relationship or become a distorted obsession or overdependence. The desire for success can fuel hard work and innovation or it can devolve into a toxic, egoistic pursuit that tramples on the welfare of others. The challenge, therefore, is not to extinguish our desires, which would be to extinguish our very vitality, but to learn how to channel them in creative and life-giving ways. This requires self-awareness, discernment, and an ethical framework.

**Desire, the Ego, and Authentic Love**

The greatest danger in our life of desire comes from the ego. When passion is driven by an egocentric motive, it becomes a destructive force. The ego, with its need for validation, control, and self-preservation, often “plays tricks on us.” For example, a good desire, like the desire for knowledge, can become toxic when the ego twists it into a desire for absolute control. Similarly, the innate drive for self-preservation is good and necessary, but it becomes ethically problematic when it comes at the cost of others’ welfare. 

This is powerfully illustrated in the theological command to “love your neighbor as yourself.” The source notes astutely point out that this implies you must first truly love yourself. Without a healthy self-love, which accepts one's own goodness and worth, any attempt to love a neighbor can become a disguised “ego project.” It can manifest as people-pleasing to prove one's own lovability or as a performance of righteousness, rather than a genuine act of connection. The ego distorts the desire for connection into a desire for self-gratification.

**Desire as a Doorway to the Divine**

While our desires can be messy and conflicted, they are also our primary point of contact with the divine. The persistent feeling of “dis-ease” or dissatisfaction we experience in life is not a sign of failure, but a spiritual clue. It points toward a deeper desire that cannot be satisfied by finite things. The student who realizes their deepest longing is not for “success in the traditional sense, but permanent joy” is on the verge of a theological discovery. They are recognizing that material achievements and individual milestones are insufficient to fill the void of our existential lack.

The story of Fr. James Martin, S.J., and Thomas Merton serves as a perfect case study. Martin, successful by worldly standards at General Electric, was nonetheless dissatisfied, suffering from stress and a sense of unease. This was a desire for something more, a desire for peace. Seeing a documentary on the Trappist monk Thomas Merton, Martin was struck by the “peace that to me seemed unknown” on Merton's face. This encounter awakened a deeper desire within him, not necessarily to join a monastery, but to lead a “more contemplative, more religious, life.” His worldly dissatisfaction was the starting point that opened him to a transcendent desire. This illustrates a core tenet of Ignatian spirituality: our deepest, most authentic desires are clues to God's will for us. Furthermore, the Ignatian way is founded on the belief that God first *desires* to be in a relationship with us. Our searching and longing for God is ultimately a response to God’s prior and more powerful search for us.

**Conclusion: From Desire to Faith**

Understanding desire is the essential first step in the theological journey of this course. It establishes the human condition as one of inherent longing and incompleteness. This sense of lack is what propels us to ask the big questions about meaning, purpose, and existence, leading us to contemplate the difference between a **Problem and a Mystery** and to cultivate a sense of **Wonder**. Our desires shape which of the **Six Paths** we might take to approach the divine. When our desires are healthy and well-ordered, they can lead to authentic **Faith** and **Spirituality**. Conversely, when our desires are distorted by the ego, they lead to **Distorted Images of God** and prevent us from being open to authentic **Revelation**. The entire theological enterprise, in a sense, is an attempt to understand, purify, and orient our deepest human desires toward their ultimate end: union with the source of all goodness, truth, and beauty.
keyTakeaways: Human beings are fundamentally 'desiring beings.' This innate drive, stemming from a sense of 'existential lack,' is the engine of human action and the starting point for theological inquiry.

Desire is ambivalent and is best understood through the analogy of 'fire.' It can be a creative, life-giving force that nourishes, or a destructive, consuming force that kills, depending on its orientation.
Our desires are often conflicting, pulling us in different directions. The central challenge is not to eliminate desire but to learn how to channel it in creative and life-giving ways.

The ego can distort good desires, turning love into obsession, success into ruthless ambition, and connection into people-pleasing. Authentic love of others requires a healthy self-love to avoid becoming an 'ego project.'
Worldly dissatisfaction, stress, and a sense of 'dis-ease' can be important spiritual clues, pointing to a deeper, transcendent desire that finite things cannot satisfy.
The story of James Martin discovering Thomas Merton illustrates how our mundane desires and dissatisfactions can become doorways to a deeper, more religious life and a longing for God.
From an Ignatian perspective, our deepest, most authentic desires are signals of God's will for us. This is rooted in the belief that God first desires a relationship with us.

Our desires often point beyond the tangible. The fact that we desire intangible things like joy, love, meaning, and connection suggests that human fulfillment is found beyond the purely material realm.

glossary.definition: The fundamental, innate drive in human beings for something more, stemming from a sense of incompleteness. It is the engine of human action and the starting point for the search for meaning and God.
The philosophical and theological concept that human beings are not self-sufficient. We have a built-in sense of being fragmented or incomplete, which gives rise to our desires.
The dual nature of desire, which can be either creative and life-giving ('nourishing') or destructive and harmful ('killing'), often compared to the properties of fire.
A strong, intense form of desire. While a powerful motivator, it becomes dangerous when driven by the ego rather than by a life-giving purpose.

In this context, the part of the self concerned with self-preservation, validation, and control. An unchecked ego can distort pure desires, turning them into selfish or harmful pursuits ('ego projects').

A state of inner restlessness, dissatisfaction, or unease with one's life. Theologically, this is seen not as a negative state but as a positive spiritual signpost, pointing toward a deeper, unfulfilled desire for God or ultimate meaning.

A good desire, such as love, that has been warped into a harmful form. Examples include obsession, where the other person is treated as an object, and overdependence, where one's identity is lost in the other.

A Christian spiritual approach, founded by St. Ignatius of Loyola, which holds that our deepest, most authentic desires are pointers to God's will for us. It is based on the belief that God actively desires a relationship with humanity.
The foundational premise of the course that theological reflection begins with concrete human experience, specifically the universal experience of desire and longing for meaning.
The dichotomy illustrating desire's ambivalence. Creative desire leads to growth, connection, and life ('nourishes'), while destructive desire leads to obsession, harm, and fragmentation ('kills').

studyTips: Master the 'fire' analogy for desire. Be prepared to use it to explain how a single desire (e.g., for security, for love, for knowledge) can have both a creative/nourishing outcome and a destructive/killing outcome.

Pay close attention to the distinction between a healthy desire and an 'ego project.' A common exam trap is to confuse genuine altruism with people-pleasing that is secretly designed to make oneself feel lovable.

Use the specific student stories (pressured into medicine, missing family) as your go-to examples. Connecting abstract concepts like 'existential lack' to these concrete narratives will make your answers stronger.
Remember the James Martin/Thomas Merton story as a key illustration of how dissatisfaction (dis-ease) can be a positive spiritual catalyst, leading from a worldly desire to a transcendent one.

Trace the path of desire: It starts with an 'existential lack,' manifests as a specific want (for a career, for joy, etc.), is shaped by the ego, and can ultimately become a doorway to the divine. Understanding this flow is key to the entire topic.
The phrase 'love your neighbor as yourself' is theologically dense. Focus on how it implies that a healthy, non-egotistical self-love is the necessary foundation for authentic love of others.

connections.relationship: Desire often leads us to confront life's great Mysteries. While the desire for a stable career might be a 'Problem' to be solved, the desire for 'permanent joy' or to understand love is a 'Mystery' to be lived in, not solved.
connections.example: A student might try to solve the 'problem' of loneliness by joining clubs, but the deeper desire for connection is a 'mystery' that unfolds over time through relationships and vulnerability.

connections.relationship: When our desires are purified of ego and oriented toward the good, they cultivate a sense of wonder. Instead of desiring to control or possess, we desire to appreciate and stand in awe of creation and others.
connections.example: A desire for knowledge can be about passing an exam, or it can become a sense of wonder at the complexity of linear algebra or the beauty of the natural world, leading to a deeper appreciation of the Creator.

connections.relationship: Our dominant desires often predispose us to one of the Six Paths. A deep desire for order and morality might lead one down the Path of Duty, while a desire for ecstatic connection might lead to the Path of Devotion.
connections.example: The desire for peace and contemplation that James Martin felt drew him toward a path resembling devotion and mysticism, similar to Thomas Merton's.

connections.relationship: Faith is not merely an intellectual belief but a response of the whole person—including our deepest desires—to the divine. Authentic faith involves entrusting our desires, hopes, and longings to God.
connections.example: The student who prayed for guidance about her medical career wasn't just asking for information; she was aligning her desire for a meaningful life with her faith in a God who guides.

connections.relationship: Our unexamined and disordered desires can be projected onto God, creating distorted images. A desire for absolute control can lead to an image of God as a micromanaging puppet master, while a desire to avoid responsibility can lead to an image of a permissive God who makes no demands.
connections.example: If someone's deepest desire is for retribution against those who have wronged them, they might create a 'God of Vengeance' in their own image, ignoring the God of mercy and forgiveness.

connections.relationship: Revelation is God's self-disclosure, and it often speaks directly to our deepest human desires. God's revelation is not just a list of facts but an invitation that answers the longing for love, meaning, and belonging that is at the heart of human desire.
connections.example: The Ignatian principle that 'God desires a relationship with us' is a form of revelation that directly addresses our own deep-seated desire for connection and to be wanted.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain the concept of 'existential lack' and how it relates to the student's story about being pressured into a medical career.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Existential lack is the idea that humans are fundamentally incomplete and constantly desire something more to fill this void. In the story, the student experiences this as a conflict of desires. While there's a desire to please her parents and for the stability medicine offers, her deeper 'existential lack' is a lack of autonomy and authentic purpose, causing her to feel that the path is not truly her own.

examPrepQuestions.question: Using the 'fire' analogy, explain how the desire for love can be both creative and destructive.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Like fire, the desire for love is a powerful force. In its creative form, it provides warmth, connection, and mutual growth, nourishing both individuals. In its destructive form, it can become an obsessive fire that consumes one's identity (overdependence) or seeks to possess the other, turning a person into an object of the ego's desire.

examPrepQuestions.question: How does the story of Fr. James Martin and Thomas Merton illustrate the theological concept of 'dis-ease'?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Theologically, 'dis-ease' is a spiritual restlessness that points to a deeper longing. Fr. Martin's stress and dissatisfaction with his corporate job, despite his success, represented this 'dis-ease.' It was a sign that his worldly life could not satisfy his deepest desires, and it acted as a catalyst, opening him to the possibility of a more contemplative life embodied by Merton.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, what is the primary reason 'Desire' is considered the 'Human Starting Point' for theology?
choices: A) Because desire is the fundamental engine of human life and the source of our search for meaning.
B) Because all desires are sinful and must be overcome to find God.
C) Because theology is primarily concerned with cataloging human wants.
D) Because desire is a modern philosophical concept.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course frames desire as the universal, motivating force that stems from our 'existential lack' and propels our quest for meaning, making it the essential starting point for theological reflection. The other options are incorrect interpretations.

quizQuestions.question: The lecture notes use 'fire' as a primary analogy for desire. What key concept does this analogy illustrate?
choices: A) That desire is always dangerous and should be extinguished.
B) That desire is a warm and comforting feeling.
C) That desire is ambivalent—it can be both creative/life-giving and destructive/harmful.
D) That desire provides the only light for our path in life.
quizQuestions.explanation: The fire analogy highlights the dual nature or 'ambivalence' of desire. It is a powerful force that can either 'nourish' (like a cooking fire) or 'kill' (like a wildfire), depending on its orientation and control.

quizQuestions.question: A student feels an intense need to get the highest grade in the class to feel worthy. According to the lectures, this is likely an example of:
choices: A) A purely creative and life-giving desire.
B) An 'ego project' where the desire for success is distorted by the need for validation.
C) A complete lack of desire, characteristic of a depressive state.
D) A desire that points directly to God's will.
quizQuestions.explanation: When a desire (for success) is tied to proving one's worthiness, it becomes an 'ego project.' The ego has distorted a potentially good desire into a self-validating exercise, rather than a genuine pursuit of knowledge.

quizQuestions.question: What did Fr. James Martin see in Thomas Merton that awakened a deeper desire within him?
choices: A) A life of worldly success and wealth.
B) A countenance that radiated a deep and unknown peace.
C) An argument for why the Trappist life is superior.
D) A list of rules for how to pray.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source material explicitly states that Martin was struck by the expression on Merton's face, which 'radiated a peace that to me seemed unknown.' This peace spoke to Martin's own 'dis-ease' and awakened a desire for a more contemplative life.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of 'existential lack' suggests that human beings are:
choices: A) Fundamentally evil.
B) Completely self-sufficient.
C) Inherently incomplete and oriented towards something more.
D) Lazy and unmotivated by nature.
quizQuestions.explanation: 'Existential lack' or 'incompleteness' is the core idea that we are not whole on our own, which is why we are 'desiring beings' constantly reaching for something beyond ourselves to find fulfillment.

quizQuestions.question: The theological interpretation of the command 'love your neighbor as yourself' implies that:
choices: A) Loving oneself is selfish and must be avoided.
B) One should always prioritize the neighbor's needs above one's own, even to the point of self-harm.
C) A healthy, non-egotistical self-love is the necessary foundation for authentically loving others.
D) Love is a transaction where you give love to receive it from your neighbor.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes highlight that if you do not truly love yourself, 'love' for a neighbor can become a performance or an 'ego project' to gain approval. Therefore, a proper self-love is a prerequisite for genuine, other-centered love.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of a 'distorted love' as described in the notes?
choices: A) A friendship based on mutual respect and support.
B) A parent who provides for their child's needs.
C) An obsessive relationship where one person's identity is consumed by the other.
D) The simple joy of being with family.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes explicitly list obsession and overdependence as 'distortions of love.' These are examples of desire's destructive potential, where the healthy desire for connection becomes a harmful force.

quizQuestions.question: According to the Ignatian perspective discussed, our deepest and most authentic desires are considered to be:
choices: A) Sinful temptations that must be ignored.
B) Random biological impulses with no meaning.
C) Clues or signs pointing to God's will for us.
D) Illusions created by the ego.
quizQuestions.explanation: Ignatian spirituality holds a positive view of desire, believing that our deepest, most authentic longings (for peace, joy, love, purpose) are placed in us by God and can guide us toward the life God wants for us.

quizQuestions.question: The feeling of 'dis-ease'—a general dissatisfaction with life despite outward success—is seen theologically as:
choices: A) A sign of personal failure and weakness.
B) A spiritual catalyst pointing toward a deeper, unfulfilled longing.
C) A medical condition requiring a doctor.
D) Proof that God does not exist.
quizQuestions.explanation: Like the experience of James Martin, 'dis-ease' is interpreted not as a failure but as a positive spiritual signal. It indicates that finite, worldly things are not enough and points us toward a transcendent desire for God or ultimate meaning.

quizQuestions.question: A student who realizes that 'it is not always about the individual milestones... but rather any small pieces of joy' is shifting their desire from:
choices: A) God to worldly things.
B) Tangible achievements to intangible, experiential fulfillment.
C) Family to career.
D) Joy to success.
quizQuestions.explanation: This realization marks a shift in what is being desired. It moves away from external markers of success ('individual milestones') toward an internal, experiential good ('small pieces of joy'), which is a deeper and often more authentic form of desire.

topics.title: Problem vs. Mystery

glossaryTerms.definition: A mental gap or challenge that is external to the observer. It calls for the application of a specific technique to arrive at a definitive, solvable resolution.

A reality that is not external to the observer but involves them personally. Its meaning is inexhaustible, and it does not call for a solution but for engagement, commitment, and deeper participation.
The Latin term for mystery, referring to a reality that is beyond full human comprehension, endlessly full of meaning, and which evokes a sense of awe or the sacred.
A specific method or procedure used to analyze and solve a problem. It is the characteristic tool for dealing with problems, but is inadequate for engaging with mysteries.
The common error of treating a mystery as if it were a problem. This leads to frustration, inadequacy, and a misunderstanding of the nature of the reality being faced.
The proper disposition toward a mystery, involving personal commitment, presence, and participation, rather than detached analysis or problem-solving.
A fundamental shift in one's personal stance toward reality, moving from a desire for control and certainty (problem-solving) to one of openness, intuition, and relationship (engaging mystery).

In the context of God, to 'understand' can imply an impossible attempt to grasp or contain the infinite Divine within the confines of finite human reason. The course suggests the goal is not to achieve this, but to 'misunderstand less'.
A Latin phrase by St. Augustine meaning, 'If you understand, it is not God.' It emphasizes that God, as the ultimate Mystery, can never be fully grasped or contained by the human mind.

entries.content: No matter how corny it is, it does point to a truth. And the truth is that maybe it's good to examine the reason behind my life motions. The reason behind the motions. Why am I doing what I'm doing?

Let's start with the first Latin word, mysterium. When he talks about mysterium, you're talking about something that's beyond your imagination. Something that's always full. Something that's always so meaningful. For Rudolph Otto, experiencing the sacred is experiencing something completely other from yourself.

When you fully know something... it's very easy to take it for granted and discard it. But when you're dealing with something that you can't really understand, somehow it really captures your attention.
Augustine also said: 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus'—'If you understand, if you can categorize, if you can pin down, maybe it's not God.'
The point of theology is not to understand God, but to misunderstand God less. It's about getting rid of misconceptions.
The problem of 'problem vs. mystery' arises when you take the disposition of problem-solving to a situation that does not call for a clear-cut solution but calls you to be more present and open.

There in the starless dark, the poise, the hover,
There with vast wings across the cancelled skies,
There in the sudden blackness, the sudden pall
Of nothing, nothing, nothing—nothing at all.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: In religious and perhaps aesthetic terms, mystery refers to things that cannot be proved or disproved but that nevertheless exert a force on our thinking and our lives.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Examples of mystery include: why, for example, there is anything and not nothing; why matter is relatively consistent in its behavior; why suffering and injustice persist; why life is never quite enough; what follows death.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: What, though, has all this to do with the business of the busy world? Is our world not already sufficiently turbulent without dropping “mystery” into the smoky cauldron? And has not science, once and for all, roasted the old witch over the Bunsen burner?
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Mystery requires not understanding so much as engagement—an existential reorienting of self toward what is not known but intuited.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: A Looming Shadow
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: It is rather strange that half a millennium after Galileo and Newton and, in our own times, Edison, Einstein, Henry Ford and the United Nations, we still find people in poverty, we are still distressed over the loss of everything from youth to global security, still haunted by the tangible nightmares of crime, terrorism and human torture; still dismayed by our growing economic disparity and dwarfed in the looming shadow of our inadequacy to do much about it.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Most of all, we are still humbled in that bone-house of memory where our brightest and most noble efforts to end war and injustice lie, a mockery of their rhetoric, amid the footnotes to our failures. We are compelled to ask, “What have we done wrong—or failed to do?”
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Anna’s King of Siam says, “Is a puzzlement.” The mystic, “It’s a mystery.”
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Mystery, on the other hand, requires not understanding so much as engagement—an existential reorienting of self toward what is not known but intuited...
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Our failures to end war, injustice, poverty and violence have come at a time in the evolution of the cosmos that is most manifest, today, in both the blessings and the destructive capacities our technologies have made possible.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: As catastrophes of the practical imagination, of humanistic idealism unrestrained by historical memory, these failures urge upon us the truth that our institutions, resolutions and technological wonders have failed us and will continue to do so.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: At the very least, it will make everything that comes afterward seem easy by comparison.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Jacque had lived in the same dorm with Brad and me during freshman year. Though wildly different from Brad in outlook and interests, the two became close. After an accounting class one day, standing in a snowfall outside of our old freshman dorm, I told Jacque how angry I was at God, and how I had decided I would no longer go to church. My comments were flung at her like a challenge. You're the believer, I thought, explain this.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: "Well," she said softly, “I've been thanking God for Brad's life.”
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: I can still remember standing in the cold and having my breath taken away by her answer. Rather than arguing about suffering, she was telling me that there were other ways to relate to God, ways other than as the Great Problem Solver.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: She hadn't answered my question about suffering. Rather, her words reminded me that the question of suffering (or the 'mystery of evil' as theologians say) is not the only question to ask about God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: It also reminded me that there are other questions that are equally important—such as “Who is God?" Not being able to answer one question does not mean that others are not equally valid. Her answer opened a window onto another vista of faith.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: That's not to say I ever found an entirely satisfying answer for the mystery of suffering—or for why my friend's life was ended at twenty-one. But it helped me understand the importance of being in relationship with God, even during difficult times.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

topics.review:
Kumusta! Let's get you ready for your Theology 11 final exam. Today, we're focusing on a foundational topic from SET 1: "Problem vs. Mystery." Understanding this distinction is crucial because it shapes how we approach not just theology, but life's most profound questions.

### The Big Idea: Choosing the Right Lens

At its core, the distinction between a "problem" and a "mystery" is about recognizing that not all questions are the same, and therefore, they cannot all be approached in the same way. The fundamental error we often make, both in life and in faith, is applying a problem-solving mindset to a situation that is, in fact, a mystery. This topic teaches us to develop the discernment to know the difference and to adopt the right disposition for each.

A **problem** is defined as a mental gap that seeks a clear-cut solution. It is something external to you, like a broken appliance or a math equation. You can stand apart from it, analyze it, apply a specific **technique** or method, and solve it. Once solved, you can discard it and move on. The goal is to gain control and find a definitive answer. Our modern, scientific, and technological world excels at solving problems.

A **mystery**, on the other hand, is something entirely different. The source material describes it as a "mental gap that invites a person into further understanding since its meaning is inexhaustible." A mystery is not external to you; it is a reality in which you are personally involved. You cannot stand apart from it because you are part of it. Think of love, suffering, freedom, or the meaning of your own life. These are not puzzles to be solved but realities to be entered into. The Latin word, *mysterium*, points to something beyond our full comprehension, something always full and meaningful.

### The Error: When Technique Fails

The central argument is that our culture trains us to be expert problem-solvers. This is valuable, but it becomes a "failure of discernment" when we approach everything as a problem. As one of the readings powerfully illustrates, humanity has made incredible technological and scientific progress (like Galileo, Newton, Einstein), yet we are still "humbled in that bone-house of memory" where our best efforts to solve war, poverty, and injustice have failed. These are not merely technical problems awaiting a better policy or technology. They are deep mysteries of the human condition, entangled with freedom, evil, and the very structure of our being. To treat them as simple problems is to misunderstand their nature and guarantee our failure.

This is the "looming shadow of our inadequacy" mentioned in the notes—the realization that our problem-solving tools are insufficient for life's deepest challenges. A mystery does not call for a solution; it calls for engagement, presence, and an "existential reorienting of self toward what is not known but intuited."

### Theological Application: God, the Ultimate Mystery

This framework finds its ultimate expression in theology. God is the ultimate Mystery, not the ultimate Problem. To approach God as a problem to be solved, categorized, or pinned down is a fundamental misunderstanding. This is the profound insight behind St. Augustine's famous dictum: *'Si comprehendis, non est Deus'*—'If you understand, it is not God.' If you think you have fully grasped or figured God out, what you have grasped is likely an idol of your own making, not the living God. The experience of the sacred, as theologian Rudolph Otto noted, is an encounter with something "completely other," something that defies our categories.

This reframes the entire purpose of theology. As your notes state, "The point of theology is not to understand God, but to misunderstand God less." It is a process of purification, of shedding our distorted images and misconceptions so that we can encounter the Mystery more authentically. We don't seek to possess knowledge about God like we possess the answer to a math problem; we seek to be possessed *by* the Mystery of God.

### Living the Questions: The Personal Dimension

The story of the student whose friend, Brad, died, provides a moving, personal illustration. The narrator, in his grief and anger, approaches God as a "Great Problem Solver," demanding an answer to the "problem of suffering." He flings his question at his friend Jacque like a challenge: "You're the believer, explain this." This is a problem-solving stance.

Jacque's response is a masterclass in engaging a mystery. She doesn't offer a philosophical treatise on evil. Instead, she says softly, "I've been thanking God for Brad's life." This answer takes the narrator's breath away because it doesn't solve his problem; it dissolves it. It reframes the entire situation. It shifts the focus from demanding an answer to the mystery of suffering to embracing the equally profound mystery of gratitude and relationship. Her response opened a "window onto another vista of faith," reminding him that relationship with God is more important than having all the answers.

This is what it means to "live the questions now." A mystery invites you to commit to it, to enter it, to allow it to change you. You only get to know more about it by living within it, not by standing outside and analyzing it. This approach moves us away from a desire for Absolute Certainty and into a disposition of Wonder, which is the true starting point for a genuine relationship with the Divine.
keyTakeaways: A 'problem' is an external challenge that can be solved with a specific technique, leading to a definitive answer.
A 'mystery' is a reality in which one is personally involved; it is inexhaustibly meaningful and is not solved but engaged with through presence and commitment.
The primary intellectual and spiritual error is applying a problem-solving mindset to a situation that is a mystery, which is a 'failure of discernment.'
God is the ultimate Mystery, not a problem to be figured out. As St. Augustine taught, 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' (If you understand, it is not God).
The goal of theology is not to 'understand' God in a controlling sense, but to 'misunderstand God less' by clearing away false or distorted images.
Engaging a mystery requires an 'existential reorientation'—a shift from seeking control and answers to cultivating relationship, presence, and openness.
Profound human experiences like love, death, and suffering are mysteries that invite us to 'live the questions' rather than demanding simple solutions.
Humanity's failure to 'solve' systemic issues like war and poverty demonstrates that these are not just problems, but deep mysteries of the human condition that defy purely technical fixes.

glossary.definition: A mental gap or challenge that is external to the observer. It calls for the application of a specific technique to arrive at a definitive, solvable resolution.

A reality that is not external to the observer but involves them personally. Its meaning is inexhaustible, and it does not call for a solution but for engagement, commitment, and deeper participation.
The Latin term for mystery, referring to a reality that is beyond full human comprehension, endlessly full of meaning, and which evokes a sense of awe or the sacred.
A specific method or procedure used to analyze and solve a problem. It is the characteristic tool for dealing with problems, but is inadequate for engaging with mysteries.
The common error of treating a mystery as if it were a problem. This leads to frustration, inadequacy, and a misunderstanding of the nature of the reality being faced.
The proper disposition toward a mystery, involving personal commitment, presence, and participation, rather than detached analysis or problem-solving.
A fundamental shift in one's personal stance toward reality, moving from a desire for control and certainty (problem-solving) to one of openness, intuition, and relationship (engaging mystery).

In the context of God, to 'understand' can imply an impossible attempt to grasp or contain the infinite Divine within the confines of finite human reason. The course suggests the goal is not to achieve this, but to 'misunderstand less'.
A Latin phrase by St. Augustine meaning, 'If you understand, it is not God.' It emphasizes that God, as the ultimate Mystery, can never be fully grasped or contained by the human mind.

studyTips: Focus on the *disposition* or *attitude* as the key differentiator. A 'problem' elicits a desire for control and technique. A 'mystery' invites presence and engagement. This is a likely exam focus.
Memorize the anecdote about Brad's death. Be able to explain how the narrator's angry question represents a 'problem' mindset and how Jacque's response exemplifies a 'mystery' mindset.
Be able to write out and explain St. Augustine's quote, 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus,' and connect it directly to the idea of God as the ultimate Mystery.
Think of a personal example of a time you treated a mystery (like a relationship conflict or a career choice) as a problem to be solved, and reflect on the outcome. This will solidify the concept.
Pay close attention to the distinction between the goal of Science (to solve problems) and the goal of Theology (to misunderstand mystery less). This contrast is a powerful way to frame the topic.
On the exam, if you see a question about large-scale failures like war or poverty, connect it to this topic by explaining that they are mysteries of the human condition, not just technical problems.

connections.relationship: Our human desire for control and clarity can lead us to mistakenly treat mysteries as problems, seeking simple answers where none exist.
connections.example: Desiring a definitive answer for 'why good people suffer' is an example of applying a problem-solving desire to the mystery of suffering.

connections.relationship: These are the two dispositions that correspond to 'problem' and 'mystery.' The problem-solving mindset seeks Absolute Certainty, while the proper response to a mystery is Wonder and awe.
connections.example: Looking at a starry sky, a problem-solver might try to count the stars. A person in a state of wonder simply gazes in awe, engaging the mystery of the cosmos.

connections.relationship: Treating God as a problem to be solved or as a 'Great Problem Solver' (like a cosmic vending machine) is a primary way we create distorted images of God.
connections.example: Praying to God with the expectation of a specific, guaranteed outcome treats God as a problem-solver, not as a mystery to be in relationship with. This is the 'God as a Fixer' distortion.

connections.relationship: Revelation is not God handing us a 'solution manual' to the problem of existence. It is God's self-communication *within* the Mystery, inviting us into a deeper relationship, not ending the inquiry.
connections.example: The life of Jesus is God's revelation, not as a list of rules to solve life, but as a person to encounter and a way to live within the mystery of God's love.

connections.relationship: The concept that 'God is not a being' (among other beings) reinforces that God is the ultimate Mystery. A 'being' can be objectified, studied, and 'solved' like a problem. Mystery cannot.
connections.example: If God were just a very powerful being, we could theoretically understand Him. The idea that God is Being-Itself places Him in the category of Mystery, fundamentally unknowable in a problem-solving sense.

connections.relationship: Faith is the trust and commitment required to engage with a mystery. It is not blind belief, but the very faculty through which we enter into realities like love and God, which cannot be 'proven' like problems.
connections.example: We don't 'solve' for love; we have faith in a person and enter into a relationship. Similarly, faith is how we enter the mystery of God.

connections.relationship: Rudolph Otto's concept of the sacred as the 'Mysterium Tremendum et Fascinans' (a terrifying and fascinating mystery) is a direct encounter with reality as mystery, not as a problem.
connections.example: Experiencing a profound sense of awe in a cathedral or in nature is an encounter with the sacred, which points to a reality beyond our full grasp—a mystery.

examPrepQuestions.question: In your own words, define the key differences between a 'problem' and a 'mystery' as discussed in the course.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: A problem is an external challenge that can be solved with a technique, and once solved, it is finished. A mystery, however, involves the person's own being and is inexhaustible; it is not meant to be solved but to be engaged with through presence and commitment.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain the meaning and significance of St. Augustine's quote, 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus.'
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The quote means 'If you understand, it is not God.' Its significance lies in framing God as the ultimate Mystery. If we believe we have fully figured out, defined, or understood God, we have likely reduced God to a mental idol and are not dealing with the true, living God who is always beyond our complete grasp.

examPrepQuestions.question: Using the course anecdote about Brad's death, explain how one might wrongly approach suffering as a 'problem' versus rightly engaging it as a 'mystery.'
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Approaching suffering as a 'problem' is what the narrator did by demanding God explain why his friend died. This seeks a clear-cut answer or solution. Engaging suffering as a 'mystery' is what Jacque did; instead of trying to 'solve' the suffering, she reoriented the situation toward gratitude and relationship ('thanking God for Brad's life'), demonstrating presence within the painful mystery.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, what is a 'problem'?
choices: A) A reality whose meaning is inexhaustible.
B) A mental gap that invites a person into further understanding.
C) A challenge that can be solved using a specific technique.
D) An experience that calls for an existential reorientation.
quizQuestions.explanation: A 'problem' is defined as a challenge that is external to the self and can be addressed with a specific technique to find a solution. The other options describe a 'mystery'.

quizQuestions.question: The phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' is attributed to which thinker?
choices: A) Rudolph Otto
B) St. Augustine
C) The King of Siam
D) Albert Einstein
quizQuestions.explanation: St. Augustine of Hippo is credited with this famous saying, which highlights the mysterious and incomprehensible nature of God.

quizQuestions.question: What is described as the 'failure of discernment' in this topic?
choices: A) Refusing to believe in God.
B) Applying a spiritual solution to a technical issue.
C) Treating a mystery as if it were a problem.
D) Dismissing something as false simply because we don't understand it.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core error discussed is the failure to discern the difference between a problem and a mystery, leading one to apply an inappropriate problem-solving mindset to a mystery.

quizQuestions.question: In the anecdote about the death of the friend Brad, Jacque's response ('I've been thanking God for Brad's life') serves to:
choices: A) Provide a clear, logical answer to the problem of suffering.
B) Shift the focus from seeking a solution to being in a relationship.
C) Prove that faith is irrational.
D) Argue that suffering is not real.
quizQuestions.explanation: Jacque's response is a classic example of engaging a mystery. She doesn't solve the 'problem' of suffering but reorients the narrator toward the mystery of gratitude and relationship with God, even in difficult times.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would be best approached as a MYSTERY?
choices: A) Repairing a flat tire.
B) Learning to love another person.
C) Balancing a chemical equation.
D) Following a cooking recipe.
quizQuestions.explanation: Loving another person is a reality you are personally involved in, its meaning is inexhaustible, and it cannot be 'solved' with a technique. The other options are all clear examples of problems.

quizQuestions.question: According to the lecture notes, the point of theology is NOT to understand God, but to:
choices: A) Prove God's existence.
B) Misunderstand God less.
C) Create a perfect society.
D) Disprove other religions.
quizQuestions.explanation: This key phrase emphasizes that theology's goal is to purify our understanding and remove misconceptions about the Mystery of God, rather than to 'solve' or completely comprehend God.

quizQuestions.question: A 'technique' is the appropriate tool for dealing with a...?
choices: A) Mystery
B) Paradox
C) Problem
D) Revelation
quizQuestions.explanation: A technique is a specific method used to find a solution, which is the defining characteristic of how one approaches a problem.

quizQuestions.question: The lecture's discussion of humanity's inability to end war and poverty, despite technological advancement, illustrates that:
choices: A) Technology is inherently evil.
B) These are deep mysteries of the human condition, not just technical problems.
C) We have not yet found the right scientific solution.
D) Only a global government can solve these issues.
quizQuestions.explanation: This example shows that our problem-solving tools (technology, institutions) are inadequate for these deep-seated issues because they are mysteries rooted in human nature, not just logistical problems.

quizQuestions.question: Which disposition is required to properly engage with a mystery?
choices: A) Detached analysis and a desire for control.
B) The application of a proven formula.
C) Presence, engagement, and existential reorientation.
D) An insistence on a clear, immediate answer.
quizQuestions.explanation: Unlike a problem, which calls for control and analysis, a mystery calls for the person to be present and to reorient themselves toward engagement and relationship rather than a solution.

quizQuestions.question: Rudolph Otto's concept of the 'sacred' as something 'completely other' aligns with the idea of God as:
choices: A) A solvable puzzle.
B) A predictable force.
C) An ultimate mystery.
D) A human invention.
quizQuestions.explanation: The experience of something 'completely other' than ourselves, which inspires awe, is the experience of the sacred. This directly aligns with the concept of God as a Mystery that transcends our categories and understanding.

topics.title: Absolute Certainty

glossaryTerms.definition: A state of mind characterized by the conviction of having complete, infallible knowledge. In the course, it is presented as a mental trap where one is not open to new perspectives, driven by a desire for control, and often leading to arrogance and intolerance.

The practice of actively challenging and evaluating the fundamental values—ethical, political, and religious—to which one is committed. It is a constructive means of acknowledging human finitude and preventing the stagnation of one's beliefs.

A perspective attached to the pursuit of absolute certainty that attempts to simplify complex realities (like a person, truth, or God) into simpler, more manageable, and controllable components. It is contrasted with the attitude of wonder, which embraces complexity and mystery.

A form of reductionism characterized by a firm and rigid adherence to a specific set of rules, beliefs, doctrines, or ideologies. It seeks certainty by appealing to an absolute, unchangeable external authority.

A form of reductionism where truth is believed to be ultimately and entirely dependent on the individual. It posits that objective truth does not exist, thereby reducing the concept of truth to individual perspective or feeling.

The philosophical doctrine, articulated by Charles Sanders Peirce, which states that human beings are inherently fallible in their knowledge of factual matters. It asserts that no belief can be supported with absolute certainty, and all knowledge is provisional.

A term used to describe believers whose certainty in their faith makes them arrogant, compassionate, and intolerant. They consciously or unconsciously exclude others who do not share their level of certainty from their social and spiritual world.
A mental trap characterized by overthinking a situation to the point that a decision or action is never taken. It represents a state of being trapped in endless deliberation, leading to inaction.
A mental trap where an individual does not bother to think critically at all. Instead of engaging with questions or ideas, one simply reacts or follows impulses without reflection.

The division of society into two opposing, hostile groups where dialogue breaks down, often fueled by each side's absolute certainty in its own righteousness. The course cites 'DDS vs. Dilawan' as a local example.

entries.content: Certainty prevents some believers from being compassionate, sympathetic, or even tolerant of others who are not as certain in their faith. Their arrogance turns them into the 'frozen chosen,' consciously or unconsciously excluding others from their cozy, believing world.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This essay contains four arguments in defense of the creative role of doubt in religion.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: 1) creative doubt is a means of constructively acknowledging human finitude.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: 4) the avoidance of doubt may reflect a relinquishing of freedom and a dehumanizing quest for certainty.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: A college student approached his professor after class. With anguish he complained, "I don't know whether you know it or not, but this class is painful." The professor had encouraged his students to doubt creatively.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Creative doubt is the activity of challenging and evaluating the fundamental values—ethical, political, and religious—to which one is committed.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: The dialogue Euthyphro illustrates the Socratic attitude, where Socrates reveals Euthyphro's confusion about piety, challenging him to rethink his fundamental ideas even as he prosecutes his own father for an act he believes is pious.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Socrates viewed himself as a gadfly with a god-given task of stimulating men to think, to evaluate critically the fundamental principles in terms of which they were structuring their lives.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: When encouraged to proclaim the truth, Emmanuel responds: "The Truth? The truth, Lazarus, is perhaps something so unbearable, so terrible, something so deadly, that simple people could not live with it!"
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: The following arguments are in defense of stimulating creative doubt in certain contexts, while acknowledging that there are other settings in which such activity would be inappropriate.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Acknowledging human limitations
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: First, creative doubt is a means of constructively acknowledging human limitations.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Charles Sanders Peirce's doctrine of Fallibilism states simply that with regard to matters of fact human beings are fallible; they can never be absolutely certain that they are correct.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Science has made the progress it has precisely because of a methodology that acknowledges limitations and, therefore, encourages reevaluation of conclusions.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Peirce asks, should the philosopher or theologian be less sensitive to his fallibility, less prepared to alter his beliefs if reason and evidence warrant? No knowledge claim, concludes Peirce, is ever absolute but 'always swims, as it were, in a continuum of uncertainty and indeterminacy.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: In 'The Ascent of Man,' Bronowski stands in a pond at Auschwitz where the ashes of millions were flushed, reflecting on what men do when dogmatically certain they are right.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Bronowski pleads with the audience in the words of Oliver Cromwell: 'I beseech you, in the bowels of Christ, think it possible you may be mistaken.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Creative doubt is a constructive expression of the recognition that one is not God and, therefore, just may be mistaken.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: A college student discussing new questions with a family member was interrupted: 'If you just wouldn't think about these kinds of questions, you would be so much happier.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Challenging the quest for certainty
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: The quest for certainty (and the avoidance of doubt) often manifests itself in the effort to find some authority to whom burdensome freedom and responsibility can be surrendered. The easiest way to secure the illusion of certainty is to surrender one's personal freedom and responsibility for making choices.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: This is the crucial point Fyodor Dostoevsky makes in 'The Grand Inquisitor' passage of The Brothers Karamazov, where the Inquisitor accuses Christ of giving humanity the unbearable burden of freedom.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: The Inquisitor argues that men prefer peace and even death to freedom of thought, and have gratefully laid their freedom at the feet of the Church, which assumes responsibility for the choices they cannot stand to make.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: The Inquisitor concludes: 'We have corrected Thy work and have founded it upon miracle, mystery and authority. And men rejoiced that they were again led like sheep, and that the terrible gift [freedom] that had brought them such suffering, was, at last, lifted from their hearts.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: In summary, four arguments have been advanced for creative doubt: 1) it acknowledges human finitude, 2) it keeps beliefs vital, 3) it is a weapon against idolatry, and 4) its avoidance reflects a dehumanizing quest for certainty.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Bertrand Russell maintained that 'to teach how to live without certainty, and yet without being paralyzed by hesitation, is perhaps the chief thing that philosophy, in our age, can still do for those who study it.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: There is something majestic and compelling about the fact that in those areas of life where value is at stake, one must make an uncertain judgment and then act courageously. The very essence of human dignity is the courage to be unsure.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Speech bubble with the text: 100% Legit! On God! On God! On God!
Firm/rigid adherence to fundamentals. This is presented as a response to mystery that comes from a desire for absolute certainty.
The belief that objective truth does not exist. This is presented as another response to mystery.
What you know is limited.
Avoid absolute certainty.
We can't say whether or not there's a God.
To know is to grasp something.
To know is to be able to say what something is or is not.
To know is to have control over something.
Regarding Truth, the stance is that 'we don't know anything!'
You can't fully know something because it is always changing and in flux.
Absolute Certainty
There is no such thing as absolute certainty, with the possible exception of death.
The pursuit of absolute certainty is attached to Reductionism.
Reductionism is a perspective attached to the pursuit of absolute certainty and is contrasted with wonder.
Reductionism
A form of reductionism involving a firm and rigid adherence to a set of rules, beliefs, doctrines and ideologies.
A form of reductionism where truth is believed to be ultimately dependent on the individual.
In relativism, objective truth does not exist.
Relativism represents a reduction of truth to individuals.
Relativism leads to a dissolving of shared values.
The desire for absolute certainty reveals a proclivity towards control.
Thinking in absolutes leads to deadly polarization.
Examples of deadly polarization: Repub vs. Democrats; DDS vs. Dilawan.
You can't reduce somebody by absolutism, as it doesn't capture everything of a person.
Truth doesn't align so neatly, so one should avoid judgmentalism.
Mental traps
A mental trap of overthinking that leads to underdoing.
A mental trap where one is not open to new perspectives and is not truly thinking.
A mental trap where one does not bother to think at all, instead just reacting or doing.
Avoid absolute certainty and perfectionism.
The desire for absolute certainty reveals our desire for control.
Day 5
The problem of relativism is the dissolving of shared values.

topics.review:
This topic, 'Absolute Certainty,' serves as a critical foundation within SET1: The Human Starting Point. It directly confronts a common but deeply problematic human tendency: the desire for complete, unshakable certainty, especially in matters of faith, values, and truth. The central argument is that this quest for absolute certainty is not only a philosophical error but also a spiritual and psychological trap. It can lead to intellectual stagnation, moral arrogance, and even violence. Instead of seeking absolute certainty, the course advocates for a more mature and courageous stance: embracing 'creative doubt' and the humility of human finitude.

At its core, the pursuit of absolute certainty is a desire for control. When we feel we 'know' something absolutely, we feel we have control over it. This is why the notes describe it as a 'mental trap where one is not open to new perspectives.' Such a person is not truly thinking; they are merely reinforcing a closed system. This mindset creates what the lectures call the 'frozen chosen'—believers so secure in their own rightness that they become incapable of compassion, sympathy, or tolerance for those who see the world differently. Their certainty becomes a wall that excludes others from their 'cozy, believing world.' The course uses a powerful, modern Filipino colloquialism to illustrate this posturing: the student who confidently declares something is '100% Legit! On God! On God! On God!', is performing an act of absolute certainty.

The most devastating consequence of this mindset is violence. The lectures point to Jacob Bronowski's reflection in 'The Ascent of Man.' Standing in a pond at Auschwitz where the ashes of millions were flushed, Bronowski observes that such atrocities are what people do when they are dogmatically certain they have a monopoly on the truth. This leads to his impassioned plea, quoting Oliver Cromwell: 'I beseech you, in the bowels of Christ, think it possible you may be mistaken.' This powerful line encapsulates the moral urgency of abandoning the quest for certainty.

As an antidote, the course introduces the concept of 'Creative Doubt.' This is not a cynical or paralyzing skepticism, but rather 'the activity of challenging and evaluating the fundamental values—ethical, political, and religious—to which one is committed.' The model for this is Socrates, who acted as a 'gadfly' to the Athenian state, challenging figures like Euthyphro to critically examine their most basic assumptions. Creative doubt is a constructive process, a way of acknowledging our inherent human limitations. This is formalized in Charles Sanders Peirce's doctrine of 'Fallibilism,' which states that human beings are fallible and can never be absolutely certain about matters of fact. As Peirce argued, if science progresses by constantly re-evaluating its conclusions, shouldn't theology and philosophy be equally open to revision in the face of new evidence and reason? No knowledge claim, he states, 'always swims, as it were, in a continuum of uncertainty and indeterminacy.'

Perhaps the most profound argument against the quest for certainty comes from Fyodor Dostoevsky's 'The Grand Inquisitor' parable in 'The Brothers Karamazov.' The Inquisitor accuses the returned Christ of giving humanity an 'unbearable burden'—freedom. He argues that people do not want the freedom to choose; they want the peace and security of being told what to believe. They crave certainty. The Inquisitor explains that the Church 'corrected' Christ's work by founding it upon 'miracle, mystery and authority,' thereby lifting the 'terrible gift' of freedom from people's hearts. They 'rejoiced that they were again led like sheep.' This illustrates a crucial point: the avoidance of doubt is often a 'dehumanizing quest for certainty' that involves surrendering our freedom and responsibility to an external authority.

Philosophically, the pursuit of absolute certainty is linked to 'Reductionism.' Reductionism is the attempt to simplify complex realities (like a person, truth, or God) into manageable, controllable parts. The course identifies two primary forms of reductionism that arise from this desire:
1.  **Fundamentalism:** A firm and rigid adherence to a set of rules, beliefs, and doctrines. This provides certainty by creating an unchangeable, absolute external authority. It avoids the messiness of mystery by declaring it solved.
2.  **Relativism:** The belief that truth is entirely dependent on the individual and that objective truth does not exist. While it seems like the opposite of fundamentalism, it is also a form of reductionism. It reduces the vastness of Truth to the manageable confines of individual perspective. It provides a different kind of certainty—the certainty that *my* truth is unassailable because it is *mine*. The primary problem with this, as the notes point out, is the 'dissolving of shared values,' making community and ethical consensus impossible.

Both fundamentalism and relativism are attempts to escape the anxiety of uncertainty. They lead to 'deadly polarization' (e.g., DDS vs. Dilawan) because they make genuine dialogue impossible. If I have absolute truth (fundamentalism) or if your truth has no claim on me (relativism), we have nothing to discuss.

In conclusion, this topic positions us at a crucial starting point. It argues that to be fully human and to engage authentically with the divine, we must resist the temptation of absolute certainty. We must learn, as Bertrand Russell suggests, 'how to live without certainty, and yet without being paralyzed by hesitation.' The course concludes that there is a profound dignity in this struggle. Embracing our finitude, questioning our own beliefs, and acting with conviction despite our lack of absolute proof is not a sign of weak faith, but its very essence. 'The very essence of human dignity is the courage to be unsure.'
keyTakeaways: The pursuit of absolute certainty is a spiritual and psychological trap that can lead to arrogance, intolerance (the 'frozen chosen'), and even violence, as exemplified by Bronowski's reflection at Auschwitz.
Creative Doubt is the antidote to absolute certainty. It is the Socratic activity of challenging and evaluating one's own fundamental values to keep beliefs vital and acknowledge human limitations.

The doctrine of Fallibilism, by Charles Sanders Peirce, states that human beings are inherently fallible and can never achieve absolute certainty in matters of fact, encouraging intellectual humility.

Dostoevsky's 'Grand Inquisitor' illustrates that the quest for certainty is often a dehumanizing escape from the 'unbearable burden of freedom,' where people willingly surrender their responsibility to an authority that offers certainty.

The desire for certainty leads to Reductionism, the simplification of complex realities. Its two main forms are Fundamentalism (rigid adherence to external doctrines) and Relativism (reducing truth to individual perspective).
Both Fundamentalism and Relativism are ultimately mental traps that hinder true thinking and lead to 'deadly polarization' (e.g., DDS vs. Dilawan) by making genuine dialogue impossible.
A key problem with Relativism, which claims truth is purely individual, is the 'dissolving of shared values,' which undermines community and ethics.
Mature faith and human dignity do not lie in possessing absolute answers, but in having 'the courage to be unsure'—to make commitments and act courageously without the guarantee of being right.

glossary.definition: A state of mind characterized by the conviction of having complete, infallible knowledge. In the course, it is presented as a mental trap where one is not open to new perspectives, driven by a desire for control, and often leading to arrogance and intolerance.

The practice of actively challenging and evaluating the fundamental values—ethical, political, and religious—to which one is committed. It is a constructive means of acknowledging human finitude and preventing the stagnation of one's beliefs.

A perspective attached to the pursuit of absolute certainty that attempts to simplify complex realities (like a person, truth, or God) into simpler, more manageable, and controllable components. It is contrasted with the attitude of wonder, which embraces complexity and mystery.

A form of reductionism characterized by a firm and rigid adherence to a specific set of rules, beliefs, doctrines, or ideologies. It seeks certainty by appealing to an absolute, unchangeable external authority.

A form of reductionism where truth is believed to be ultimately and entirely dependent on the individual. It posits that objective truth does not exist, thereby reducing the concept of truth to individual perspective or feeling.

The philosophical doctrine, articulated by Charles Sanders Peirce, which states that human beings are inherently fallible in their knowledge of factual matters. It asserts that no belief can be supported with absolute certainty, and all knowledge is provisional.

A term used to describe believers whose certainty in their faith makes them arrogant, compassionate, and intolerant. They consciously or unconsciously exclude others who do not share their level of certainty from their social and spiritual world.
A mental trap characterized by overthinking a situation to the point that a decision or action is never taken. It represents a state of being trapped in endless deliberation, leading to inaction.
A mental trap where an individual does not bother to think critically at all. Instead of engaging with questions or ideas, one simply reacts or follows impulses without reflection.

The division of society into two opposing, hostile groups where dialogue breaks down, often fueled by each side's absolute certainty in its own righteousness. The course cites 'DDS vs. Dilawan' as a local example.

studyTips: Memorize Oliver Cromwell's quote cited by Bronowski: 'I beseech you, in the bowels of Christ, think it possible you may be mistaken.' It perfectly summarizes the moral argument against absolute certainty.

Master the central argument from Dostoevsky's 'Grand Inquisitor.' Be able to explain how the quest for certainty is a desire to escape the 'burden of freedom' by surrendering it for 'miracle, mystery and authority.'

Pay close attention to the relationship between Certainty -> Reductionism -> Fundamentalism/Relativism. Understand how both fundamentalism (absolute external rules) and relativism (absolute internal feeling) are two sides of the same reductionist coin, both trying to eliminate uncertainty.
Connect 'Creative Doubt' to Socrates' metaphor of the 'gadfly.' This will help you remember that its purpose is to be productively annoying—to stimulate critical thinking in oneself and others.
Don't get confused: 'Creative Doubt' is presented as a virtue, not a vice. It is the opposite of paralyzing skepticism or apathetic disbelief. It's an active, engaged process.

Be ready to contrast the 'frozen chosen,' who are paralyzed by their certainty, with the ideal of having the 'courage to be unsure,' which is presented as the height of human dignity and mature faith.

connections.relationship: The desire for absolute certainty is an attempt to treat a 'Mystery' (like God, love, or truth) as a 'Problem.' A problem has a definitive solution and can be controlled, whereas a mystery is something we enter into and can never fully grasp. The pursuit of certainty denies mystery.
connections.example: Trying to 'prove' God's existence with 100% certainty is treating God as a problem to be solved. Engaging with God through faith, despite uncertainty, is entering into the Mystery of God.

connections.relationship: Reductionism, the direct result of seeking absolute certainty, is the explicit opposite of 'Wonder.' Wonder is an openness to reality in its full complexity and mystery, while reductionism seeks to break it down and control it, thereby destroying wonder.
connections.example: A scientist who reduces a sunset to mere wavelengths of light is practicing reductionism. A person experiencing the sunset with awe and 'Wonder' is open to the mystery and beauty that cannot be fully explained.

connections.relationship: The lecture notes explicitly group 'absolute certainty' and 'perfectionism' together as things to be avoided. Both stem from a desire for control and an intolerance for finitude, flaw, and ambiguity. Both are quests to eliminate the messiness of being human.
