entries.content: One strength of this group is a healthy independence that enables them to see things in a fresh way-something that their own religious community often desperately needs. Those on the 'outside,' who are not bound by the usual restrictions on what is 'appropriate' and 'not appropriate' to say within the community, can often speak more honestly.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: It's also a reminder that for those on the path of independence—believers who have left organized religion—the search for a perfect religious community may be one without end.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Disbelief
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Those traveling along the path of disbelief not only find that organized religion holds no appeal (even if they sometimes find its services and rituals comforting), but have also arrived at an intellectual conclusion that God may not, does not, or cannot exist.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The cardinal benefit of this group is that they take none of the bland reassurances of religion for granted. Sometimes they have thought more deeply about God and religion than some believers have.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: They also have a knack for detecting hypocrisy, cant, or lazy answers: a religious-baloney detector. Tell a person in this group that suffering is part of God's mysterious plan and needs to be accepted unquestioningly, and he will rightly challenge you to explain yourself.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The main danger for this group is that they sometimes expect God's presence to be proven solely in an intellectual way.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Return
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This path gets more crowded every year. People in this group typically begin life in a religious family but drift away from their faith. After a childhood in which they were encouraged (or forced) to attend religious services, they find them either tiresome or irrelevant or both. Religion remains distant, though oddly appealing.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Then something reignites their curiosity about God. Maybe they've achieved some financial or professional success and ask, “Is that all there is?” Or after the death of a parent, they start to wonder about their own mortality. Or their children ask about God, awakening questions that have lain dormant within themselves for years. “Who is God, Mommy?"
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Thus begins a tentative journey back to their faith-though it may not be the same faith they knew as children. Perhaps a new tradition speaks more clearly to them. Perhaps they return to their original religion but in a different, and more committed, way than when they were young.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: So I firmly stepped onto the path of disbelief. This journey continued for a few months until a conversation with a mutual friend of Brad. Jacque (she pronounced it “Jackie”) came from a small town outside of Chicago and was what my friends derisively called a 'fundamentalist,' though we had scant idea of what that meant. (It meant that her faith informed her life.)
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Jacque's response nudged me onto the path of return.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Exploration
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: A few years ago, I worked with an Off-Broadway acting company that was producing a new play about the relationship between Jesus and Judas called The Last Days of Judas Iscariot. After some meetings with the actor who would play Judas, as well as the playwright and the director, I was invited to help the cast better understand the subject material. In time they asked me to serve as 'theological consultant' for the play.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Over the course of six months, I found myself talking with the actors not simply about Jesus and Judas but also about their spiritual lives, answering questions prompted by our freewheeling discussions about the Gospels, about sin and forgiveness, and about faith. Several of the actors had toggled between one religious tradition and another, seeking something that would 'fit.'
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: One actor, named Yetta, who played Mary Magdalene, told me that her mother was Catholic and her father was Jewish. They decided to let her choose her own religion when she was grown. “But,” she said, “I haven't chosen yet.”
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: My time with the actors was one of not only discovering the theater but also meeting people who were traveling along a path I hadn't encountered before. They were on the path of exploration.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Given their profession, this was not surprising. A good actor often researches a new role by spending time with a person from a particular background... So the idea of “exploration” comes naturally to them. Stepping into another person's shoes for a time is not that different from entering into another religious tradition for a time.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Exploration comes naturally to Americans in particular and is a theme celebrated not only in U.S. history but in our great works of literature... Our homegrown religious writers—especially the transcendentalists Ralph Waldo Emerson and Henry David Thoreau—were inner explorers. 'Afoot and lighthearted, I take to the open road,' wrote Walt Whitman, 'Healthy, free, the world before me, / The long brown path before me leading wherever I choose.'
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Exploration comes naturally in American faith as well. Turned off by their childhood faith, or by the failings of organized religion, and lacking extensive religious training, many Americans searching for a religion that 'fits' embark on a quest—itself a spiritual metaphor.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The benefit of walking along the path of exploration is plain. After a serious search, you may discover a tradition ideally suited to your understanding of God, your desires for community, and even to your own personality.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Likewise, returning to your original community may give you a renewed appreciation for your 'spiritual home.' Explorers may also be more grateful for what they have found and are not as likely to take their communities for granted. The most grateful pilgrim is the one who has finished the longest journey.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Another danger is a lack of commitment. Your entire life may become one of exploration— constant sampling, spiritual grazing. And when the path becomes the goal, rather than God, people may ultimately find themselves unfulfilled, confused, lost, and maybe even a little sad.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Confusion
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This final path crosses all the other ones at various points. People on the path of confusion run hot and cold with their childhood faith— finding it relatively easy to believe in God at times, almost impossible at others. They haven't 'fallen away,' but they've not stayed connected either. They cry out to God in prayer and then wonder why there doesn't seem to be an answer. They intuit God's presence during important moments, and perhaps even during religious services, but find themselves bothered by the problems of belonging to a church, synagogue, or mosque. They may pray from time to time, particularly when in dire need, and they may go to services on key holidays. But for this group, finding God is a mystery, a worry, or a problem.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The main benefit of this path is that it often helps people to finetune their approach to their childhood faith. Unlike those who consider themselves clearly religious or clearly nonreligious, these people have not yet made up their minds, and so they are constantly refining their ideas about a religious commitment.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: But confusion can lapse into laziness. Avoiding worship services because of a particular criticism can lead to leaving organized religion entirely because it's too much work, or because it takes too much energy to belong to a group that demands, say, charity and forgiveness.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Much of my adult life, before entering the Jesuits, was spent on this path. As a boy, I was raised in a loving family with a lukewarm Catholic background. My family went to church regularly, but we didn't engage in those practices that mark very religious Catholics—saying grace at meals, speaking regularly about God, praying before going to bed, and attending Catholic schools. And in college I grew increasingly confused about God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: After Jacque's mysterious answer moved me to give God another chance, I returned to church, but in a desultory way. I wasn't sure exactly what, or who, I believed in.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: That experience helped me to step off the path of confusion and onto the path of belief, which led to the Jesuits.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: THOSE ARE THE SIX paths on which many seem to travel. What does St. Ignatius have to say to people on each of those paths about finding God? The answer is: plenty.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The way of Ignatius is an invitation to those who have always believed in God, who believe in God but not in religion, who have rejected God, who are coming back to God, who are exploring, and who are confused. Ignatius's approach meets you on your path and leads you closer to God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Socrates was addressing students, teachers, and political leaders. Emmanuel was concerned with simple, uneducated people. What one does in a classroom is different from what one does with a ninety-five-year-old grandmother whose dogmatic views are her source of joy.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Day 4
How 2 Good Presentation
I - Identify: know what we're talking about.
D - Define: say what it means accurately.
E - Explain: why the topic is significant & explain its parts.
A - Apply: show how it works.
Application doesn't replace explanation.
S - Synthesize: Connect to other topics.
There are three angles for understanding God: 1. Experiential, 2. Philosophical, 3. Theological.
Experiential
Name things properly.
Discern!
Jan 26 (I.D.E.A.S)
I - Identify: know what exact topic you're talking about.
D - Define: say what the topic means accurately.
E - Explain: talk about the component parts and why it is important.
A - Apply: show how your topic plays out in real life.
S - Synthesize: connect it to other topics and to concepts outside the immediate subject.

topics.review:
In our exploration of theology, understanding our own position in relation to the Divine is a critical first step. The topic of "The Six Paths," as articulated by Jesuit priest James Martin, S.J., provides a foundational framework for this self-reflection. It is not a rigid typology for categorizing people, but rather a descriptive tool to help us understand the various ways individuals seek, relate to, or distance themselves from God throughout their lives. The core idea is that our spiritual journey is dynamic; we may find ourselves on different paths at different times, or even straddling more than one simultaneously. This framework matters because it fosters empathy for others' spiritual journeys and provides a language to articulate our own, often complex, relationship with faith, religion, and God.

The Six Paths are born from the observation that while each person's journey to God is unique, broad patterns emerge. These paths are often influenced by our life circumstances, personal history, and intellectual and emotional dispositions. As the lecture notes suggest, a "period of disengagement" during adolescence, where one seeks an individual identity apart from pre-made structures like family religion, can be a major catalyst for moving from one path to another.

1.  **The Path of Belief**: This is the path for those whose faith has been a constant, largely positive force in their lives. Often raised in religious families, they are comfortable with their belief in God, participate in religious services, and find meaning and strength in their faith community. James Martin uses the example of Walter Ciszek, a Jesuit priest who endured Soviet labor camps, sustained by the deep-seated faith instilled in him from childhood. The benefit of this path is the profound sense of security, meaning, and community it provides. However, its pitfalls include a potential for complacency—where childhood faith fails to mature into an adult understanding—and a tendency to judge those on other paths.

2.  **The Path of Independence**: Individuals on this path maintain a belief in God but have consciously separated themselves from organized religion. They may be termed "spiritual but not religious." They might find institutional religion hypocritical, restrictive, or simply not relevant to their personal connection with the Divine. The lecture notes clarify that this is different from mere confusion; it is a clear choice. The strength of this path lies in its fresh perspective; those on the outside can often critique institutions honestly and see things that insiders miss. The danger, as Martin notes, is that the search for a perfect community can become an endless, isolating quest.

3.  **The Path of Disbelief**: This path is characterized by an intellectual conclusion that God does not or cannot exist. It is more than just an absence of belief; it is a reasoned position. People on this path are often sharp thinkers who have contemplated religious questions more deeply than some believers. Their great benefit is their "religious-baloney detector"—they challenge easy, platitudinous answers and demand intellectual rigor. A potential danger is the expectation that God's existence can be proven solely through intellectual or scientific means, potentially closing them off to other ways of knowing, such as experience or mystery.

4.  **The Path of Return**: This path is for those who grew up with a religious background, drifted away, and are now tentatively finding their way back. This return is often prompted by significant life events: achieving professional success and feeling an existential emptiness, the birth of a child who asks about God, or facing one's own mortality. They return not to the same childish faith, but with a new, more mature and intentional commitment. Martin shares his own story of being nudged from disbelief back toward faith by a friend's simple, non-argumentative witness.

5.  **The Path of Exploration**: This path is marked by an active search across different religious traditions to find one that "fits." These individuals, like the actors Martin worked with, are spiritual 'samplers' or 'grazers.' They have a positive view of religion in general but are on a quest to find their spiritual home. The benefit is that when they do find a tradition, they are often deeply appreciative and committed, having made a long journey. The primary danger is that the exploration itself becomes the goal. A lack of commitment can lead to a state of perpetual seeking, which can ultimately feel unfulfilling and confusing.

6.  **The Path of Confusion**: This path intersects with all others. People here have a fluctuating relationship with faith; they run hot and cold. They believe in God at times, and find it impossible at others. They might pray in moments of crisis but feel alienated by organized religion's demands and inconsistencies. As the lecture notes highlight, they want to be religious, but many things are discouraging. The benefit is that this constant questioning can lead to a more refined and authentic faith if they persist. The danger is that confusion can devolve into laziness, using valid criticisms of religion as an excuse to avoid the hard work of spiritual growth, charity, and forgiveness. Martin identifies his own young adult life as being on this path before his journey led him to the Jesuits.

Ultimately, the Six Paths framework is a tool for discernment, not judgment. It connects directly to the course's larger themes by showing how the universal human **Desire** for meaning (Set 1) manifests in different approaches to the Divine. It illustrates the tension between personal **Spirituality** and organized **Religion** (Set 2). Finally, it reveals how our position on a path can be shaped by our **Images of God** (Set 3), and how moving from one path to another can be a form of personal **Revelation**. Understanding where you are is the first step in consciously engaging with your own spiritual journey.
keyTakeaways: The Six Paths (Belief, Independence, Disbelief, Return, Exploration, Confusion) by James Martin, S.J., are descriptive frameworks, not judgmental labels, for understanding different approaches to God.
A person's position on a path is not static; it can change throughout their life due to experiences like a "period of disengagement," major life events, or intellectual inquiry.
The Path of Belief is characterized by a lifelong, stable faith, but carries the risk of complacency and judgment towards others.
The Path of Independence distinguishes between belief in God (spirituality) and participation in organized religion, with individuals choosing the former but not the latter.

The Paths of Confusion, Independence, and Exploration can be confused. Confusion is an ambivalent state of wanting to believe but being discouraged. Independence is a clear decision to be spiritual without religion. Exploration is an active search for a religious home.
The Path of Disbelief is an intellectual conclusion against God's existence, valued for its rigorous questioning but limited if it only accepts intellectual proofs.
The Path of Return involves revisiting a childhood faith with a new, more mature perspective, often triggered by major life questions.
Each path has unique benefits (e.g., the security of Belief, the critical eye of Disbelief) and pitfalls (e.g., the potential aimlessness of Exploration, the laziness of Confusion).

glossary.definition: A framework developed by James Martin, S.J., that categorizes the diverse ways people relate to God into six broad, non-static spiritual journeys: Belief, Independence, Disbelief, Return, Exploration, and Confusion.
A spiritual journey where belief in God has been a consistent and integral part of one's life, often from a young age. Characterized by regular prayer, church attendance, and a confident faith.
A spiritual journey where an individual believes in God but has made a conscious decision to separate from organized religion. They are often described as 'spiritual but not religious'.
A spiritual journey characterized by the intellectual conclusion that God may not, does not, or cannot exist. It is a reasoned position, not merely an absence of faith.
A spiritual journey of individuals who grew up with religion, drifted away, and later in life begin a tentative journey back to faith, often with a more mature and committed perspective.

A spiritual journey involving an active search or 'sampling' of various religious traditions to find one that best fits the individual's personality and understanding of God. It is characterized by openness but risks a lack of commitment.

A spiritual journey marked by an inconsistent and ambivalent relationship with faith. Individuals on this path fluctuate between belief and doubt, finding it easy to believe at times and impossible at others.

A developmental stage, often during adolescence, where an individual seeks to discover their personal identity separate from established institutions and pre-defined structures, such as their family's religion.

An acronym for a method of analysis taught in the course: Identify the topic, Define it accurately, Explain its parts and significance, Apply it to a real-life example, and Synthesize it with other course concepts.

studyTips: Focus on distinguishing between the Paths of Independence, Exploration, and Confusion. This is a key point of nuance from the lecture. Independence is a firm choice, Exploration is an active search, and Confusion is a state of ambivalence.

For the exam, don't just memorize the definitions. Be prepared to apply the paths to a hypothetical person's story. Think about what life events might move someone from the Path of Belief to the Path of Confusion, for example.
Remember that the paths are not a 'ranking.' No path is presented as inherently 'better' than another, only different. Avoid judgmental language in your answers.
Use the I.D.E.A.S. framework (Identify, Define, Explain, Apply, Synthesize) to structure your thoughts for a potential essay question on any one of the six paths.
Pay attention to the benefits AND pitfalls of each path. A good answer will demonstrate an understanding of both the positive aspects and the potential dangers associated with each spiritual journey.
Connect the personal stories from the reading to the paths they illustrate: Walter Ciszek (Belief), the actor Yetta (Exploration), and James Martin's own journey (Confusion to Return/Belief).

connections.relationship: The innate human 'Desire' for the infinite, for meaning, and for God is the engine that drives a person onto any of the six paths. Whether one is seeking, returning, or even actively disbelieving, it is in response to this fundamental human longing.
connections.example: A person on the Path of Exploration is actively trying to satisfy their Desire by sampling different traditions, hoping one will resonate with their inner longing for a spiritual home.

connections.relationship: The Path of Independence is the clearest illustration of the distinction between personal spirituality (belief in God) and organized religion (institutional practice). It shows that one can exist without the other.
connections.example: Someone who says, 'I feel closest to God in nature, but I find church services to be empty rituals,' is living out the Path of Independence and highlighting the divide between their spirituality and institutional religion.

connections.relationship: The Six Paths can be seen as different expressions or states of 'Faith.' The Path of Belief shows a stable and integrated faith, the Path of Confusion a wavering faith, and the Path of Disbelief an intellectual rejection of the premises of faith.
connections.example: Walter Ciszek's ability to endure a Soviet gulag demonstrates a faith that is a deep, anchoring reality (Path of Belief), while someone who only prays when they are in trouble exhibits a faith that is situational and uncertain (Path of Confusion).

connections.relationship: The path a person is on can be a direct result of their image of God. A distorted image of God as a punitive judge might lead someone to the Path of Disbelief or Independence. Conversely, a pitfall of the Path of Belief is becoming complacent with a childish or incomplete image of God.
connections.example: Someone who leaves the church because they were taught God is an angry scorekeeper who punishes any misstep is rejecting a 'Distorted Image of God.' Their journey might place them on the Path of Independence or Disbelief as a reaction.

connections.relationship: Each path represents a different way individuals might seek or be open to Divine 'Revelation.' Revelation isn't just a formal doctrine; it can be a personal, transformative experience that confirms, challenges, or initiates a spiritual journey.
connections.example: For a person on the Path of Return, seeing their newborn child for the first time could be a moment of personal revelation that awakens dormant spiritual questions and leads them back toward God.

connections.relationship: The experience of wonder can be a gateway to any of the paths. It can solidify the Path of Belief (wonder at creation), trigger the Path of Exploration (wondering what other traditions teach), or even challenge the Path of Disbelief (an experience of profound beauty that defies a purely materialist explanation).
connections.example: An astrophysicist on the Path of Disbelief who is continually struck with awe and wonder at the elegance of the universe's physical laws might begin to question if there is something more, potentially nudging them toward Confusion or Exploration.

examPrepQuestions.question: Using the lecture notes, explain the key difference between the Path of Confusion and the Path of Independence, particularly regarding their views on organized religion.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The key difference lies in disposition and clarity. The Path of Confusion has a more negative or ambivalent view of religion, feeling discouraged by its flaws but still wanting to be part of it. The Path of Independence has a clearer, more decisive stance: they believe in God but have deliberately chosen to separate from organized religion, not out of ambivalence, but as a conscious choice for an individual spiritual life.

examPrepQuestions.question: According to James Martin, S.J., what are the two main pitfalls for someone on the Path of Belief?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The two main pitfalls for the Path of Belief are: 1) a complacency that causes one's faith to stagnate, clinging to childish understandings instead of maturing into an adult faith; and 2) an inability to understand people on other paths, which can lead to judging them for their doubt or disbelief.

examPrepQuestions.question: How does the example of the actor 'Yetta' in James Martin's book perfectly illustrate the Path of Exploration?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Yetta, whose parents were Catholic and Jewish, was left to 'choose her own religion' but had not yet chosen. This places her on the Path of Exploration, as she is in a state of active, open searching for a spiritual tradition that 'fits,' without having committed to one yet. Her situation embodies the quest and 'sampling' nature of this path.

quizQuestions.question: According to James Martin's framework, a person who was raised Catholic, stopped going to church in college, but starts attending services again after their first child is born is most likely on which path?
choices: A) The Path of Exploration
B) The Path of Return
C) The Path of Confusion
D) The Path of Belief
quizQuestions.explanation: The Path of Return is specifically for those who drift away from their childhood faith and then begin a journey back, often prompted by major life events like the birth of a child.

quizQuestions.question: Which path's main benefit is described as having a 'religious-baloney detector' and challenging lazy religious answers?
choices: A) The Path of Independence
B) The Path of Confusion
C) The Path of Disbelief
D) The Path of Exploration
quizQuestions.explanation: The Path of Disbelief is characterized by intellectual rigor and a tendency to question religious claims, which Martin describes as a knack for detecting hypocrisy and demanding better arguments.

quizQuestions.question: A college student feels a strong connection to God but finds church hypocritical and restrictive. They pray on their own but have made a firm decision not to belong to any religious institution. Which path are they on?
choices: A) The Path of Confusion
B) The Path of Independence
C) The Path of Disbelief
D) The Path of Return
quizQuestions.explanation: This describes the Path of Independence perfectly: a continued belief in God combined with a conscious, deliberate separation from organized religion.

quizQuestions.question: What is a primary danger of the Path of Exploration?
choices: A) Judging others for their beliefs.
B) A complacency that leads to a stagnant faith.
C) The search itself becomes the goal, leading to a lack of commitment.
D) Expecting God to be proven only in an intellectual way.
quizQuestions.explanation: Martin warns that for explorers, the 'constant sampling' can prevent them from ever committing to a path, making the journey the goal rather than God, which can be unfulfilling.

quizQuestions.question: The story of Walter Ciszek, who endured Soviet prisons sustained by his faith, is used to illustrate which path?
choices: A) The Path of Belief
B) The Path of Return
C) The Path of Independence
D) The Path of Confusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Walter Ciszek's story is the primary example used for the Path of Belief, showing a faith instilled from childhood that provides profound strength through extreme hardship.

quizQuestions.question: According to the lecture transcript, what distinguishes the Path of Confusion from the Path of Exploration?
choices: A) Confusion has a negative view of religion, while Exploration has a positive view.
B) Exploration involves trying different religions, while Confusion is about staying within one.
C) People on the Path of Confusion do not believe in God.
D) People on the Path of Exploration do not believe in God.
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture notes state that Explorers 'still have a very positive view of religion' while those in Confusion have a 'more negative view of religion because we've seen how... religion has failed people.'

quizQuestions.question: Which path is described as crossing all the other paths at various points?
choices: A) The Path of Exploration
B) The Path of Independence
C) The Path of Return
D) The Path of Confusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Martin explicitly states that the Path of Confusion 'crosses all the other ones at various points,' as doubt and uncertainty can arise on any spiritual journey.

quizQuestions.question: The 'period of disengagement' discussed in the lecture often happens during adolescence and involves...
choices: A) Deciding to become a priest or nun.
B) Forgetting all religious teachings from childhood.
C) Trying to discover one's individual self apart from institutions.
D) Fully committing to the faith of one's parents.
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture defines the period of disengagement as a time when 'you are trying to discover your individual self apart from institutions and pre made structures,' which often involves questioning one's family religion.

quizQuestions.question: A primary pitfall for the Path of Belief is...
choices: A) an endless search for a perfect community.
B) laziness and avoiding the hard work of forgiveness.
C) complacency and clinging to a faith that doesn't mature.
D) an intellectual rejection of God's existence.
quizQuestions.explanation: Martin identifies complacency, where one's faith stagnates at a childhood level, as a subtle but significant danger for those on the Path of Belief.

quizQuestions.question: Someone who is 'spiritual but not religious' is a classic example of which path?
choices: A) The Path of Disbelief
B) The Path of Confusion
C) The Path of Independence
D) The Path of Exploration
quizQuestions.explanation: The phrase 'spiritual but not religious' directly corresponds to the definition of the Path of Independence: believing in a higher power or having a spiritual life while rejecting the structures of organized religion.

topics.title: Sacred

glossaryTerms.definition: An experience that interrupts ordinary life and opens an individual to life's deeper meaning, characterized by a sense of awe, grandeur, and being part of something greater than oneself.
Derived from Latin for 'daily,' it refers to the ordinary, non-flashy, everyday movements of life. It is presented as the neutral opposite of the sacred, distinct from 'profane' or 'mundane'.
A metaphor for getting stuck in the quotidian, where life becomes an endless, meaningless cycle of just surviving without a deeper sense of purpose.

A term coined by Rudolf Otto to describe the unique experience of the Holy or the sacred; the feeling of being in the presence of something wholly other, greater than oneself, and beyond comprehension.
Rudolf Otto's three-part Latin phrase describing the numinous experience. It is a mystery (mysterium) that is simultaneously terrifying (tremendum) and fascinating (fascinans).
The component of the numinous experience that signifies it is a profound mystery, something beyond our full intellectual grasp or imagination.
The component of the numinous experience that is terrifying, awesome, and overwhelming. It highlights our powerlessness and vulnerability before the sacred.
The component of the numinous experience that is irresistibly attractive and captivating. It is the quality of the sacred that draws a person towards it, despite any fear.
A characteristic of an encounter with the sacred, analogous to falling in love, where one's attention is captured and focused, disrupting the normal flow of life.

entries.content: Before I dive in, I really want to talk to you about adobo and why adobo specifically my Lola's adobo.

The adobo I grew up with... is an adobo that's a lot darker... My Lola serves this adobo with garlic rice and the most perfect sunny side up... That was my first glimpse of heaven. It's having my lola's adobo when I was seven years old.

Every time I have this adobo, it really does feel like an out of body experience for me. It's damn, damn good. I'm sharing this to you because this is going to be the key analogy of this entire module. My Lola's adobo.
The first is that I eat my Lola's unbelievably delicious adobo. And as I told you, it's an out of body experience. I'm still so mind blown by it even today.
All of this really as a quick summary of our four main concepts for this sacred, faith, spirituality and religion.

Here's the best reason why I love the beach... somehow whenever I stand on the shore like this, I really feel small... a better way to put it is that I get a sense that there's something far greater than me, that there's a greater scheme of things.

But of course you can feel it on a mountain too. So see, whether it's a mountain or a beach, when you're really right smack in the middle of nature and you're enveloped by its grandeur, you feel small and in some sense you have a healthy sense of self forgetfulness.

I think one of the most anxious experiences in our life is when we're so self conscious... But I think that's why people go to mountains and beaches... Because in some sense they just forget it all and they become absorbed with where they are.

For some people it's places that make them feel like they're part of a grander scheme of things. But for others it could be people. For others it could be moments. So see, whether in places or people or experiences, have we ever had those moments where we feel that life is just so much more than what we perceive?

What I'm talking about here, everybody, is that feeling of, or that sense of grandeur that we have, that feeling of being part of something more, or that there is indeed something more. This, everyone, let's call this the experience of the sacred.
It might be helpful if we examine its opposite, which is... not the word mundane, not the word profane. The way I want to approach this is to talk about the word quotidian.

The word quotidian, right? It comes from the Latin word quotidianus... And cotidianus means daily. So when we think of the quotidian, we think of daily movements of our lives, right? The conclusion is where life hammers us. It's our daily, ordinary, non flashy attempts at surviving and thriving.
I'm not saying that being part of the quotidian is a negative experience. It's not. Most of our lives are lived in the quotidian. We get life done in the daily.

But there is a possibility of getting stuck in the quotidian. You can get stuck in the Covidian in what I would describe as a washing machine existence... That life just becomes this endless meaningless cycle of just surviving.

The good news is that you don't have to get stuck... there are basic things that you can do in each day to make sure that you don't get caught up in the hamster rhythm... And you know the most basic way to do it? It's to simply ask the question, why?

I think there's a lot of negative baggage with those words. When you say profane, it's something that sort of insults the sacred. When you see mundane, you're talking about a boring version of the quotidian... So I think the word quotidian is the one that feels more neutral.

So what then is the sacred? A preliminary definition. It's hard to really come up with a clear dictionary definition. But a good place to start is this: that the Sacred is an experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning.
The sacred is this thing that opens you out to something greater than yourself. And that life is not just about going with the motions of our temporal existence.
The secret opens you up in a way, surprises you, kind of leaves you emotional at times, makes you feel moved... I think that's the feeling that you get when you really encounter a mystery.
What's the experience of the sacred like? I think it's akin to the experience of falling in love.

There's something about falling in love that has an arresting quality... It captures your attention so that you focus on this person whether you like it or not. So see the tension? I like it, but this makes me uncomfortable. That is what the experience of the sacred is like.

As the German Lutheran theologian Rudolph Otto would put it, when he talks about the experience of the sacred... he talks about this experience of being before the sacred's arresting quality as the numinous.
When I am before something greater than myself and beyond my comprehension, that's what he calls the experience of the numinous.
And he described this experience in his famous Latin description that the numinous is mysterium tremendum et fascinans.
That's why it's also tremendum. It's terrifying. It's out of your control.

Especially when you're someone who's so... very proud of your singleness... To fall in love with somebody can be such an uncomfortable experience. It somehow messes with your emotional wiring. And all of these fears will come out... What if I get rejected?

Every relationship is a risk. Guess what people, including a married man... A vow is not something like, I'm going to choose you today. And you can guarantee I'm going to choose you every time. It's choosing somebody each day. And so the risk is, what if I don't get chosen?
It's scary. It's terrifying. To be in front of the sacred is to be in such a vulnerable position that you realize you don't have full control.

And yet somehow it's fascinans. Fascinans brings about the English word as fascinating. When you're fascinated by something, somehow it pulls you towards itself, it's attractive. I don't want it, but I also want it.

Mysterium tremendum et fascinans. That... is what we get whenever we encounter, in some sense, God. And what I've come to observe throughout the years is that you don't necessarily have to be a religious person to encounter God in this manner.
Module 2
The topics of Module 2 are: Sacred, Faith, Spirituality, and Religion.
The experience of falling in love has an arresting quality, which is analogous to an encounter with the sacred.
The sacred invites us to faith.
An encounter with the sacred gives a 'feel of grander things'.
The sacred opens you to life's deeper meaning.
The quotidian refers to the daily and is the antonym of the sacred.
Mysterium: Part of the experience of the sacred that is 'beyond our imagination'.
Tremendum: Part of the experience of the sacred that is 'terrifying' and 'beyond control'.
Fascinans: Part of the experience of the sacred that is 'attractive'.
A diagram of a thistle plant next to the word 'Strathmore,' possibly illustrating the sacred found in the mundane (a paper company's logo).
Sacred Faith Spirituality Religion
The experience of the sacred involves something far greater than ME!
Sacred
The sacred makes you feel you are part of a grand scheme of things.
Life is so much more than what we perceive.
quotidian: daily movements of life.
The sacred can be found in ordinary, lived-in events.
A prompt for reflection: consider a washing machine and ask 'Why?' to find deeper meaning in a mundane object.
The Sacred opens to you life's deeper meaning.
Encountering the sacred is a thrilling and uncomfortable experience.
The sacred bothers your usual way of going about life.
The sacred has an arresting quality to it; it captures your attention.
According to theologian Rudolf Otto, the experience of the sacred is the experience of the Holy or the numinous.

mysterium tremendum et fascinans: A concept of the sacred described as a mystery ('mysterium': beyond our imagination, always full), that is both terrifying ('tremendum': out of our control) and fascinating ('fascinans': attractive).

topics.review:
This module introduces the concept of 'the Sacred' as the foundational experience for approaching the divine. The central argument is that before we can discuss faith, spirituality, or religion, we must first understand the raw, personal encounter with something 'more' that breaks through our ordinary lives. The professor uses a deeply personal and culturally resonant analogy to anchor this entire discussion: his Lola's adobo. This specific adobo, served with garlic rice and a perfect sunny-side-up egg, represents a 'glimpse of heaven'—an ordinary meal that transcends itself to become an 'out of body experience.' This illustrates the core idea that the sacred is not necessarily found in grand, explicitly religious settings, but can erupt within the most common aspects of our lives.

The experience of the sacred is defined as an event or moment that opens you to life's deeper meaning. It's characterized by a sense of grandeur, a feeling of being part of a larger scheme of things, making one feel 'small' in a healthy, self-forgetful way. This can happen in nature, like standing on a beach or a mountaintop, where the sheer scale of the environment leads to a sense of awe. It can also be found in relationships with people or in significant personal moments. The common thread is the realization that 'life is just so much more than what we perceive.'

To better understand the sacred, the lecture contrasts it with its opposite, not as 'profane' or 'mundane'—words that carry negative baggage—but as the 'quotidian.' Derived from the Latin 'cotidianus' (daily), the quotidian refers to the ordinary, daily movements of life where we survive and thrive. This is not inherently negative; it's where most of life happens. The danger, however, is getting stuck in the quotidian, leading to what is described as a 'washing machine existence'—a meaningless, repetitive cycle. The lecture suggests that the way out of this trap is to consciously interrupt the rhythm by asking a simple but profound question: 'Why?'. This act of questioning can re-infuse daily actions with meaning and open a path back to the sacred.

To describe the quality of this encounter, the lecture introduces the work of German Lutheran theologian Rudolf Otto. Otto terms the experience of the Holy as the 'numinous'—an experience of being before something greater than oneself and beyond comprehension. He famously described this numinous experience with the Latin phrase 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans.' This tripartite phrase is a cornerstone of the topic. 'Mysterium' refers to the fact that the sacred is a mystery, something beyond our full grasp and imagination, not a problem to be solved. 'Tremendum' points to the terrifying aspect of the encounter. It is awesome and overwhelming because it is completely out of our control, placing us in a position of profound vulnerability. It shatters our sense of self-sufficiency. 'Fascinans' captures the other side of the coin: it is irresistibly attractive and fascinating. It pulls us in, captivates us, and makes us want to draw closer, despite the fear.

This paradoxical tension is powerfully illustrated through the analogy of falling in love. Falling in love has an 'arresting quality' that captures your attention. It's 'tremendum' because it makes you vulnerable, introducing risks of rejection and emotional turmoil, messing with your 'emotional wiring.' The lecture notes that every relationship is a risk, as it involves a daily choice. Yet, it's also 'fascinans' because the attraction is undeniable; you feel drawn to the person, wanting the connection despite the fear. This 'I don't want it, but I also want it' feeling is the hallmark of an encounter with the sacred. This experience, the lecture concludes, is not exclusive to religious people; it's a fundamental human experience of encountering a reality—which many would name God—that is a mystery both terrifying and attractive. It is this foundational experience that then 'invites us to faith,' setting the stage for the subsequent discussions on faith, spirituality, and religion in the course.
keyTakeaways: The Sacred is an experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning and the sense of being part of something much greater than yourself.
The primary analogy for the Sacred is the professor's 'Lola's adobo'—an ordinary meal that provides an extraordinary, 'out of body' experience, showing the sacred can be found in the everyday.

The opposite of the Sacred is the 'quotidian,' which refers to neutral, daily life. This is distinct from the negative terms 'profane' or 'mundane.' The danger is getting trapped in a meaningless 'washing machine existence.'
According to theologian Rudolf Otto, the experience of the Sacred is 'numinous'—an encounter with something holy, greater than oneself, and beyond comprehension.

Otto describes the numinous experience as 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans': a Mystery ('mysterium') that is both terrifying and overwhelming ('tremendum') yet also irresistibly attractive and fascinating ('fascinans').

Falling in love is used as a key analogy for the 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' experience. It has an 'arresting quality' and involves both the fear of vulnerability ('tremendum') and a powerful attraction ('fascinans').
The experience of the sacred is not limited to religious people; it is a fundamental human encounter with a greater reality.
In the context of the course, the encounter with the Sacred is the starting point that 'invites' a person to a response, which can take the form of Faith, Spirituality, and/or Religion.

glossary.definition: An experience that interrupts ordinary life and opens an individual to life's deeper meaning, characterized by a sense of awe, grandeur, and being part of something greater than oneself.
Derived from Latin for 'daily,' it refers to the ordinary, non-flashy, everyday movements of life. It is presented as the neutral opposite of the sacred, distinct from 'profane' or 'mundane'.
A metaphor for getting stuck in the quotidian, where life becomes an endless, meaningless cycle of just surviving without a deeper sense of purpose.

A term coined by Rudolf Otto to describe the unique experience of the Holy or the sacred; the feeling of being in the presence of something wholly other, greater than oneself, and beyond comprehension.
Rudolf Otto's three-part Latin phrase describing the numinous experience. It is a mystery (mysterium) that is simultaneously terrifying (tremendum) and fascinating (fascinans).
The component of the numinous experience that signifies it is a profound mystery, something beyond our full intellectual grasp or imagination.
The component of the numinous experience that is terrifying, awesome, and overwhelming. It highlights our powerlessness and vulnerability before the sacred.
The component of the numinous experience that is irresistibly attractive and captivating. It is the quality of the sacred that draws a person towards it, despite any fear.
A characteristic of an encounter with the sacred, analogous to falling in love, where one's attention is captured and focused, disrupting the normal flow of life.

studyTips: Master the 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' concept by personalizing it. Use the lecture's analogy of falling in love and apply it to your own experiences. What feels both scary (tremendum) and attractive (fascinans) to you? This makes the abstract concept concrete.

Focus on the professor's specific word choice: 'quotidian' over 'profane' or 'mundane'. Be ready to explain *why* 'quotidian' is a more precise, neutral term for the opposite of the sacred. This shows you've grasped the nuance of the lecture.

Use the 'Lola's adobo' story as your primary anchor for the whole module. It connects the Sacred (a transcendent meal) to Faith, Spirituality, and Religion (the responses to that experience). If you can explain this analogy, you understand the core of SET2.

Create a simple chart with two columns: 'Tremendum' and 'Fascinans'. List the examples from the lecture for each (e.g., Tremendum: fear of rejection, loss of control; Fascinans: attraction, feeling drawn in). This will help you organize the paradoxical nature of the sacred.

Practice asking 'Why?' about a mundane object in your room, as suggested in the notes (like the washing machine prompt). This active exercise helps you internalize the method for breaking out of the 'quotidian' and finding potential meaning in the everyday.

Don't just memorize Rudolf Otto's name. Connect him specifically to 'numinous' and 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans.' Think of him as the theologian who provided the vocabulary to describe the *feeling* of encountering the sacred.

connections.relationship: The sacred is a direct, felt experience of Mystery. The 'mysterium' in Otto's phrase directly connects to the SET1 concept that a mystery is not a problem to solve, but a reality to be entered into. The sacred is the ultimate mystery we encounter.
connections.example: Feeling small at the beach is not a problem to figure out; it's a mysterious reality of your place in the universe that you experience and are absorbed by.

connections.relationship: Wonder is the human disposition that allows us to perceive the sacred. The ability to be filled with wonder is a prerequisite for recognizing a 'Lola's adobo' moment or feeling awe on a mountaintop.
connections.example: Without a sense of wonder, a starry night is just a collection of gas balls. With wonder, it can become an experience of the sacred, evoking a sense of grandeur.

connections.relationship: The experience of the sacred is the catalyst for faith. The source states, 'The sacred invites us to faith.' Faith is the personal, trusting response to the 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' encounter.
connections.example: After a profound experience of awe in nature (the sacred), a person might choose to trust that there is a benevolent creator behind it. That act of trust is faith.

connections.relationship: Religion provides the language, rituals, and community to interpret, celebrate, and sustain the experience of the sacred over time. It gives a framework to the 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' encounter.
connections.example: An individual's private, numinous experience of God's majesty might lead them to join a church, where that experience is collectively remembered and named through scripture, song (like a hymn about God's greatness), and sacraments.

connections.relationship: An unbalanced understanding of the sacred's dual nature can lead to distorted images of God. Focusing solely on one aspect of the numinous creates an incomplete picture.
connections.example: Focusing only on the 'tremendum' can create a distorted image of God as a terrifying, distant, and punishing judge. Conversely, focusing only on the 'fascinans' can lead to a 'buddy Jesus' image that lacks any sense of awe, reverence, or divine majesty.

connections.relationship: The experience of the sacred is a form of natural revelation. It is God, or the divine, revealing a glimpse of itself through creation, human experience, and ordinary events, rather than through specific scripture or dogma.
connections.example: The feeling of being part of a 'grander scheme of things' while on a mountain is a moment of revelation; something about the nature of reality is revealed to the person in that experience.

examPrepQuestions.question: The lecture purposefully uses the term 'quotidian' as the opposite of the 'sacred,' rather than 'profane' or 'mundane.' Explain the concept of the quotidian and why this specific word choice is significant for understanding the lecture's approach to the sacred.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The quotidian, from the Latin 'cotidianus' meaning 'daily,' refers to the ordinary, everyday, and non-flashy aspects of life. It's the realm of our daily routines, work, and efforts to survive and thrive. The choice of 'quotidian' is significant because it is a neutral term. Unlike 'profane,' which implies a violation or insult to the sacred, or 'mundane,' which suggests boredom, 'quotidian' simply describes the state of ordinary existence where most of our lives are lived. This neutrality is key because the lecture argues that the sacred can be found *within* the quotidian (like in a meal of adobo). The danger isn't the quotidian itself, but getting stuck in it in a 'washing machine existence,' where daily life loses its meaning. The term 'quotidian' allows for a reality where the sacred is not a separate, distant realm, but one that can break through the ordinary at any moment.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain Rudolf Otto's concept of the numinous experience as 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans.' Define each of the three Latin terms and use the lecture's analogy of 'falling in love' to illustrate the complete concept.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer:
Rudolf Otto, a German theologian, described the encounter with the sacred as 'numinous,' which he defined as 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans.' This phrase breaks down into three parts:
1.  **Mysterium:** This refers to the sacred as a profound mystery, something 'wholly other' and beyond our complete comprehension. It's not a problem to be solved but a reality that envelops us.
2.  **Tremendum:** This captures the terrifying, awesome, and overwhelming aspect of the encounter. It makes us feel small and vulnerable, realizing we are not in control.
3.  **Fascinans:** This describes the attractive, fascinating quality of the sacred that irresistibly pulls us in and captivates our attention.

The lecture's analogy of 'falling in love' perfectly illustrates this dynamic. When you fall in love, it is a **mysterium** because the other person and the feeling itself are profound and can't be fully rationalized. It is **tremendum** because it's terrifying; you become vulnerable, risk rejection, and your emotional life is thrown into beautiful chaos, messing with your 'emotional wiring.' You lose a sense of control. Yet, it is also **fascinans** because the attraction is powerful and compelling. You are drawn to the person and want the relationship despite the risks. This tension of being both scared ('tremendum') and attracted ('fascinans') is the essence of Otto's concept of the numinous experience.

examPrepQuestions.question: Using the key analogies from the lecture (Lola's adobo, the beach/mountain), explain what the 'experience of the sacred' is and how it relates to the 'quotidian.' How does this experience serve as a starting point for Faith and Religion?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer:
The 'experience of the sacred' is defined in the lecture as an encounter that opens you up to life's deeper meaning and a sense of being part of something far greater than yourself. It's not an idea but a felt experience. The analogy of Lola's adobo illustrates that this experience can emerge from the 'quotidian,' or daily life. A simple, familiar meal transcends its physical properties to become a 'glimpse of heaven,' an 'out of body experience.' Similarly, standing on a beach or mountaintop makes one feel 'small' and 'self-forgetful,' absorbed by a grandeur that points to a reality beyond the immediate. These moments are eruptions of the sacred within the ordinary.

The quotidian is the daily, non-flashy rhythm of life. It's not inherently bad, but it can become a 'washing machine existence' if it lacks meaning. The sacred is what breaks this cycle. It has an 'arresting quality' that interrupts the mundane flow and surprises us, moves us, and makes us ask 'Why?'.

This experience serves as a crucial starting point for Faith and Religion because it is the initial, raw data of the divine. The 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' encounter is a call that demands a response. Faith is the personal act of trust and commitment in response to that call—choosing to believe in the 'something more' that was glimpsed. Religion, then, provides the structured system—with its stories, rituals, doctrines, and community—to help individuals and groups make sense of, remember, and collectively respond to these sacred experiences. In short, the sacred is the spark, faith is the personal 'yes' to the spark, and religion is the vessel that holds and tends the fire for a community over generations.

quizQuestions.question: According to the lecture, what is the 'key analogy of this entire module' for understanding the sacred, faith, spirituality, and religion?
choices: A) Standing on a beach and feeling small
B) The professor's Lola's adobo
C) The experience of falling in love
D) A thistle plant on a paper company logo
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture explicitly states, 'I'm sharing this to you because this is going to be the key analogy of this entire module. My Lola's adobo.' It represents the sacred found in the ordinary.

quizQuestions.question: The lecture uses the term 'quotidian' as the opposite of the sacred. What is the primary reason for choosing this word over 'profane' or 'mundane'?
choices: A) 'Quotidian' sounds more academic and theological.
B) 'Quotidian' implies something evil that must be avoided.
C) 'Quotidian' is a neutral term for 'daily life,' whereas 'profane' and 'mundane' have negative connotations.
D) 'Quotidian' refers only to boring and repetitive tasks.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source states, 'I think the word quotidian is the one that feels more neutral.' It avoids the negative baggage of 'profane' (insulting the sacred) and 'mundane' (boring).

quizQuestions.question: Who is the German Lutheran theologian credited with the concept of the 'numinous' and the phrase 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans'?
choices: A) Karl Rahner
B) Paul Tillich
C) Gabriel Marcel
D) Rudolf Otto
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture clearly attributes the concept of the numinous and the phrase 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' to 'the German Lutheran theologian Rudolph Otto.'

quizQuestions.question: In Rudolf Otto's phrase 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans,' what does 'tremendum' refer to?
choices: A) The attractive and fascinating quality of the sacred.
B) The mysterious and incomprehensible nature of the sacred.
C) The thrilling and exciting aspect of the sacred.
D) The terrifying, awesome, and uncontrollable aspect of the sacred.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes define 'tremendum' as the part of the experience that is 'terrifying' and 'out of your control,' making you feel vulnerable.

quizQuestions.question: The lecture compares the experience of the sacred to falling in love because both have an 'arresting quality.' What does this mean?
choices: A) It can lead to you being arrested by authorities.
B) It stops you from growing as a person.
C) It captures your attention and disrupts your normal way of life.
D) It is a temporary feeling that eventually fades away.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source explains that the 'arresting quality' means 'It captures your attention so that you focus on this person whether you like it or not,' thus bothering your usual way of going about life.

quizQuestions.question: What is the 'washing machine existence' a metaphor for?
choices: A) A life dedicated to cleanliness and order.
B) Getting stuck in a meaningless, repetitive cycle of daily life.
C) The complex and mysterious nature of the sacred.
D) The dual experience of fear and attraction.
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture describes getting stuck in the quotidian as a 'washing machine existence... That life just becomes this endless meaningless cycle of just surviving.'

quizQuestions.question: Which component of the numinous experience refers to its irresistibly attractive and captivating quality?
choices: A) Mysterium
B) Numinous
C) Fascinans
D) Tremendum
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes define 'fascinans' as the 'attractive' part of the experience that 'pulls you towards itself.'

quizQuestions.question: According to the lecture, what is the most basic way to avoid getting stuck in the 'hamster rhythm' of the quotidian?
choices: A) To go on vacation to a beach or mountain.
B) To simply ask the question, 'Why?'.
C) To end a relationship that feels too risky.
D) To eat a delicious meal every day.
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture states, 'And you know the most basic way to do it? It's to simply ask the question, why?' This simple question can break the cycle and invite deeper meaning.

quizQuestions.question: How does the lecture primarily define 'the Sacred'?
choices: A) Any object or place officially designated by a religion.
B) An experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning.
C) The feeling of fear and terror before God.
D) The collection of all religious beliefs and practices.
quizQuestions.explanation: A preliminary definition given in the lecture is 'that the Sacred is an experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning.'

quizQuestions.question: The experience of the sacred is described as paradoxical. Which pair of feelings best represents this paradox?
choices: A) Joy and Happiness
B) Fear and Attraction
C) Confusion and Clarity
D) Sadness and Grief
quizQuestions.explanation: The paradox is captured by 'tremendum' (terrifying/fear) and 'fascinans' (fascinating/attraction). The lecture emphasizes this 'I don't want it, but I also want it' tension.

topics.title: Faith

glossaryTerms.definition: The personal and ultimate acceptance of the sacred. It is not a possession but an active, chosen conviction that must be cultivated, shaping one's entire life.

A mature understanding and practice of faith that has grown beyond childhood conceptions. It is capable of grappling with complex life questions, suffering, and doubt, as advocated by William A. Barry.

Intellectual assent to a set of propositions or dogmas. It is distinguished from faith, as one can believe in a doctrine without the personal commitment and spiritual transformation that marks genuine faith.

A form of doubt that plays a positive role in faith by challenging inherited beliefs and preventing them from becoming stagnant or meaningless. This critical assessment helps a person make their faith personal and vital.

glossaryTerms.term: The Dark Night of the Soul
glossaryTerms.definition: A term from the mystic St. John of the Cross describing a stage in spiritual development characterized by a profound and painful sense of God's absence. It is an experience of spiritual desolation or 'godforsakenness' within the life of a believer.

In Paul Tillich's framework, this is the doubt that is inherent to faith itself. It concerns one's 'ultimate concern' and arises from human finitude. It is overcome not by achieving certainty but through courage.

A term describing the experience of 'godforsakenness' or divine absence felt by some deeply religious individuals, such as the mystics. It is not atheism in the sense of disbelief, but a painful perception of God's seeming absence within a life of faith.
Defined by theologian Paul Tillich as 'the state of being ultimately concerned.' This means faith is the act of committing oneself wholly to what one believes to be of ultimate value and meaning.
A form of doubt, distinguished by Paul Tillich, that is characteristic of scientific inquiry. It involves a systematic questioning of hypotheses as part of the process of seeking knowledge.
A form of doubt, distinguished by Paul Tillich, that represents a general attitude of rejecting the possibility of any certainty.

A concept from Paul Tillich, referring to that which an individual holds as having ultimate value, meaning, and importance, demanding total commitment. For Tillich, faith is the state of having an ultimate concern.

entries.content: There was something about that adobo that really inspired me to learn how to cook. I know how to cook and that's really so I believe in preserving my Lola's cooking because of this experience of her adobo. So I decided to learn how to cook like her.
The experience of something greater than yourself... After that initial experience... Now comes an invitation. An invitation to accept this experience as a part of your own life.
So faith. What's faith? It's the acceptance of the sacred. Personally and ultimately.

It's possible that your experience of the Sacred can just become a passing experience... But faith is saying, this is my experience. And there's something I want to do to make this experience very much a part of my life.
But take note of this, that the Sacred invites us to faith. ... Faith is not an imposition, but an invitation. You really cannot force faith on a person because faith is something that you choose.
The more you impose faith, the less attractive it becomes.

See, this is what a lot of people miss, that faith is not an object we have, as it is a conviction we uphold. It's interesting because whenever we talk about faith, especially in Christian theology, they often think about faith as just this entire deposit of teachings.
What you're going to see here, that faith isn't so much that list of things you say yes to, but it is a choice that you make to make the sacred a part of your life.
Look at what James Martin says in the reading.
While you already have the basics. Soil, seeds, water, you have to cultivate and nurture.

What religion does for you is that it gives you the materials to cultivate your faith. It gives you teachings, it gives you practices, it gives you certain ways of living, but you have to cultivate that yourself.

The way my faith looks like may have similarities to your faith, but it's going to be very different. Our faith can have similarities... but ultimately they are different. And I can't impose my way of relating to God to... anyone else.
Allow me to offer this idea coming from St. Thomas Aquinas.
For Thomas Aquinas he says that when it comes to the existence of God, the existence of God is not self evident.
What that means is that we cannot obviously without any controversy, say that there is a God... God's existence, that's controversial.
No one here, I think would reasonably deny the existence of air. God's not like the air we breathe. It's not like it's controversial that there's air, but God's existence, that's controversial.
Maybe that's what faith is about: it enables us to grow out of these images, especially if they tend to be distorted.
It is this very inexplicability, moreover, that may explain the origin of religious consciousness and the emergence of its principal vehicle, which is faith.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: But belief is not faith. Belief can take form as assent to dogma without gaining anything spiritually from that assent—without the commitment that marks genuine faith.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: This doesn't mean that you need to believe in God in order to find Ignatius's insights useful. But to do so, you have to understand where God fits into his worldview.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Their lives, like every life, are not free from suffering, but faith enables them to put their sufferings into a framework of meaning.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Others sometimes envy people who walk along the path of belief. "If only I had faith like you!” one friend often tells me. While I understand her sentiment, that perspective makes faith seem like something you have rather than have to work at keeping.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: It's as if you're born with unquestioning faith, like being born with red hair or brown eyes. Or as if faith were like pulling into a gas station and filling your tank. Neither metaphor is apt.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Ultimately, faith is a gift from God. But faith isn't something that you just have.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Perhaps a better metaphor is that faith is like a garden: while you may already have the basics— soil, seeds, water—you have to cultivate and nourish it. Like a garden, faith takes patience, persistence, and even work.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: An adult life requires an adult faith.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Think of it this way: you wouldn't consider yourself equipped to face life with a third-grader's understanding of math. Yet people often expect the religious instruction they had in grammar school to sustain them in the adult world.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: In his book A Friendship Like No Other, the Jesuit spiritual writer William A. Barry invites adults to relate to God in an adult way. Just as an adult child needs to relate to his or her parent in a new way, he suggests, so adult believers need to relate to God in new ways as they mature.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: As I mentioned, you would hardly consider yourself an educated adult if you ended your academic training as a child. Yet many believers cease their religious education as children, and expect it to carry them through adulthood. People in this group often find that they need to reeducate themselves to understand their faith in a mature way.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This lukewarm agnosticism came to a boil during my college days at the University of Pennsylvania. During freshman and sophomore years at Penn, my friends and I spent many late nights arguing loudly about religion (usually after too many beers or too much pot). Those late-night sessions raised doubts about the God to whom I had prayed when I was young. But at the time they were just random doubts and unconnected questions.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Her reply said that you can live with the question of suffering and still believe in God—much as a child can trust a parent even when he doesn't fully understand all of the parent's ways.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Not incidentally, he was also impressed by the composure and dignity of the Christian martyrs.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 'when I was delighting in [hearing] the Christians slandered, and saw them fearless of death, [I] perceived that it was impossible they could be living in wickedness and pleasure' (Second Apology, 11).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Dostoevsky's wide-ranging and nuanced engagement with atheism, motivated and informed by his personal grapplings with the subject - he once admitted that his own 'hosanna' had passed through 'a furnace of doubt' - is a case in point.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Tikhon speaks of 'complete' atheism and 'complete' faith, with the presumable implication that very few atheists are so completely, just as very few believers - i.e. the saints - are so completely.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Envisioning the Christian spiritual life as a series of ascending steps, Tikhon's metaphor implies 'the most complete atheism' isn't simply next-best to the 'most complete faith'. Instead it is a prerequisite for it: a stage that all those seeking complete faith must pass through en route.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 'The deeper the darkness'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: There is another, and arguably more significant, kind of 'atheism' within the Christian tradition: the experiential 'atheism' of some of the mystics, referring to experiences of 'godforsakenness'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: John of the Cross, Mother Teresa, Padre Pio and even Jesus himself were not atheists in the term's normal meaning. Nevertheless, the experiences of 'godforsakenness' that all underwent are worthy of comment.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: According to the Gospels, Christ cried out from the Cross the opening lines of Psalm 22: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Mark 15.34).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: By the medieval period, these crucial words of Christ's came to be deeply significant among Christian mystics identifying with the sufferings of the Cross.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In the medieval period, for example, influential figures such as Blessed Angela of Foligno (1248-1309), St Mechthild of Magdeburg (c. 1210-c. 1285) and Blessed Margaret Ebner (1291-1351) reported periods of feeling abandoned by God.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Two and a half centuries later, such episodes would receive their classic crystallization as 'the dark night of the soul' of the Spanish poet-theologian St John of the Cross.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: When this purgative contemplation oppresses a man, he feels very vividly indeed the shadow of death... all of which reflect the feeling of God's absence, of being chastised and rejected by Him, and of being unworthy of Him, as well as the object of his anger... for now it seems that this affliction will last forever. (St John of the Cross)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: I keep my eyes fixed on the East in the night which surrounds me... But I strain my eyes in vain... The more I fix my gaze the dimmer my sight becomes... the deeper the darkness which envelopes me. I am alone by day and night and no ray of light comes through to enlighten me. (Padre Pio, 1918)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Dostoevsky, too, seems not to have been a stranger to such episodes. When viewing Hans Holbein's Dead Christ in the Tomb... at the gallery in Basel, his wife reports that he stood transfixed, and seemed on the verge of an epileptic seizure.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: John of the Cross aside, perhaps the most famous sufferer of such spiritual desolation is Mother Teresa of Calcutta.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Her 40 years of near-constant 'darkness', thirsting for the God who had once felt so near (and sometimes, rarely and fleetingly, did still), were only fully revealed with the publication of her private writings in 2007.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Darkness is such that I really do not see - neither with my mind nor with my reason. - The place of God in my soul is blank. - There is no God in me. - When the pain of longing is so great - I just long & long for God - and then it is that I feel - He does not want me - He is not there... (Mother Teresa)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Given statements such as these, it is not at all surprising that Teresa's revelations were widely interpreted as proof of her having been an atheist, pure and simple (and thus, naturally, a hypocrite). But, of course, things are not nearly so simple.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: While one can indeed speak here of an experiential 'atheism' - the 'atheism' of one who acutely, and painfully, perceives God's seeming absence - this is plainly not atheism in any simple or straightforward sense of the word. Apparent atheistic statements are set alongside, and hence qualified by, expressions of abiding trust and abandonment to the One who seems so absent.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: When I try to raise my thoughts to Heaven - there is such convicting emptiness that those very thoughts return like sharp knives & hurt my soul... If this brings You glory... if my suffering satiates Your Thirst - here I am Lord, with joy I accept all to the end of life - & I will smile at Your Hidden Face - always. (Mother Teresa)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Just as Justin's affirmation of the Most True God qualifies and explains his acceptance of the label 'atheist', so too do Teresa's prayers concerning her lack of faith undermine any identification of her as an actual atheist.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf
