entries.content: It was precisely Teresa's feeling of abandonment that proved the catalyst for her ever-deepening faith in, and service to, Christ. The two hallmarks of her spirituality, devotion to the Eucharist and devotion to the destitute, stem from her longing to be in Christ's presence.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Taking Scripture at its word on both counts - 'This is my body' (Mark 14.22) and 'Just as you did it to one of the least of my brother, you did it to me' (Matt. 25.40) - she sought to fill the void of his absence at the level of her felt experience.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: It will be an abiding theme of this book that the difference between faith and unbelief - between following, howsoever falteringly, the Most True God, and not - is indeed one of mighty significance.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 2) creative doubt can play a role in keeping one's fundamental beliefs alive and vital.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Socrates would have argued for an ethics of belief: one is morally justified in holding a belief only to the extent that it can be justified after critical assessment.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: St. Emmanuel, Unamuno's creation, was a parish priest whose life consisted of consoling and helping his people, to the point that they depended on him thoroughly, refusing to die unless holding his hand.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Committed to his parishioners' happiness, Emmanuel argues that "it's better for them to believe everything, even things that contradict one another, than to believe nothing... Protestation destroys contentment and peace.'"
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Paul Tillich's analysis of faith is 'the state of being ultimately concerned.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Ultimate concern is always intentional: that is, if one is ultimately concerned, one is ultimately concerned about something.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Faith is wholly committing oneself to that which one believes to be of ultimate value.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Tillich himself argues that 'it must be stated as sharply and insistently as possible that in every act of faith there is cognitive affirmation.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Where there is cognitive affirmation, there is the possibility of error due to human limitations.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Tillich has forcefully insisted that doubt is a part of the very structure of faith.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Tillich distinguishes between methodological doubt (scientific humility), skeptical doubt ('an attitude of actually rejecting any certainty'), and existential doubt. He explicitly claims only the latter is an essential dimension of faith.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: The author argues that because faith involves cognitive affirmation, methodological and skeptical doubt should also be seen as part of the structure of faith.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Humility insists upon the question: Does one's understanding of God correspond to reality? Creative doubt encourages this question. Dogmatism suppresses it.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Keeping fundamental beliefs alive
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: A second argument stresses that creative doubt can play a role in keeping one's fundamental beliefs from becoming dead dogmas.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Most basic beliefs and value commitments are initially inherited. If these beliefs are not challenged by creative doubt, they tend to become simply verbal professions having little vitality.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: During the 1960s, T. J. Altizer's 'death of God theology' challenged traditional Christian belief at Emory University. An observer noted there was more 'god-talk' at Emory after Altizer arrived than in its entire previous history.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: The Altizer example underlines the fact that challenging traditional dogma can stimulate people to think seriously about their fundamental beliefs and commitments.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Fundamental beliefs need to be continually challenged if they are to remain alive and vigorous. Without creative doubt how do a person's beliefs mature? How does a person make his own that which he has simply inherited?
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: It is often painful to evaluate critically one's inherited beliefs, but the pain gives birth to beliefs that are alive. Out of the pain come vital ideas that the person has made his own.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: A final but crucial note: Simply because there is an element of doubt and uncertainty surrounding fundamental principles should not prohibit commitment in depth to one's basic beliefs.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: There is nothing incompatible between deep commitment to a principle and an open attitude (spawned by creative doubt) that makes possible a future reevaluation of that principle.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes once observed that, 'the highest courage is to stake everything on a premise that you know tomorrow may disprove.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Philip Blair Rice adds a deeper insight: 'If there is a still higher courage, it stems from the realization that the premises are such that tomorrow can neither prove nor disprove them in a way that will fully still our uncertainties.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Paul Tillich: 'Doubt is overcome... by courage. Courage does not deny that there is doubt, but it takes the doubt into itself as an expression of its own finitude.... Courage does not need the safety of an unquestionable conviction. It includes the risk without which no creative life is possible.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Faith is not an imposition.
Faith is an invitation.
Faith is like a garden (implying it requires cultivation, growth, and care).
see more [unclear?]
God's existence is not self-evident.
Faith is how we make sense of God's communication.
Faith
Faith is the acceptance of the sacred as personally and ultimately valuable.
The sacred is central to faith.
Faith can be chosen and not imposed.
Faith is not an object we "have" or possess.
Faith involves different ways of relating, but is grounded in belief and principles.

topics.review:
The topic of "Faith," situated within the course's second set, "Approaches to the Divine," presents faith not as a static set of doctrines to be memorized, but as a dynamic, deeply personal, and active response to an experience of the sacred. The fundamental idea is that faith is a choice and a commitment—a way of life that must be consciously cultivated. It is the human answer to the divine invitation, a process that is often marked by growth, struggle, doubt, and paradox.

The core definition of faith presented is "the acceptance of the sacred as personally and ultimately valuable." This definition immediately distinguishes faith from mere belief. An experience of the sacred—whether it is the profound inspiration felt from a grandmother's cooking, the awe of nature, or a moment of deep personal connection—can be a fleeting event. Faith, however, is the conscious decision to accept this experience and integrate it into one's life. It is an invitation, not an imposition. As the lectures emphasize, one cannot force faith upon another person; the more it is imposed, the less attractive it becomes. This is because faith is not an object one possesses, but a conviction one upholds through personal choice.

A central metaphor, drawn from the writings of James Martin, S.J., beautifully illustrates this active quality: faith is like a garden. While one may be given the basic elements—soil, seeds, and water (analogous to the teachings, practices, and community provided by a religion)—the individual must actively cultivate and nourish it. This requires patience, persistence, and work. This analogy powerfully refutes the common misconceptions of faith as something one is simply born with (like brown eyes) or a resource one can passively receive (like filling a car with gasoline). Each person's garden of faith will be unique, sharing similarities with others but ultimately cultivated in a personal way.

This personal cultivation necessitates growth. The course materials stress the need for an "adult faith" to navigate an adult life. Drawing on the work of Jesuit spiritual writer William A. Barry, the argument is made that just as a third-grader's understanding of mathematics is insufficient for an adult, a childhood understanding of religion cannot sustain a person through the complexities of adult life. Many people cease their religious education as children and find their simplistic faith unable to provide meaning amidst adult suffering and questions. An adult faith requires re-education and a willingness to relate to God in new, more mature ways, much like an adult child must forge a new kind of relationship with their parents.

A significant and perhaps surprising component of this mature faith is doubt. Far from being the opposite of faith, doubt is presented as an integral part of its very structure. St. Thomas Aquinas provides the foundation for this by noting that God's existence is not self-evident; it is a controversial matter, unlike the undeniable existence of the air we breathe. This lack of absolute, empirical proof creates the space where faith is necessary. Theologian Paul Tillich deepens this insight by defining faith as "the state of being ultimately concerned." For Tillich, because faith involves cognitive affirmation about ultimate reality, and because humans are finite, the possibility of error is always present. Therefore, "existential doubt"—the struggle concerning one's ultimate concern—is an unavoidable element of faith. Doubt is not overcome by certainty, but by courage, which embraces the risk inherent in any creative life.

This "creative doubt" serves a vital function: it prevents fundamental beliefs from becoming "dead dogmas" or empty verbal professions. By challenging inherited beliefs, doubt forces a person to critically assess and ultimately make those beliefs their own. This process can be painful, but it gives birth to a faith that is alive and vigorous. Dostoevsky's own admission that his faith passed through a "furnace of doubt" exemplifies this transformative struggle.

The most profound exploration of this theme comes from the concept of experiential "atheism" found within the Christian mystical tradition, particularly in the "dark night of the soul" articulated by St. John of the Cross. This is not atheism in the conventional sense of disbelief, but a profound and agonizing experience of God's absence or "godforsakenness." The most striking modern example is Mother Teresa of Calcutta, whose private writings revealed nearly forty years of continuous spiritual "darkness." She felt "the place of God in my soul is blank." Yet, these expressions of divine absence were always set alongside statements of abiding trust and commitment. This paradox is key: her feeling of abandonment became the very catalyst for her faith. It drove her to seek the absent Christ in the Eucharist and, most famously, in the poorest of the poor. This shows that the experience of darkness is not a failure of faith, but can be a crucible in which a deeper, more resilient faith is forged, echoing Jesus's own cry of forsakenness from the Cross.

In conclusion, faith is a multifaceted and challenging approach to the divine. It begins with a personal response to an experience of the sacred, develops through active cultivation, matures by outgrowing childish conceptions, and is paradoxically strengthened by the crucible of doubt and even the experience of divine absence. It builds upon the human desire for meaning (SET 1) and provides a personal framework for engaging with the teachings of religion (SET 2) and navigating the complexities of divine images and revelation (SET 3). It is not a path of easy answers or absolute certainty, but one of courageous, lifelong commitment.
keyTakeaways: Faith is the personal and ultimate acceptance of the sacred; it is an active choice and a commitment, not a static object to be possessed or a set of doctrines to be memorized.
Faith is an invitation, not an imposition. It cannot be forced on a person, as its essence lies in free, personal choice.

Using James Martin's metaphor, faith is like a garden: it requires active cultivation, patience, and work. Religion may provide the materials (teachings, practices), but the individual must personally nurture their faith.
An adult life requires an adult faith. As argued by William A. Barry, a childhood understanding of religion is insufficient to address the complex questions and sufferings of adulthood.
Belief, as intellectual assent to dogma, is distinct from faith, which is a deep, personal commitment that shapes one's life.
According to Paul Tillich, faith is 'the state of being ultimately concerned,' and existential doubt is an integral, unavoidable part of its structure, overcome by courage rather than certainty.
Creative doubt is essential for a mature faith. It challenges inherited beliefs, prevents them from becoming 'dead dogmas,' and allows an individual to make their faith truly their own.

The 'dark night of the soul' (St. John of the Cross) refers to an experiential 'atheism' within faith—a profound feeling of God's absence. As seen in Mother Teresa, this experience can paradoxically become the catalyst for a deeper commitment and service.

glossary.definition: The personal and ultimate acceptance of the sacred. It is not a possession but an active, chosen conviction that must be cultivated, shaping one's entire life.

A mature understanding and practice of faith that has grown beyond childhood conceptions. It is capable of grappling with complex life questions, suffering, and doubt, as advocated by William A. Barry.

Intellectual assent to a set of propositions or dogmas. It is distinguished from faith, as one can believe in a doctrine without the personal commitment and spiritual transformation that marks genuine faith.

A form of doubt that plays a positive role in faith by challenging inherited beliefs and preventing them from becoming stagnant or meaningless. This critical assessment helps a person make their faith personal and vital.

glossary.term: The Dark Night of the Soul
glossary.definition: A term from the mystic St. John of the Cross describing a stage in spiritual development characterized by a profound and painful sense of God's absence. It is an experience of spiritual desolation or 'godforsakenness' within the life of a believer.

In Paul Tillich's framework, this is the doubt that is inherent to faith itself. It concerns one's 'ultimate concern' and arises from human finitude. It is overcome not by achieving certainty but through courage.

A term describing the experience of 'godforsakenness' or divine absence felt by some deeply religious individuals, such as the mystics. It is not atheism in the sense of disbelief, but a painful perception of God's seeming absence within a life of faith.
Defined by theologian Paul Tillich as 'the state of being ultimately concerned.' This means faith is the act of committing oneself wholly to what one believes to be of ultimate value and meaning.
A form of doubt, distinguished by Paul Tillich, that is characteristic of scientific inquiry. It involves a systematic questioning of hypotheses as part of the process of seeking knowledge.
A form of doubt, distinguished by Paul Tillich, that represents a general attitude of rejecting the possibility of any certainty.

A concept from Paul Tillich, referring to that which an individual holds as having ultimate value, meaning, and importance, demanding total commitment. For Tillich, faith is the state of having an ultimate concern.

studyTips: Focus on the key distinction between 'faith' and 'belief.' An exam question might ask you to explain why someone can 'believe' all the right things but still lack 'faith.'
Be able to explain the 'garden' metaphor in detail. Contrast it with the rejected metaphors (born with faith like hair color, filling a tank at a gas station) to show a deeper understanding.

Master the paradox of 'atheism' within faith. Use Mother Teresa as your primary example to explain how the experience of God's absence (the dark night) can coexist with and even fuel a profound life of faith and service.
Connect Paul Tillich's definition of faith ('ultimate concern') with his view on doubt. Understand that for Tillich, courage, not certainty, is the answer to the existential doubt inherent in faith.

Think of the progression: a childhood faith is insufficient (Barry), so an adult faith is needed, and this adult faith is strong enough to include 'creative doubt' (Tillich) and even endure the 'dark night' (John of the Cross, Mother Teresa).
The adobo analogy is a simple but effective way to remember the core idea: an *experience* of the sacred leads to an *invitation* to faith, which is a *choice* to make it part of your life.

connections.relationship: Faith is the primary way a person responds to an experience of the Sacred. The Sacred presents an invitation, and faith is the personal, ultimate acceptance of that invitation.
connections.example: If you have a profound experience of the Sacred while looking at a sunset, that's the initial event. Faith is the choice to say, 'This experience is meaningful, and I will orient my life in a way that honors it.'

connections.relationship: Religion provides the 'materials' for faith. In the garden metaphor, religion is the soil, seeds, and tools (doctrines, rituals, community) that help cultivate and give structure to one's personal faith.
connections.example: A person's faith in God (a personal commitment) is expressed and nurtured through the specific prayers, scriptures, and sacraments provided by the Catholic religion.

connections.relationship: Faith is a core component of one's spirituality. While spirituality is the broader quest for connection and meaning, faith is the specific commitment to a particular way of understanding and relating to the ultimate.
connections.example: Many people might have a general 'spirituality' related to nature, but a person of faith might commit to seeing that nature as God's creation and actively care for it as an act of devotion.

connections.relationship: Faith is the human response to the divine as Mystery. A problem can be solved, but a mystery must be entered into. Faith is the act of entering the mystery of God without needing to have it all figured out.
connections.example: The suffering of an innocent person is a profound mystery. Faith doesn't 'solve' it but allows a person to trust in a framework of ultimate meaning (God's love) even amid incomprehensible pain.

connections.relationship: A mature, adult faith, especially one that incorporates 'creative doubt,' is the primary tool for correcting and moving beyond distorted or childish images of God.
connections.example: A person might grow up with a distorted image of God as a punishing scorekeeper. A mature faith, through questioning and reflection, can grow into an image of God as a merciful and loving parent.

connections.relationship: The concept of 'Christian Atheism' directly connects to the discussion of the 'dark night of the soul' within the topic of Faith. It shows that an experience of God's absence is a recognized, albeit painful, part of the Christian faith journey for some.
connections.example: Mother Teresa's decades-long experience of 'darkness' is a prime example of the experiential 'atheism' discussed in this course, demonstrating that it is a phenomenon within faith, not an abandonment of it.

connections.relationship: Revelation is God's self-communication to humanity. Faith is the human act of receiving, accepting, and responding to that Revelation.
connections.example: If God's Revelation is a love letter sent to humanity, faith is the act of reading that letter, believing it, and writing back with one's life.

connections.relationship: The innate human 'Desire' for more, for transcendence, is the starting point of the spiritual journey. Faith is one of the ultimate destinations of that desire, where the longing for the sacred finds a home in a committed relationship.
connections.example: The restless feeling that 'there must be something more to life' (Desire) can lead to a search that culminates in an experience of the Sacred, which then invites a person to a life of Faith.

examPrepQuestions.question: Using the 'garden' metaphor from the readings, explain why faith is not something one passively possesses but must actively cultivate. How does religion fit into this analogy?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The 'garden' metaphor, from James Martin, posits that faith must be actively cultivated. Like a garden, it requires work, patience, and persistence to grow. Religion provides the 'materials' for this cultivation—the soil (community), seeds (scripture), and water (sacraments)—but the individual must do the active work of planting, weeding, and nurturing. One cannot simply 'have' a garden; one must garden. Similarly, one cannot just 'have' faith; one must live it out.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain Paul Tillich's statement that doubt is a part of the very structure of faith. Why is it overcome by courage and not by certainty?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Paul Tillich defines faith as 'the state of being ultimately concerned.' Because this involves a cognitive claim about ultimate reality and because humans are finite, there is always the possibility of being wrong. This creates existential doubt. It cannot be overcome by certainty, because absolute proof for ultimate matters is impossible for finite beings. Instead, it is overcome by courage—the courage to commit and live fully despite the risk and uncertainty, taking the doubt into itself as an expression of one's finitude.

examPrepQuestions.question: How can the experience of 'the dark night of the soul,' as exemplified by Mother Teresa, be considered a part of faith rather than a loss of it?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The 'dark night of the soul' is an experience of God's profound absence or 'godforsakenness.' In Mother Teresa's case, this was not a loss of faith because her feeling of darkness coexisted with an unwavering commitment and trust in the God who seemed absent. Paradoxically, this painful void became the catalyst for her service; she actively sought the absent Christ in the Eucharist and in the poor. This shows that such an experience, while agonizing, can be a crucible that purifies and deepens faith rather than extinguishing it.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course materials, what is the primary definition of faith?
choices: A) The intellectual assent to a list of church doctrines.
B) The personal and ultimate acceptance of the sacred.
C) A feeling of certainty that God exists.
D) The cultural tradition one is born into.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core definition provided is that faith is 'the acceptance of the sacred. Personally and ultimately.' It is presented as a personal choice and commitment, distinct from simply agreeing with doctrines (A), having certainty (C), or cultural inheritance (D).

quizQuestions.question: The 'garden' metaphor for faith, attributed to James Martin, is meant to emphasize that faith...
choices: A) is a natural gift you are born with.
B) provides immediate and beautiful results.
C) requires active work, patience, and cultivation.
D) is a private matter that should not be shared.
quizQuestions.explanation: The garden metaphor highlights the active, ongoing effort required for faith. It takes 'patience, persistence, and even work,' contrasting with the idea that it's a passive gift (A) or provides instant gratification (B).

quizQuestions.question: According to William A. Barry, why is a 'childhood faith' often insufficient for an adult?
choices: A) Because children are incapable of real faith.
B) Because adult life presents complex challenges and sufferings that a simplistic faith cannot address.
C) Because adults should abandon religion in favor of science.
D) Because church teachings change so frequently.
quizQuestions.explanation: Barry's argument, using the 'third-grader's math' analogy, is that an unexamined childhood faith is not equipped to handle adult complexities, doubts, and sufferings, thus requiring a move toward a more mature, 'adult faith'.

quizQuestions.question: Paul Tillich defines faith as 'the state of being ultimately concerned.' What does he argue is an essential part of this faith?
choices: A) Absolute certainty
B) Existential doubt
C) Daily church attendance
D) Blind obedience
quizQuestions.explanation: Tillich forcefully insists that because faith involves cognitive affirmation made by finite humans, existential doubt is an essential and unavoidable dimension of the very structure of faith.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of 'the dark night of the soul,' described by St. John of the Cross, refers to:
choices: A) A state of final damnation and loss of salvation.
B) A period of intellectual questioning about doctrine.
C) An experience of feeling abandoned by God within a life of faith.
D) The decision to become an atheist after being a believer.
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'dark night' is not a loss of faith (D) or simple intellectual doubt (B), but a profound and painful spiritual desolation where a believer feels God's absence vividly, as if 'chastised and rejected by Him'.

quizQuestions.question: How did Mother Teresa's experience of spiritual 'darkness' influence her life's work?
choices: A) It caused her to abandon her ministry and live in seclusion.
B) It proved to her that God did not exist, making her a hypocrite.
C) It became the catalyst for her to seek the absent Christ in the Eucharist and the poor.
D) It made her write books condemning the Church for its lack of answers.
quizQuestions.explanation: The materials explain that her feeling of abandonment was the 'catalyst for her ever-deepening faith in, and service to, Christ.' Her longing for the absent God drove her to find Him in 'the least of these' and in the Eucharist.

quizQuestions.question: According to the notes, what is the primary function of 'creative doubt'?
choices: A) To lead a person to atheism.
B) To make a person feel guilty about their beliefs.
C) To keep fundamental beliefs from becoming 'dead dogmas' by forcing critical assessment.
D) To prove that all religious beliefs are false.
quizQuestions.explanation: Creative doubt is presented as a positive force that revitalizes faith. By challenging inherited beliefs, it forces a person to think seriously and make the beliefs their own, preventing them from being empty, unexamined professions.

quizQuestions.question: The lecture distinguished between 'faith' and 'belief.' 'Belief' was characterized as:
choices: A) A deeper, more authentic commitment than faith.
B) A personal relationship with the divine.
C) An outdated concept in modern theology.
D) Possible intellectual assent to dogma without the commitment of genuine faith.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source material clearly states, 'But belief is not faith. Belief can take form as assent to dogma without gaining anything spiritually from that assent—without the commitment that marks genuine faith.'

quizQuestions.question: According to the lecture, citing St. Thomas Aquinas, why is faith necessary for a person to accept God's existence?
choices: A) Because God's existence is self-evident and obvious to everyone.
B) Because God's existence is not self-evident and is a controversial matter.
C) Because only priests are allowed to prove God's existence.
D) Because God's existence was proven false by science.
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture explains Aquinas's view that God's existence is 'not self-evident.' Unlike the air we breathe, it is a 'controversial' topic, which creates the space where faith, as a choice and commitment, becomes necessary.

quizQuestions.question: The story about the experience of tasting 'Lola's adobo' was used to illustrate that faith often begins with:
choices: A) A long and difficult period of academic study.
B) A personal experience that becomes an invitation to a deeper commitment.
C) A command from a religious authority figure.
D) A random, meaningless event.
quizQuestions.explanation: The adobo story illustrates how a powerful personal experience (tasting the food) can spark an invitation (to learn the recipe) that leads to a commitment (preserving the family cooking tradition). This parallels how an experience of the sacred can invite a person into a life of faith.

topics.title: Spirituality

glossaryTerms.definition: The personal, lived experience and quest for connection with a transcendent reality or the divine. It is concerned not just with what one believes, but with who one becomes through practices like prayer, meditation, and reflection.

The organized, communal expression of faith, which includes shared beliefs, rituals, moral codes, and a body of accumulated wisdom and tradition. It provides a structure and community for personal spirituality.

An acronym describing a popular modern stance where an individual identifies as having a spiritual life but does not adhere to a specific organized religion. They often cultivate a personal faith by drawing from various traditions.

A term coined by sociologist Robert Bellah to describe a purely individualistic and privatized form of faith, named after a person he interviewed. It serves as a key example of the potential pitfalls of spirituality detached from a religious community.
A Greek term for a profound change of heart and mind. In a theological context, it means a transformative turning of the soul away from self-sufficiency and toward God.

A concept from the philosopher Simone Weil, who asserted that pure, disciplined attention directed toward God is the highest form of prayer. It signifies active, contemplative engagement rather than passive thought.
A form of prayer that moves beyond petition (asking for things) and focuses on a simple, silent awareness of God's presence. Its goal is communion and relationship rather than communication of needs.

A concept in Christian spirituality, particularly from St. John of the Cross, describing a stage of spiritual development characterized by a sense of God's absence. The notes refer to it as a form of 'purgative contemplation'—a period of spiritual purification.

A concept from Socrates, who argued that "the unexamined life is not worth living." In a spiritual context, it implies that a mature faith requires a continual and creative reappraisal of one's fundamental beliefs and values.

entries.content: One thing, for instance, is that I go to Mass daily. I don't force anyone to go to Mass daily, but certainly that's something I do to center myself and to reconnect myself to the more important things in life.

I also acknowledge that there are many people who do not have this context and in fact have struggles with religion or probably have a conviction which is similar to being spiritual but not religious. So we're going to talk about all of that in this module.

And then I actually learned how to cook her adobo by cooking every day. And that cooking indeed becomes part of my daily routines. Because it's one thing to want to cook and it's another to actually cook.

Quite often... you do meet a lot of people who claim some kind of spirituality. It's like they want to figure out who God is, but they don't like religion... they have many hang ups about the way religion talks about God. And somehow, in a way that they don't often care to admit, there's something quite attractive about him. Mysterium tremendum.
Student: So spirituality is basically one's I guess, ability to be religious.
Student: Spirituality is like believing in the spiritual rather than the physical.
We talked about four types of spirituality, meaning one and sacred.
Balance is used when you're dealing with two contrasting things, like work and play. Whereas spirituality and religion are not contrasts. They supplement each other.
Spirituality becomes grounded in a religion, and a religion becomes more personal for someone because of spirituality.
In recent years, amid the quantum antics of technology and the violent insanity of global restiveness, I have been reading more about religion and spirituality than about any other topic.

While I had ample experience praying contemplatively on my own, and worshipping together during Catholic Masses, the Quakers' 'gathered silence' (praying silently together) was a type of contemplation I'd never before imagined. Their tradition enriched my own.

entries.content: SPIRITUAL BUT NOT RELIGIOUS
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Before we tackle the question of how to find God, a digression on two important ideas: religion and spirituality.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Everybody seems to be spiritual these days—from your college roommate to the person in the office cubicle next to yours to the subject of every other celebrity interview. But if “spiritual” is fashionable, “religious” is unfashionable. This is usually expressed as follows: “I'm spiritual but not religious.”
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: It's even referred to by the acronym SBNR. There are so many people who describe themselves as SBNR that sometimes I wonder if the Jesuits might attract more people if they promoted the Spiritual but Not Religious Exercises.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Still, it's not surprising that, given all the problems with organized religion, many people would say, 'I'm not religious,' adding, 'I'm serious about living a moral life, maybe even one that centers on God, but I'm my own person.' Spiritual, on the other hand, is taken to mean that, freed from unnecessary dogma, you can be yourself before God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The term may also imply that you have sampled a variety of religious beliefs that you have integrated into your life. You meditate at a Buddhist temple (which is great); participate in Seders with Jewish friends at Passover (great too); sing in a gospel choir at a local Baptist church (great again); and go to Midnight Mass on Christmas Eve at a Catholic church (also great). You find what works for you, but you don't subscribe to any one church: that would be too confining. Besides, there's no one creed that represents exactly what you believe.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: But there's a problem. While 'spiritual' is obviously healthy, 'not religious' may be another way of saying that faith is something between you and God. And while faith is a question of you and God, it's not just a question of you and God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Let's consider a person who wants to follow Jesus Christ on her own. Perhaps she has heard that if she follows Christ, she will enjoy financial success—a popular idea today. Were she part of a mainstream Christian community, though, she would be reminded that suffering is part of the life of even the most devout Christian. Without the wisdom of a community, she may gravitate toward a skewed view of Christianity. Once she falls on hard times financially, she may drop Christ, who has ceased to meet her personal needs.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This reminds me of a passage from a book called Habits of the Heart, written by Robert Bellah, a sociologist of religion, and other colleagues, in which they interviewed a woman named Sheila about her religious beliefs. 'I believe in God,' she said. 'I'm not a religious fanatic. I can't remember the last time I went to church. My faith has carried me a long way. It's Sheilaism. Just my own little voice.'
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: More problematic than Sheilaism are spiritualities entirely focused on the self, with no place for humility, self-critique, or a sense of responsibility for the community. Certain New Age movements find their goal not in God, or even the greater good, but in self-improvement—a valuable goal-but one that may degenerate into selfishness.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Religion without spirituality can become a dry list of dogmatic statements divorced from the life of the spirit. This is what Jesus warned against.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Spirituality without religion can become a self-centered complacency divorced from the wisdom of a community. That's what I'm warning against.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: For St. Ignatius Loyola the two went hand in hand. (If anything, Ignatius was criticized for being too spiritual, as his way struck some people as not centered enough on the church.) His way understands the importance of being both spiritual and religious.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Socrates, as recounted in Plato's Apology, argued: The unexamined life is not worth living.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Socrates meant that to be a rational human being is to have a kind of creative doubt about one's fundamental value beliefs, a creative doubt that promotes, within reason, a continual reappraisal of such beliefs.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: To avoid thinking about such questions would perhaps make one happier, but also 'a little less human, and her fundamental beliefs would be considerably less alive.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Dostoevsky's insight is that freedom is a burden, for with freedom comes the responsibility of continually reevaluating the very foundations of one's life. To flee from this burden is to flee from the very freedom that is a defining characteristic of the fully human.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: As Peter Bertocci put it, such flight 'is to miss the whole point of being human.'
