topics.review:
In our exploration of SET3: Images and Revelation, the topic of "Perfectionism" serves as a critical examination of a subtle yet profound spiritual obstacle. At its core, perfectionism is not merely a personality trait characterized by high standards; it is a theological and spiritual pitfall rooted in the desire for excessive control. It represents a fundamental misunderstanding of the nature of God, the world, and ourselves. This topic matters immensely because this drive for control, this demand for flawlessness, paradoxically becomes the very thing that stifles spiritual growth, distorts our image of the divine, and can lead to significant harm to ourselves and the communities we are part of.

The course materials define perfectionism as an "excessive control over the processes of one's life, situations, difficulties, and paradoxes." It is a posture that demands reality adjust to one's own idealized standards, rather than adjusting oneself to the complex, often messy, nature of reality. This is a form of ego, whether it stems from arrogance (overinflating one's own importance) or insecurity (an obsessive focus on one's own perceived flaws). It is the toxic belief that the goal of life is to achieve full control, a goal that is not only impossible but spiritually damaging.

A central framework for understanding this topic is the "Three Dangers of Perfectionism." First, perfectionism disables us from seeing life as it truly is. It creates a sheltered existence, filtering reality through a lens of what *should* be, rather than engaging with what *is*. By relying on pre-thought solutions and overthinking ('Order'), the perfectionist fears the randomness and unexpected scenarios of life ('Chaos'). While a life that is too chaotic can become unstructured and lazy, the course notes emphasize that a life that is too ordered becomes anxious and critical. True growth, it is argued, happens in the 'chaos'—in the moments when things don't go our way. Perfectionism resists this, limiting our perspectives and keeping us from the very experiences that would foster wisdom and resilience.

Second, perfectionism renders growth unnecessary or impossible. Growth is a dynamic process, not a static achievement. The course posits that "the Good Life is about a dynamic state of growth rather than a static state of perfection." A perfectionist, fearing failure, avoids the very 'chase for perfectioning'—the process of becoming better—that defines a flourishing life. Growth happens when we learn how to lose well, when bad results teach us lessons. Perfectionism, by demanding a flawless outcome, denies the value of the process. This is highlighted by the insight that "mediocrity is judged by the process, not the result." A lucky win from a lazy effort is a mediocre process, and one cannot build a life on luck. Perfectionism’s fear of an imperfect process leads to stagnation, disabling action and perpetuating a state of being 'stuck'.

Third, perfectionism makes us more prone to harm ourselves and others. "Perfectionism has victims," the notes state repeatedly. For the self, it manifests as a constant feeling of inadequacy, of 'not measuring up,' often fueled by an image of God as a harsh parent. This leads to profound shame and self-harm, whether psychological or physical. It also harms others by placing impossible expectations on them. We project our need for flawlessness onto our friends, families, and communities, judging them harshly when they inevitably fall short. This tendency is driven by people-pleasing and ego-centric impulses, creating a toxic environment for all.

This spiritual danger is powerfully illustrated by the story of the friend searching for a perfect church. He is an intelligent, compassionate man who believes in God. Yet, his search leads him from his family’s Episcopalian church (too affluent) to considering a Catholic church (disagreed on women's ordination), to Buddhism (couldn't reconcile with his personal God), and finally to a Unitarian church (not a clear enough belief system). His quest for a flawless community, one that perfectly aligned with all his ideals, ended in him belonging to no community at all. He stays home on Sundays. This story is a stark warning that "the search for a perfect religious community is a futile one" and that "the danger of not settling for any tradition because none is perfect" is spiritual isolation. It is a modern-day enactment of the old adage, "Do not let the perfect be the enemy of the good."

In the larger context of Theology 11, perfectionism is a lens through which we can understand several key course themes. It is a prime example of a "Distorted Image of God," casting the divine as a demanding taskmaster rather than a source of grace. It fundamentally misunderstands the nature of "Revelation," which requires an open and humble posture to receive truth, a posture perfectionism's need for control makes impossible. By rejecting the untidy, paradoxical reality of life and faith, the perfectionist treats God and community as a "Problem" to be solved rather than a "Mystery" to be lived. Ultimately, abandoning the real, imperfect church for an idealized, non-existent one can be seen as a form of "Christian 'Atheism'"—a disbelief in the embodied, messy, and yet sacred reality of the faith community here and now. The antidote, as suggested by the puzzle piece analogy, is to embrace our imperfections, for it is these jagged edges that allow us to connect with God and with others into a beautiful, complex, and much larger picture.
keyTakeaways: Perfectionism is a spiritual pitfall defined by an excessive desire for control over life's processes, rooted in a demand that reality conform to one's idealized standards.
The first danger of perfectionism is that it disables us from seeing life as it truly is, sheltering us from the 'chaos' of unexpected events where true growth occurs.
The second danger is that it renders growth impossible by fearing failure and prioritizing a flawless result over the dynamic 'process of perfectioning.'
The third danger is that perfectionism makes us prone to harming ourselves (through shame and inadequacy) and others (through unrealistic expectations).

In a religious context, perfectionism leads to a futile search for a perfect community, often resulting in spiritual isolation, as illustrated by the story of the man who rejected every church for its flaws.
Perfectionism fosters a distorted image of God as a harsh, demanding parent, where presence and relationship are replaced by performance and judgment.
The spiritual alternative to perfectionism is embracing the maxim 'Do not let the perfect be the enemy of the good,' valuing process over outcome, and learning to 'lose well.'
The good life is not a static state of perfection but a dynamic state of growth, which requires embracing our imperfections as the very means by which we connect to a larger reality.

glossary.definition: A spiritual and psychological disposition defined by the need for excessive control over the processes of one's life, situations, difficulties, and paradoxes. It is characterized by a demand that reality adjust to one's idealized expectations rather than adjusting to reality as it is.
A static, flawless state where nothing goes wrong and nothing needs to be added. In this course, it is contrasted with the dynamic and imperfect nature of reality and growth.
A state of relying on pre-thought solutions and overthinking to control outcomes. An excessive attachment to order leads to anxiety, criticism, and a fear of the unknown.

The randomness of life, including unexpected events, difficulties, and scenarios that are outside of one's control. While feared by perfectionism, this is the environment where genuine growth and learning occur.
The concept that a good and flourishing life is characterized by a continuous process of learning, developing, and becoming, rather than by achieving a static, final state of perfection.
An unchanging, flawless end-state that perfectionism seeks but which is ultimately lifeless and contrary to the nature of spiritual development. It is the antithesis of a dynamic state of growth.

The ongoing process of becoming better or growing. This concept emphasizes the value of the journey, the effort, and the learning from failures, as opposed to focusing solely on achieving a perfect final product.

The idea that quality should be judged by the effort and intention of the process, not just the outcome. An example is achieving a good result through sheer luck despite a lazy or poor process, which is considered mediocre.
The self-centered driver of perfectionism. It can manifest as arrogance (overinflating one's importance and control) or insecurity (an obsessive focus on one's own perceived flaws and performance).

studyTips: Commit the 'Three Dangers of Perfectionism' to memory: 1) distorts reality, 2) stifles growth, 3) causes harm. This is a foundational framework and is highly likely to be an essay question.

Be able to recount and analyze the story of the friend who couldn't find a perfect church. It's the primary case study for how perfectionism manifests in a religious context, leading to spiritual isolation.

Focus on the key distinction between 'perfection' (a static, dead-end state) and 'perfectioning' (a dynamic, life-giving process). This contrast is central to the course's argument against perfectionism.

Practice connecting 'Perfectionism' to 'Distorted Images of God.' A common exam question might ask you to explain how a perfectionist mindset creates a false image of God as a harsh judge rather than a loving parent.
Remember the puzzle piece analogy: our 'imperfect' shape is what allows us to connect to the bigger picture of community and God. This is a simple but powerful metaphor to use in your answers.
The phrase 'Do not let the perfect be the enemy of the good' is the thesis statement for overcoming perfectionism. Be ready to explain its meaning using specific examples from the course notes.

connections.relationship: Perfectionism is a primary source of distorted images of God. It projects its own need for control and flawlessness onto the divine, creating an image of God as a harsh, demanding scorekeeper rather than a God of grace and mercy.
connections.example: Someone who believes they are only lovable to God when they perform perfectly (in prayer, morals, etc.) is operating under a perfectionist-driven, distorted image of God.

connections.relationship: Perfectionism treats life and faith as a 'Problem' to be solved and controlled. The alternative, embracing Mystery, involves surrendering control and entering into the unpredictable and paradoxical nature of life and God, which a perfectionist cannot do.
connections.example: A perfectionist approaches a community conflict by trying to find a flawless solution that pleases everyone, treating it as a problem. A mystery-based approach would involve patient presence and accepting that a perfect, clean solution may not exist.

connections.relationship: Perfectionism has an extreme aversion to paradox. It seeks a world of 'either/or' clarity and rejects the 'both/and' nature of reality. It cannot accept that a church can be both holy and sinful, or that a person can be both good and flawed.
connections.example: The friend who rejects the Catholic Church for one flaw (women's ordination) while ignoring its positive aspects (service to the poor) is demonstrating a perfectionist inability to hold a paradoxical, imperfect reality.

connections.relationship: True revelation requires an open, humble, and receptive posture. Perfectionism blocks revelation because it is closed off; it's not trying to see what is truly there, but is trying to force reality to conform to its own pre-conceived perfect image.
connections.example: If God's revelation comes through the 'chaos' of a personal failure, a perfectionist will be too busy being ashamed or trying to regain control to receive the lesson or insight being offered.

connections.relationship: The story of the friend who ends up in no church directly illustrates the pitfall of the 'Path of Independence,' which is the danger of not settling for any tradition because none is perfect. It shows the lonely end-point of this path when driven by perfectionism.
connections.example: The friend's journey through different denominations and his ultimate rejection of all of them is a textbook case of this specific pitfall associated with the path of the spiritual seeker.

connections.relationship: When perfectionism leads a person to reject every real, living, imperfect church in favor of an idealized, non-existent perfect community, they are engaging in a form of practical atheism. They are disbelieving in the actual, incarnate Body of Christ as it exists in the world.
connections.example: Saying 'I believe in God, but I don't believe in the Church' can, if driven by perfectionism, become a form of Christian 'Atheism' because it rejects the primary, albeit flawed, vehicle of God's presence in community.

connections.relationship: Faith and perfectionism are opposing spiritual postures. Faith involves trust, surrender, and venturing into the unknown. Perfectionism involves skepticism, control, and demanding certainty. You cannot simultaneously trust God and try to control God.
connections.example: A person of faith plants a seed and trusts God for the harvest. A perfectionist tries to control the weather, soil pH, and sunlight, becoming anxious and crushed if the plant isn't flawless.

examPrepQuestions.question: According to the course material, what are the three main dangers of perfectionism? Briefly explain each.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The three main dangers are: 1) It disables us from seeing life as it is, by sheltering us from the 'chaos' where growth happens. 2) It renders growth impossible, because its fear of failure prevents engagement in the learning process. 3) It makes us prone to harm, causing shame in ourselves and leading us to place damaging, unrealistic expectations on others.

examPrepQuestions.question: How does the story of the friend searching for a church illustrate the spiritual pitfalls of perfectionism?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The story illustrates how perfectionism in a religious context leads to spiritual isolation. The friend's futile search for a flawless community causes him to reject every real, imperfect church he encounters. This demonstrates the maxim 'do not let the perfect be the enemy of the good,' and shows how this mindset can lead one to abandon community altogether.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain the relationship between perfectionism and having a 'distorted image of God.'
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Perfectionism creates a distorted image of God by projecting its own need for control and flawlessness onto the divine. This results in seeing God not as a source of grace, but as a harsh parent or a cosmic judge who only values performance. This image leads to shame and a feeling of never measuring up, blocking a genuine relationship.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best defines 'perfectionism' in the context of this course?
choices: A) An excessive desire for control over life's processes and a demand that reality conform to one's ideals.
B) A healthy personality trait of having high standards and a strong work ethic.
C) The theological belief that humans can achieve a sinless state on Earth.
D) A focus on aesthetics and the appreciation of perfect art and beauty.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. The course defines perfectionism not as high standards, but as a spiritually damaging need for excessive control and a refusal to accept reality's imperfections. B is what perfectionism is often confused with. C and D are outside the scope of the definition provided.

quizQuestions.question: The story of the friend who cannot find a church to join is used to illustrate which pitfall?
choices: A) The danger of syncretism.
B) The failure of organized religion to be relevant.
C) The spiritual isolation that results from seeking a perfect, flawless community.
D) The superiority of a private, individual spirituality.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. The friend's journey ends in isolation because no real-world church could meet his perfect standards. This demonstrates the core idea that the futile search for the perfect is the enemy of finding the good.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course notes, where does true growth primarily occur?
choices: A) In a state of perfect order and control.
B) In the 'chaos' of unexpected events and when things don't go your way.
C) Through solitary contemplation, away from the world's problems.
D) Only after one has achieved a state of near-perfection.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. The notes explicitly state that growth happens in chaos and when we learn to 'lose well.' Perfectionism, which fears chaos and clings to order (A), actively stifles this growth process.

quizQuestions.question: 'The good life is about a dynamic state of growth rather than a static state of perfection.' This statement argues against which perfectionist tendency?
choices: A) Valuing the process ('perfectioning') over the final outcome.
B) Seeing life as a problem to be solved.
C) Focusing only on the final, flawless result and fearing the messy process of getting there.
D) Being too 'chill' and unstructured.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. This statement directly contrasts the life-giving 'dynamic state of growth' (the process) with the dead-end 'static state of perfection' (the final product). Perfectionism is obsessed with the latter, fearing the imperfect journey.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 'Three Dangers of Perfectionism' discussed in the material?
choices: A) It disables us from seeing life as it is.
B) It renders growth unnecessary.
C) It leads to financial instability.
D) It makes us more prone to harm ourselves and others.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. While perfectionism can have wide-ranging consequences, financial instability was not listed as one of the three core spiritual dangers. The dangers are its effects on perception, growth, and causing harm.

quizQuestions.question: The image of God as a 'harsh parent' who instills a feeling of 'I didn't measure up' is a direct consequence of:
choices: A) A healthy sense of humility.
B) A perfectionist and distorted image of God.
C) The path of devotion.
D) The concept of the Sacred.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. This image is a key example of how a perfectionist mindset projects its own impossible standards onto God, creating a distorted image that leads to shame, not grace.

quizQuestions.question: The puzzle piece analogy suggests that:
choices: A) Every person should strive to be a perfectly shaped, standalone piece.
B) A person's imperfections are what allow them to connect to a bigger picture.
C) Life is a confusing puzzle that can never be solved.
D) Some people are simply not shaped to fit into society.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. The analogy is used to show that our flaws and 'imperfect shape' are not defects, but are necessary for connection and belonging within a community or a larger reality.

quizQuestions.question: How does perfectionism relate to the course concept of 'Mystery'?
choices: A) It embraces mystery as the highest form of perfection.
B) It treats mystery as a problem to be solved and controlled, thus rejecting its nature.
C) It finds perfection within the heart of mystery.
D) It is unrelated to the concept of mystery.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. A core aspect of 'Mystery' is that it is something to be entered into, not solved or controlled. Perfectionism, with its need for control and predictable outcomes, is fundamentally opposed to this posture of surrender.

quizQuestions.question: The idea that 'mediocrity is judged by the process, not the result' challenges perfectionism by:
choices: A) Suggesting that results are completely unimportant.
B) Praising lazy work that gets lucky.
C) Prioritizing the effort, learning, and intention of an action over a potentially lucky outcome.
D) Arguing that only mediocre people focus on the process.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. This statement shifts the focus of value from the flawless result (which perfectionism demands) to the quality of the process itself, which is where growth and learning happen.

quizQuestions.question: A student who refuses to join any campus organization because each one has a flaw (e.g., 'too cliquey,' 'not serious enough,' 'too demanding') is exhibiting a behavior most similar to:
choices: A) The path of the activist.
B) The experience of wonder.
C) The friend in the reading who ends up not belonging to any church.
D) The concept of absolute certainty.
quizQuestions.explanation: Correct. This is a direct application of the main story in the reading. The student's search for a 'perfect' organization mirrors the friend's search for a 'perfect' church, both likely to end in isolation because they let the perfect be the enemy of the good.

topics.title: Distorted Images of God

glossaryTerms.definition: Inaccurate or incomplete perceptions of God that are formed by personal experiences, psychological projections, or an imbalanced understanding of divine attributes. These images hinder a mature and authentic relationship with the Divine.

A distorted image of God as a cosmic rule-maker who is primarily focused on strict compliance with rules and is eager to punish infractions. This image emphasizes fear and obedience over love and relationship.

A distorted image of God who sets an impossibly high standard of perfection but provides no assistance. This God's primary tool is shame, demanding conformity and fostering a sense of constant failure.

A distorted image of God who prioritizes human comfort above all, setting no real moral or spiritual standards and permitting any behavior. This image mistakes enablement for love and leads to spiritual complacency and immaturity.

A distorted image of God as a remote creator who, like a watchmaker, wound up the universe and now leaves it to run on its own without any personal involvement in human affairs. This view fosters complacency about moral and spiritual life.

The act of worshipping something other than God as if it were God. In this context, it refers to elevating one's own limited, distorted, or projected images of God to the status of the divine, worshipping the symbol or pointer rather than the ultimate reality.

A term describing an image of God that is made small and convenient enough to be controlled by the individual. This 'god' can be ignored when it makes uncomfortable demands and brought out only when it serves one's needs, representing a self-serving and idolatrous faith.

The psychological process of unconsciously attributing one's own brokenness, fears, desires, or negative traits onto God. This is a primary cause of distorted images, where the 'god' one relates to is a reflection of the human ego rather than the Divine reality.

A deistic analogy, referenced in connection with the 'Distant Puppeteer' image, that posits God created the universe with its laws (like a watchmaker building a watch) but then left it to operate on its own without further intervention.

A term used by St. Paul (1 Cor. 8) and early Christian writers like Pseudo-Justin to refer to idols, demons, or mythological figures that are falsely given the name and worship due to the one true God. This highlights the distinction between the true God and all forms of idolatrous imposters.

entries.content: Our first contact with anybody always gives impressions. And think of that word impression, right? A synonym for that is the word image.
Quite often in any relationship, we always operate on some image we have of a person.

Maybe as you get closer to that person, you have a high resolution image of that person... Quite often when you meet a person for the first time, you still have a very blurry image. And that blurry image is often formed by our biases too.
According to Dr. Chris Pritchett in his recent lecture, Healing Our Images of God and Ourselves...

He says our cognitive beings are acceptable through reason, but our inner images are formed through experiences in deeper and more powerful ways from the messages we have received primarily in childhood, but even throughout our lives as well.

If I asked one of you... 'can you define for me what a father is?' ...he will not give me a dictionary definition... Most likely, when I say define what a father is, who will you think about? Your own father.
We do not start with concepts because you do not know someone conceptually. Experience tells us that we get to know someone first through our images or our impressions of them.
There is a difference between our concept of God, the concepts that maybe we believe or we were told to believe, and what our real images of God are.
According to Pritchett, who you think God is, is based on your images, not your concepts. Who God really is for you, is based on your images of God, not your concept.

You have been told that God is a loving father... But then what if your own experience of fatherhood isn't good? ... How can you reconcile loving father, that whole concept, with your actual image of a father that comes from your experience? There's a disconnect between our concepts and our images.

One of the things that people, you know, that scripture always says is God is always watching. Some find that to be a comforting thought, but there are people who find that a little bit creepy. ... It's that God is surveilling every little thing I do.
Allow me to show you now four distorted images of God.
God is the strict enforcer. This is a God who makes rules for you to follow and is eager to punish you if you don't follow them.
This image [The Strict Enforcer] emphasizes compliance.
The moment a relationship becomes all about the rules, it then becomes a burden.
The harsh parent... this is a God who sets a high bar of moral and spiritual perfection for you to be, but does not help.
This one [The Harsh Parent] emphasizes conformity... the harsh parent has this picture perfect performative image of you that you have to fit.

These are two distorted images that negate the human person. They're negative images because... they limit your humanity, they shrink it. You're going to see in these next two images that these are images that now are positive... distorted images... they over emphasize the human being.
The enabling friend... This is a God who yeah, expects me to be good in some general sense, but he doesn't set any real standards for me to follow.
The enabling Friend emphasizes comfort, that you do not have to follow any real moral or spiritual standard because no matter what you do, God is going to permit everything that you do.
How is it a problem when you think that love means permitting everything that a person does?

Be careful with the word enabled, right? Because it might seem like you're simply respecting a person's decisions, but if you're allowing them to take a path that's not good, it could actually lead to their death.

The distant puppeteer. Now this image is of God simply being this distant controller of the universe, someone who just controls the universe in some grand sense... But that's it. God really doesn't care about human affairs.

One old way of describing this is how the catechism would talk about the watchmaker theory... people used to think that when God created the world, he left it alone for us to figure it out on our own.
It emphasizes complacency... this is a God who doesn't care about me. And so I don't really have to care about living a good life or being a moral person.
The strict enforcer emphasizes compliance. The harsh parent emphasizes conformity. The enabling friend, comfort. The distant puppeteer emphasizes complacency.
Maybe you know it's a distorted image if it elicits those kinds of death dealing life sucking emotions.
Why are these images distorted? I have two answers for that. The first is their lack of balance.
Here's the second reason why these images are distorted. Because they project human brokenness.
These images display the worst of humanity rather than the best that we can be.
People have used God as an instrument to inflame their egos, avoid their uncertainties and control those around them.
Be careful with how God is imaged in our minds and hearts. Because who you think is God might really just be your very strict Lola or your uncaring father, just projected as some sort of deity.
The images of God I showed you are just projections of broken people rather than the image of the living God.
In our last class we talked about God from an experiential angle, paying attention to our images of God, especially those formed in childhood.
The four distorted images of God are: the stern enforcer, the harsh parent, the enabling parent, and the distant puppeteer.
These images are distorted precisely because of their lack of balance. They emphasize something that you can truly say about God, but then they exaggerate it.
These images are also distorted because they tend to reflect our egos more than they reflect who God really is.
Having an image of God is helpful, as we are tangible beings. It becomes reductionistic if we reduce God to our images, and that image then becomes an idol—a God we create.

For example, you might cling to a childhood notion of a God who will never let anything bad happen. When tragedy strikes, since your youthful image of God is not reflected in reality, you may abandon the God of your youth. Or you may abandon God completely.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Otherwise, one remains stuck in a child-like view of God that prevents fully embracing a mature faith.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: When I was a boy, for instance, I used to think of God as the Great Problem Solver who would fix all my problems if I just prayed hard enough. Let me get an A on my social studies test. Let me do well in math. Better yet, let tomorrow be a snow day.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: As I grew older, the model of God as the Great Problem Solver collapsed-primarily because God didn't seem interested in solving all of my problems. I prayed and prayed and prayed, and all my problems still weren't solved. Why not? I wondered. Didn't God care about me? My adolescent narcissism led to some serious doubts, which led me to consider the possibility that God didn't exist.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Yet I was still stuck with a big question: if God wasn't the Great Problem Solver, the God of my youth, who was He? Or She? Or It?
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: An even greater danger for explorers is not settling on any one religious tradition because it doesn't suit them: God may become someone who is supposed to satisfy their needs.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: God becomes what one writer called a 'pocket-size God,' small enough to put in your pocket when God doesn't suit you (for example, when the Scriptures say things that you would rather not hear) and take out of your pocket only when convenient.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: So for several years God the Problem Solver was replaced by a more amorphous spiritual concept: God the Life Force, God the Other, God the Far-Away One. While these are valid images of God, I had no idea that God could be anything but those abstract ideas. And I figured that things would stay that way until I died.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Here's an example: one of my favorite images of God is the God of Surprises, which I first encountered in the novitiate. My own idea of God at the time was limited to God the Far-Away One, so it was liberating to hear about a God who surprises, who waits for us with wonderful things. It's a playful, even fun, image of God. But I would have never come up with it on my own. It came to me from David Donovan, my spiritual director, who had read it in a book of that same title, by an English Jesuit named Gerard W. Hughes, who borrowed it from an essay by the German Jesuit Karl Rahner.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: That image was amplified when I read the conclusion of one of the great modern spiritual novels, Mariette in Ecstasy. Ron Hansen, an award-winning writer who is also an ordained Catholic deacon, penned the story of the religious experiences of a young nun in the early 1900s, loosely based on the life of St. Thérèse of Lisieux, the French Carmelite. At the end of the story, Mariette, who had left the monastery many years before, writes to her former novice director and assures her that God still communicates with her.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: God and 'the gods'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin's point here is to drive a wedge between the false, so-called 'gods' of the Roman world and the authentic God whom Christians proclaim as Lord.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin on the Roman gods: 'we not only deny that they . . . are gods, but assert that they are wicked and impious demons' (First Apology, 5).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This point is made more explicit in another (and perhaps also second-century) text, sometimes ascribed to Justin himself but now considered more likely to be the work of a Justin-inspired disciple, Pseudo-Justin.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Pseudo-Justin, this 'true and invariable Name' (On the Sole Government of God, 6) is only correctly 'applicable to the only true God' (Government, 2). Any others given the name, including those whom the pagans call gods, receive it unworthily.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Hence referring to Greco-Roman mythology, he speaks dismissively of 'those who think that they shall share the holy and perfect Name, which some have received by a vain tradition as if they were gods' (Government, 5).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: A very similar idea, although expressed slightly differently, appears in the New Testament in 1 Corinthians 8.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In the course of giving practical advice on the eating of food sacrificed to idols, Paul also distinguishes between the 'so-called gods in heaven or on earth' and the 'one God' (8.5-6).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Paul seems to oscillate between implying that these so-called gods are simple fictions ('no idol in the world really exists') and affirming that though real enough in themselves, these 'many gods and many lords' certainly do not deserve to share the divine name.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: But regardless of whether they are demons or mere figments, Paul's basic point is clear: 'for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things are and through whom we exist' (1 Cor. 8.4-6).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Rather, he is anxious that the Corinthians be vigilant against lapsing, or inadvertently causing others to lapse, into idolatry - that is, into the offering of worship due to 'the Most True God' to those who are falsely so-called.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Recent converts, in particular, might be fooled into thinking that 'the gods', though falsely so-called, are actually deserving of the name: 'Since some have become so accustomed to idols until now, they still think of the food they eat as offered to an idol; and their conscience, being weak, is defiled' (8.7).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Hence, lest the practice of eating food sacrificed to idols, though in itself innocuous enough, become 'a stumbling-block to the weak', Paul advises against it. Better to go without cheap meat than to confuse others about the distinction between the misnamed 'gods' and the Most - and, for that matter, only - True one.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: As far as Pseudo-Justin is concerned, the word 'God' is not a description of something but is rather a proper name. For him, the classical gods and goddesses - Zeus, Hera, Hermes, Aphrodite and the rest - are imposters.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Now, as a description of the One worshipped and glorified by the ancient Israelites, and thus latterly the early Christians too, that is a fairly awful one.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Naturally, there is no danger whatsoever of anyone in their right mind mistaking this Poppy's metaphorical name for a literal description... Yet, as McCabe points out, and as Paul and Pseudo-Justin were all too aware, such is not the case with the Christian God.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: To think that God is simply a 'god', even if the biggest and best one of them all, is to commit idolatry.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For all of them, the worship of false 'gods' in whatever form - whether Olympian super-things, demons, fictions, or naive theological constructions - amounts to the same: idol worship.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The conceptions of God that atheists deny have, it is claimed, no bearing at all on the God in whom Christians actually believe: such 'so-called gods' are naive and idolatrous caricatures, and Christian thinkers have already made short (and rather more sophisticated) work of dismissing them.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: [I]n the sense in which atheists of this sort say God 'does not exist', that atheist has merely arrived at the theological starting-point. Theologians of the classical traditions, an Augustine, a Thomas Aquinas or a Meister Eckhart, simply agree about the disposing of idolatries, and then proceed with the proper business of doing theology. (Denys Turner)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 'atheism properly and effectively criticizes false and misleading images of God'. (Étienne Borne)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Note the remark by the saintly Prince Myshkin, the protagonist of Dostoevsky's 1868 novel The Idiot, that 'there's something in [true religious feeling] that atheisms eternally glance off, and they will eternally be talking not about that.' For Myshkin, atheists are condemned always to 'miss the point'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: It would be tedious to list the well-known gods of this exceptionally superstitious twentieth century. Quite apart from surviving old ones like astrology, there are a lot of new ones like racism, nationalism, the Market, the Leader . . . you name it. (Herbert McCabe)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Regarding the former tendency, let us accept that most unbelievers aren't especially well acquainted with the minutiae of the Christian doctrine of God. The same is unquestionably true of a great many pious, practising, faith-and-works Christian believers. If the typical discourse about God really is so woefully ill-informed, then is that not at least partly the fault of Christians themselves?
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 3) creative doubt, by continually challenging the full adequacy of religious symbols, serves as a check against the idolatrous worship of one's own religious language.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: At stake in taking religion seriously is nothing less than idolatry: the one who never doubts the adequacy of his symbols is worshiping his symbols.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: To worship symbols is to worship the pointers to God rather than God; and to worship something other than God is, by definition, idolatry.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Creative doubt, then, may serve as a check against idolatry itself.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf
