topics.review:
This topic, 'Distorted Images of God,' explores a fundamental challenge in the life of faith: the gap between our intellectual concepts of God and the powerful, experience-based images that truly shape our relationship with the Divine. The central argument, drawing from thinkers like Dr. Chris Pritchett, is that our primary way of knowing and relating to God is not through abstract, dictionary-like definitions but through deeply ingrained images. These 'inner images' are formed in powerful, often subconscious ways, beginning in childhood and colored by our most significant relationships and experiences. For example, our understanding of God as 'Father' is inevitably shaped by our personal experience with our own father, for better or worse. This creates a potential disconnect: we may conceptually believe in a 'loving Father,' but if our experiential image of a father is one of absence or harshness, that image will dominate our emotional and spiritual life. Who God *really is for us* is determined by these images, not the concepts we've been taught.

Because these images are so foundational, it is critical to examine them for distortions. The course identifies four common distorted images of God. The first two are 'negative' in that they shrink and negate human freedom and personhood. 
1.  **The Strict Enforcer**: This is an image of God as a cosmic rule-maker, primarily concerned with compliance. This God is perceived as eagerly waiting to punish any infraction of the rules. A relationship with this God becomes a burden, centered on fear and rule-following rather than love and freedom.
2.  **The Harsh Parent**: This image depicts God as setting an impossibly high bar for moral and spiritual perfection without offering any help to achieve it. This God's primary tool is shame, demanding conformity to a 'picture-perfect' ideal. This image fosters a sense of constant failure and unworthiness.

The next two images are 'positive' distortions, not because they are good, but because they over-emphasize human desires and diminish divine reality, ultimately leading to spiritual harm.
3.  **The Enabling Friend**: This is a 'feel-good' God who prioritizes our comfort above all else. This God sets no real standards and permits any behavior under the guise of love. This distortion confuses enabling destructive behavior with genuine care, which can lead to a spiritual 'sugar crash' when reality sets in and a lack of true guidance leads to ruin.
4.  **The Distant Puppeteer**: This image presents God as a cosmic controller who set the universe in motion but is now utterly uninvolved in human affairs. This is a modern take on the 'watchmaker theory.' This view fosters complacency, as it implies that our moral choices are irrelevant to a God who does not care about us personally.

Why are these images considered 'distorted'? The lecture provides two primary reasons. First, they suffer from a **lack of balance**. They take a single, potentially valid attribute of God—such as justice, holiness, love, or sovereignty—and exaggerate it to the exclusion of all other attributes. God's justice without mercy becomes the Strict Enforcer; God's love without a call to holiness becomes the Enabling Friend. The authentic Christian image of God holds these seemingly contradictory attributes in a creative paradox.

Second, and more profoundly, these images are distorted because they are often **projections of human brokenness**. Instead of reflecting the living God, they reflect our own fears, insecurities, and desires. They are 'pocket-size' gods we create to serve our own egos—to control others, to avoid uncertainty, or to justify our own complacency. The image of the Harsh Parent might simply be a projection of a critical grandparent (a strict *Lola*), not the God of the Gospels. In this sense, these images are a form of idolatry. As the readings from early Christian writers like Pseudo-Justin and the Apostle Paul in 1 Corinthians highlight, there is a critical distinction between the One True God and the 'so-called gods.' To mistake our human projections—our idols—for the true God is the core error of idolatry. We reduce God to something manageable, an object we can control, rather than relating to the true, uncontainable Mystery.

This leads to a crucial insight related to 'Christian Atheism,' a concept explored in another part of the course. Thinkers like Étienne Borne and Denys Turner argue that what many modern atheists reject is not the God of classical Christian theology, but precisely these distorted, idolatrous caricatures. The atheist who denies the existence of the Strict Enforcer or the Distant Puppeteer is, in a sense, at the 'theological starting-point.' A mature faith, therefore, involves a process of 'creative doubt'—a continuous challenging of our own images to ensure we are not worshipping the symbols themselves but the God to whom they point. This process can be painful, as seen in the personal testimony of outgrowing a childhood image of God as the 'Great Problem Solver.' When God doesn't fix every problem, this childish image shatters, forcing a crisis of faith that can either lead to abandoning God entirely or, ideally, discovering a more mature and resilient understanding.

Ultimately, the study of distorted images is not just an academic exercise but a spiritual one. It calls for an examination of conscience regarding who God truly is for us. By identifying and deconstructing the false gods of our own making—be they projections of our childhood wounds or reflections of our cultural idols like nationalism or 'the Market' (as Herbert McCabe notes)—we clear the space for a more authentic encounter. We move away from the shadowy, ego-driven images, as Plato's allegory of the cave would suggest, and open ourselves to the God of Revelation who is always greater than our images, a God of Surprises who cannot be contained in a pocket or reduced to a simple formula. This journey is essential for growing from a child-like faith into a mature, adult relationship with the Divine.
keyTakeaways: Our emotionally powerful, experience-based *images* of God (e.g., from our relationship with our parents) are more influential in our spiritual lives than our intellectually learned *concepts* of God.

There are four common distorted images of God: The Strict Enforcer (emphasizes compliance), The Harsh Parent (emphasizes conformity), The Enabling Friend (emphasizes comfort), and The Distant Puppeteer (emphasizes complacency).
Distortions occur for two main reasons: 1) a lack of balance, where one divine attribute is exaggerated to the exclusion of others, and 2) the projection of human brokenness, fears, and ego onto God.
These distorted images are a form of idolatry. They reduce God to a manageable 'pocket-size God' or an idol that we create and control, rather than worshipping the true, living God who is a mystery.

The process of maturing in faith often involves the shattering of childhood or distorted images (e.g., God as the 'Great Problem Solver'). This crisis can lead to either abandoning faith or embracing a more complex and authentic relationship with God.

Thinkers like Pseudo-Justin and St. Paul distinguish between the 'so-called gods' (which are demons, fictions, or idols) and the One True God. Worshipping these false images is the essence of idolatry.

The concept of 'Christian Atheism' suggests that rejecting these distorted, idolatrous images of God is a necessary and even spiritually healthy act, and is often what secular atheists are doing as well.

Creative doubt is a vital spiritual tool. By continually questioning the adequacy of our symbols and images for God, we prevent ourselves from worshipping the symbols themselves and remain open to authentic Divine Revelation.

glossary.definition: Inaccurate or incomplete perceptions of God that are formed by personal experiences, psychological projections, or an imbalanced understanding of divine attributes. These images hinder a mature and authentic relationship with the Divine.

A distorted image of God as a cosmic rule-maker who is primarily focused on strict compliance with rules and is eager to punish infractions. This image emphasizes fear and obedience over love and relationship.

A distorted image of God who sets an impossibly high standard of perfection but provides no assistance. This God's primary tool is shame, demanding conformity and fostering a sense of constant failure.

A distorted image of God who prioritizes human comfort above all, setting no real moral or spiritual standards and permitting any behavior. This image mistakes enablement for love and leads to spiritual complacency and immaturity.

A distorted image of God as a remote creator who, like a watchmaker, wound up the universe and now leaves it to run on its own without any personal involvement in human affairs. This view fosters complacency about moral and spiritual life.

The act of worshipping something other than God as if it were God. In this context, it refers to elevating one's own limited, distorted, or projected images of God to the status of the divine, worshipping the symbol or pointer rather than the ultimate reality.

A term describing an image of God that is made small and convenient enough to be controlled by the individual. This 'god' can be ignored when it makes uncomfortable demands and brought out only when it serves one's needs, representing a self-serving and idolatrous faith.

The psychological process of unconsciously attributing one's own brokenness, fears, desires, or negative traits onto God. This is a primary cause of distorted images, where the 'god' one relates to is a reflection of the human ego rather than the Divine reality.

A deistic analogy, referenced in connection with the 'Distant Puppeteer' image, that posits God created the universe with its laws (like a watchmaker building a watch) but then left it to operate on its own without further intervention.

A term used by St. Paul (1 Cor. 8) and early Christian writers like Pseudo-Justin to refer to idols, demons, or mythological figures that are falsely given the name and worship due to the one true God. This highlights the distinction between the true God and all forms of idolatrous imposters.

studyTips: Create a 2x2 grid to memorize the four distorted images. Label one axis 'Negative/Shrinking Human' and 'Positive/Over-Emphasizing Human.' Label the other axis with the corresponding images. This will help you classify them.

Remember the four key emphases: Strict Enforcer -> Compliance, Harsh Parent -> Conformity, Enabling Friend -> Comfort, Distant Puppeteer -> Complacency. The acronym 'CCCC' might help you recall these pairings.

Pay close attention to the distinction between an *image* of God (experiential, powerful, often unconscious) and a *concept* of God (intellectual, learned). This is a foundational distinction for the entire topic.

Be prepared to explain the two reasons for distortion: 1) Lack of Balance and 2) Projection of Human Brokenness. For an exam, have a specific example ready for each, like how exaggerating God's justice leads to the Strict Enforcer (balance) or how an uncaring father can lead to the Distant Puppeteer (projection).

Connect this topic to 'Christian Atheism.' The key insight is that Christian theology itself provides the tools to critique and reject these 'so-called gods' that atheists also reject. It's a critique of idolatry, not of the true God.

Don't just memorize the definitions. Try to identify which of these distorted images you may have encountered in your own life, in media, or in conversations. Personalizing the concepts will make them stick.

connections.relationship: This topic directly explains what 'Christian Atheism' is directed against. A Christian 'atheist' is one who rejects the distorted, idolatrous images of God (like the Strict Enforcer), which are often the very 'gods' that secular atheists also deny.
connections.example: When a person says, 'I can't believe in a God who sends people to hell for minor mistakes,' they are rejecting the Strict Enforcer image. A Christian 'atheist' would agree, stating that this idol is not the true God of Christianity.

connections.relationship: The distorted image of the 'Harsh Parent' is a direct theological root of spiritual perfectionism. It fosters a belief that one must achieve a flawless moral state to be worthy of God's love, leading to shame and anxiety.
connections.example: A student who believes they must pray perfectly, never have a doubting thought, and always feel 'holy' to please God is operating under the Harsh Parent image. This is a perfectionistic trap that this distorted image creates.

connections.relationship: Distorted images act as static or noise that blocks authentic Divine Revelation. Purifying our images of God by identifying and dismantling these distortions is a necessary prerequisite to being able to receive God's self-communication more clearly.
connections.example: If someone holds the 'Distant Puppeteer' image, they will be closed off to experiencing God's personal care and guidance in their life (a form of revelation), dismissing any such experience as coincidence.

connections.relationship: Creating a distorted image is an attempt to turn the 'Mystery' of God into a solvable 'Problem.' We reduce God to a manageable formula (like the 'Great Problem Solver' or 'Strict Enforcer') to gain a sense of control and avoid the discomfort of engaging with a reality that is beyond our full comprehension.
connections.example: Viewing God as 'The Great Problem Solver' is an attempt to treat God as a vending machine for solutions (a problem-solving tool), rather than engaging with the mystery of God's presence even amidst unsolved problems.

connections.relationship: A mature faith requires moving beyond childish or distorted images. The source material shows that faith can be shattered when a simplistic image (like God as a problem-solver) collides with the reality of suffering. A resilient faith is one that has wrestled with and moved beyond these fragile idols.
connections.example: The student who prayed for an A on a test and failed might lose their faith if it was based on God as a 'Great Problem Solver.' A more mature faith would be able to sustain belief in God's goodness even in the face of disappointment.

connections.relationship: Distorted images kill wonder. They present a God who is predictable, formulaic, and rigid. Moving beyond these images, as suggested by the counter-example of 'God of Surprises,' opens the soul to wonder, playfulness, and the unexpected ways God acts in the world.
connections.example: Someone stuck with the 'Strict Enforcer' image sees the world in black-and-white rules. Someone open to the 'God of Surprises' can see divine grace in an unexpected conversation or a beautiful sunset, fostering a sense of wonder.

examPrepQuestions.question: According to the course material, why is our 'image' of God considered more powerful than our 'concept' of God? Give an example.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Our 'image' of God is more powerful because it is formed through deep, personal experiences, especially in childhood, and operates on an emotional level. In contrast, a 'concept' is a more abstract, intellectual idea. For example, one can have the concept of 'God is a loving Father,' but if their experience of their own father was one of neglect, their inner, operative image of God will likely be of a distant or uncaring figure, and this image will dictate their actual feelings and relationship with God.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain the two primary reasons why an image of God becomes distorted. Provide one of the four distorted images as an example for one of these reasons.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: The two primary reasons are a lack of balance and the projection of human brokenness. A lack of balance occurs when one attribute of God is exaggerated to the exclusion of others. Projection happens when we attribute our own flaws or fears to God. For example, the 'Enabling Friend' image demonstrates a lack of balance by exaggerating God's love and comfort while completely ignoring divine holiness and justice.

examPrepQuestions.question: What is idolatry in the context of this topic, and how does it relate to the 'pocket-size God'?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: In this context, idolatry is the worship of our own limited and distorted images of God rather than the true, living God. It is mistaking the pointer for the reality it points to. This relates directly to the 'pocket-size God,' which is an idol created for our own convenience—a God we can control, put away when demanding, and take out when useful. This act of reducing God to a manageable, self-serving object is a form of idolatry.

quizQuestions.question: Which distorted image of God is most closely associated with the 'watchmaker theory' and leads to an attitude of complacency?
choices: A) The Strict Enforcer
B) The Harsh Parent
C) The Distant Puppeteer
D) The Enabling Friend
quizQuestions.explanation: The Distant Puppeteer is the image of a God who created the universe but is no longer involved, which is the core idea of the watchmaker analogy. This belief fosters complacency because if God doesn't care about human affairs, there is no divine incentive to live a moral life.

quizQuestions.question: According to Dr. Chris Pritchett, our truest, most operative belief about who God is for us is based on our:
choices: A) Concepts learned in catechism
B) Images formed through experience
C) Logical proofs for God's existence
D) The official doctrines of the Church
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture material explicitly states that, according to Pritchett, who God really is for you is based on your images of God, which are formed through life experiences, not on the abstract concepts you have been taught.

quizQuestions.question: The distorted images of the 'Strict Enforcer' and 'Harsh Parent' are described as negative distortions because they:
choices: A) Over-emphasize human comfort
B) Deny the existence of God
C) Shrink and limit the human person
D) Are based on ancient mythologies
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture material categorizes the Strict Enforcer and Harsh Parent as negative distortions because they impose fear, shame, and rigid rules that negate human freedom and shrink one's capacity for a full life, thus limiting the human person.

quizQuestions.question: A person who believes God permits any action because 'love means accepting everything' is likely operating with which distorted image?
choices: A) The Enabling Friend
B) The Distant Puppeteer
C) The Harsh Parent
D) The God of Surprises
quizQuestions.explanation: The Enabling Friend is the image of God that emphasizes comfort and permissiveness to a fault, confusing enablement with love. This God sets no real standards and is seen as permitting everything.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary danger of reducing God to an image, such as 'The Great Problem Solver'?
choices: A) It makes God too transcendent and unknowable.
B) It leads to the creation of an idol that can be easily shattered by life experiences.
C) It focuses too much on God's mercy at the expense of justice.
D) It requires too much intellectual effort from the believer.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source material explains that when we reduce God to a simplistic image like a 'Problem Solver,' that image becomes an idol. When reality doesn't match the image (i.e., God doesn't solve all our problems), the idol shatters, potentially leading to a collapse of faith.

quizQuestions.question: The lecture identifies 'lack of balance' as a primary cause of distortion. This means:
choices: A) The image is not based on scripture.
B) The image is a projection of a person's father or mother.
C) The image exaggerates one of God's attributes while ignoring others.
D) The image is not popular within the Christian tradition.
quizQuestions.explanation: Lack of balance refers to taking one true attribute of God (like justice, love, or power) and inflating it to the point that it eclipses all other complementary attributes, creating a distorted caricature.

quizQuestions.question: According to the readings from Paul and Pseudo-Justin, what is the fundamental error in worshipping 'so-called gods'?
choices: A) They are less powerful than the one true God.
B) It is an outdated practice in the modern world.
C) It is a form of idolatry, offering worship due only to the true God to imposters.
D) Their names are difficult to pronounce.
quizQuestions.explanation: Paul and Pseudo-Justin's main point is that these 'so-called gods' (whether demons or fictions) are unworthy of the name 'god' and that offering them worship is idolatry—the misdirection of worship away from the One True God.

quizQuestions.question: The image of the 'Harsh Parent' primarily uses which tool to enforce conformity?
choices: A) Love
B) Shame
C) Miracles
D) Indifference
quizQuestions.explanation: The handwritten notes and lecture transcript specify that the Harsh Parent demands conformity to a perfect image and that its primary tool for enforcement is SHAME.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is presented as a healthy, non-distorted image of God that serves as a positive contrast in the readings?
choices: A) The God of Surprises
B) The Great Problem Solver
C) The Life Force
D) God the Other
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'God of Surprises,' encountered through the writings of Karl Rahner and others, is presented as a liberating, playful, and expansive image that contrasts with the limited and rigid distorted images.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of 'Christian Atheism' as discussed in the readings suggests that:
choices: A) It is acceptable for a Christian to not believe in God.
B) Atheists are correct in their denial of God.
C) The 'gods' that atheists often reject are the same idolatrous caricatures that good theology also rejects.
D) Christianity and atheism will eventually merge into one belief system.
quizQuestions.explanation: The readings from thinkers like Borne and Turner argue that atheism's critique is often aimed at false, distorted images of God. In this sense, a 'Christian atheist' is one who participates in this rejection of idols, which is seen as a necessary step towards authentic faith, not a denial of the true God.

topics.title: Christian "Atheism"

glossaryTerms.definition: A theological position, articulated by figures like St. Justin Martyr, that professes 'atheism' only in the sense of rejecting false, pagan, or idolatrous conceptions of God as manipulable 'beings', while still affirming faith in the 'Most True God'.
The Greek term for 'atheists' or 'godless ones'. It was used as a term of abuse by the pagan Roman crowds against early Christians who refused to worship Roman gods.
The state of intellectually believing in God ('up here') but not feeling or experiencing God's presence in one's life. It is characterized by a feeling of divine abandonment or forsakenness.

A concept from Christian spirituality (associated with St. John of the Cross) describing a period of spiritual desolation and emptiness, where a believer feels abandoned by God. It is often considered a stage of spiritual purification and growth.

Deities, such as those of the Greco-Roman pantheon (e.g., Zeus, Poseidon), characterized in the lecture notes as being egotistical, temperamental, tied to nature, and requiring appeasement, functioning more like 'super-objects' than a transcendent creator.
A term for 20th-century 'death of God' theologies. The source material explicitly distinguishes this from the main topic of Christian 'atheism'.
A term used to describe nonbelievers (atheists or agnostics) who live exceptionally selfless and moral lives, such as the aid workers mentioned in the lecture notes.

A concept from Dostoevsky's novel 'Demons', where the character Bishop Tikhon describes a person who has rejected every single false god and idol. This person is said to be on the 'next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith'.
A person who actively rejects idols or false conceptions of God. The source material suggests that both a good Christian and a critical atheist can function as an 'anti-idolater par excellence'.
A term used to describe the pagan gods, implying they are merely powerful things or objects within the universe that can be controlled and manipulated, rather than being a transcendent creator.

entries.content: There are some people who can reasonably say that there is no God and live their lives as if there is no God. You have a lot of reasonable atheists.
You have people who profess to have faith but then act in an atheistic sort of manner.
A key concept related to a feeling of abandonment by God is 'The dark knight of the soul.'
Experiential atheism is that feeling of abandonment and God's forsakenness. It's when you believe in God intellectually ('up here'), but you don't experience God or see God in your experience.
And yet somehow that experience of God forsakenness didn't deter their faith. It might have even deepened their strength.
The heart of the reading by Bultmann is that Christianity is actually a form of atheism—not atheism as we understand it, but a denial of the pagan gods.
The pagan gods of Greece and Rome were often associated with things that occur in the world, like Zeus (thunder), Poseidon (the sea), and Dionysus (wine).
These pagan gods were characterized as being egotistical, temperamental, human-like, and requiring appeasement.
The ego of the pagan gods is the way that the Greeks and the Romans would try to explain or express the instability of nature.
In pagan understanding, you must appease a god with an offering to manipulate them into doing what you want. This implies the worshipper is actually more powerful than the god.
The heart of Christian atheism is this whole idea that when Christians talk about God, God is not bound by nature. God is not bound by ego. Why? Because God is not a being.
Atheists are right in saying there is no evidence of God, if 'God' is understood as just one more existing thing (a being) in the universe.
The problem with the Greek gods is that they really are just things or objects—'super-objects'—that can be controlled and manipulated. They are 'beings'.
Christian atheism can be described as a kind of spiritual emptiness or barrenness: 'I'm doing all of these things for God, but how come I don't feel God?'
Feeling God's absence can sometimes be a period of spiritual growth, or it may mean we have to widen our expectations about how God communicates with us.
Often they seek proof for God's existence, and finding none, or encountering intense suffering, they reject the theistic worldview completely.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Likewise, sometimes the most selfless people in our world are atheists or agnostics. Some of the hardest working aid workers I met in my time working with refugees in East Africa were nonbelievers. The “secular saint” is real.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: One of my college friends practices his atheism religiously; his questions have kept me on my toes for the last thirty years. Try telling him about "God's will," and you will find yourself on the receiving end of a pointed lecture on personal responsibility.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: They coalesced when my freshman-year roommate was killed in an automobile accident during our senior year. Brad was one of my closest friends, and his death was almost too much to bear. At Brad's funeral, on a humid spring day in a wealthy suburb outside of Washington, DC, I sat in a tasteful Episcopal church, surrounded by Brad's shattered family and my grieving friends, and thought about the absurdity of believing in a God who would allow this. By the end of the service I had decided not to believe in a God who would act so cruelly. The Great Problem Solver wasn't solving problems but creating them.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: My newfound atheism was invigorating. Not only did I feel like a person with a first-rate intellect, I was proud to have rejected something that obviously had not worked. Why believe in a God who either couldn't or wouldn't prevent suffering? Atheism was not only intellectually respectable but also had some practical benefits: I now had my Sunday mornings free.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: By the way, atheism doesn't have a perfect record either. In his book No One Sees God: The Dark Night of Atheists and Believers, the writer Michael Novak points out that while many atheist thinkers urge us to question everything, especially the record of organized religion, atheists often fail to question their own record. Think of the cruelty and bloodshed perpetrated, just in the twentieth century, by totalitarian regimes that have professed 'scientific atheism.' Stalinist Russia comes to mind.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Stephen Bullivant
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The 'atheism' of Christianity
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Picture the scene: it's festival day in a provincial Roman city in the mid-second century AD, and people have come from far and wide to witness a group of local Christians being put to death.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: A young man called Germanicus stands in the arena, not just bravely facing savage beasts but actively urging them on.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Irritated by the youth's composure, the crowd who have gathered to see him torn apart cry out: 'Down with the atheists!'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Slightly later, the venerable bishop Polycarp is brought into the arena, and is ordered - on pain of death - to himself denounce his fellow Christians in the same way.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Whereupon, we are told: 'Polycarp's brow darkened as he threw a look round the turbulent crowd of heathens in the circus; and then, indicating them with a sweep of his hand, he said with a growl and a glance to heaven: "Down with the atheists!"' (Martyrdom of Polycarp, 9).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: [Footnote 1] The translation here (slightly amended) is taken from Maxwell Staniforth (trans.), 1968, Early Christian Writings, Harmondsworth: Penguin, pp. 153-67.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: St Polycarp, whom ancient tradition assures was a disciple of St John the Evangelist, was bishop of Smyrna (modern-day Izmir on the west coast of Turkey) for much of the first half of the second century.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This gripping, eyewitness account of his and his companions' martyrdom around the year 156 is the earliest such report we have, and hence offers invaluable insights into ancient Christian life, thought and practice.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: FAITH AND UNBELIEF
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For our purposes, the word translated as 'atheists' here is our old friend, atheoi.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Given the context, its meaning here is rather more transparent than it was in Ephesians. In effect, we have the Christian Polycarp and the pagan crowd mutually denouncing each other as 'infidels'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: As far as the crowd is concerned, the Christians deny and dishonour the real Roman gods, while affirming their own false one.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: At one point, the arena crowd cry out that Polycarp is a 'destroyer of our gods, who is teaching whole multitudes to abstain from sacrificing to them or worshipping them' - Martyrdom, 12.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: But as far as Polycarp is concerned, the crowd do precisely the opposite.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: On one level, this is just an instance of a broad phenomenon noted in the Introduction, of 'atheist' being used throughout much of history as a term of abuse: it is always one's enemies who are the 'atheists' (and the feeling is often mutual).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: However, in the writings of St Justin Martyr, one of Polycarp's contemporaries, the same basic idea receives a somewhat different twist.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin had himself been brought up a pagan, but in later life, having worked his way through a succession of different philosophies - Stoicism, Pythagoreanism, Aristotelianism and Platonism - he finally settled on Christianity as the 'true Philosophy'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: [Footnote 2] This, and all other quotations from Justin's and Pseudo-Justin's (see below) writings, may be found in Alexander Roberts and James Donaldson (eds), 1996, The Ante-Nicene Fathers, Volume I: The Apostolic Fathers Justin Martyr Irenaeus, Edinburgh: T & T Clark. These and a huge number of other translated texts from the early Church Fathers can also be found at: www.newadvent.org/fathers/.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Roughly a decade after Polycarp met his fate in Smyrna, Justin was himself martyred.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In his apologias, Justin attempted to explain what and why Christians believed for his pagan contemporaries, and to correct common misunderstandings about them. The charge of 'atheism' was one of these.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin, however, novelly chooses to side with both Polycarp and the crowd, declaring in his First Apology: 'we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort [i.e. the Greco-Roman ones] are concerned, but not with respect to the Most True God' (First Apology, 6).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Justin then, there is at least one, albeit qualified, sense in which Christians are, and must be, atheists.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin is far from alone within the tradition in identifying forms of genuinely Christian 'atheism', by which I mean perfectly orthodox forms and varieties of 'atheism' within Christianity.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: These forms of 'Christian atheism' should not be confused with what one might call forms of 'Christian' atheism, such as the twentieth-century 'death of God' theologies.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: After all, it is rather the point of Christianity that its dead God has a habit of not staying so for very long.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: While the rest of this book will concern itself with the theology of what one might call atheism proper (i.e. atheism as the absence of belief in a God or gods, understood in the normal way, and hence unadorned by quotation marks), it will be a fruitful and hopefully interesting prelude to consider instead the 'atheism' of proper theology.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: We will find ourselves in good company: Paul, Justin Martyr, Augustine, Meister Eckhart, Herbert McCabe, John of the Cross and Mother Teresa will all be joining us on our adventure.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The first move for us to make here is to consider more deeply Justin's above quoted remark: 'we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort are concerned, but not with respect to the Most True God'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Paul, as for Justin, Christians must therefore be atheists regarding 'the gods'. And nor is this merely an academic, highfalutinly theological point for him.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: If so, then Christians 'proclaim ourselves atheists' in a still deeper sense than Justin himself envisages.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: On this reading, Eckhart's utterance is radical enough to be fully orthodox, and a further instance of the kind of theological 'atheism' we have divined in the writings of Paul, Justin, Pseudo-Justin and McCabe.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The worship of [the] Creator is the only worship worthy of a human being... the human being is now defined, if you like, as the Creator-worshipper, the atheist with no gods to worship, no gods to petition, no gods to pray to, no gods worth praying to. (Herbert McCabe, God Still Matters)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 'Only an atheist can be a good Christian'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The Marxist philosopher Ernst Bloch, in his rich and remarkable 1972 book Atheism in Christianity, famously observed: 'Only an atheist can be a good Christian; only a Christian can be a good atheist.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: More recently, the Slovenian intellectual Slavoj Žižek writes: 'not only is Christianity (at its core, if disavowed by its institutional practice) the only truly consistent atheism, it is also that atheists are the only true believers.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Bloch and Žižek are both atheists by our definition, at least in the 'negative' sense of being without a belief in the existence of a God or gods, and the points they are making here are largely political and ethical.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In the theological literature on atheism, it is possible to identify two main tendencies along these kinds of line. The first is quite negative, and dismisses atheistic ideas and arguments as being, at best, obvious and platitudinous, and at worst, wholly beside the point.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The second tendency, meanwhile, takes the same basic point, but gives it a rather more positive spin. On this view, the atheist is cast as the anti-idolater par excellence, rejecting all (mis)conceptions of both God and the gods wholesale, and being, at least for the most part, right to do so.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: And if this is the case, then aren't atheists on to something very profound - and authentically Christian - after all?
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Evidence of both these two tendencies occurs in the writings of the Russian novelist Fyodor Dostoevsky.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: However, compare the following from a slightly later novel, 1872's Demons. Here the Orthodox bishop Tikhon tells the unbeliever Stavrogin: 'A complete atheist stands on the next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Tikhon, the 'complete atheist' is one who has rejected every single false 'god', including everything and anything that takes, or tries to take, the rightful place of the true God. Such a purified position is placed higher than all 'incomplete' forms of faith.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Both tendencies arguably tell us far more about Christian theology than they do about actual atheists.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: While there may well be a certain correlation between atheism and negative theology, it is surely rather a stretch to identify the two too closely. For they, atheists sans inverted commas, are without a belief in the existence, not only of gods but of the Most True God as well.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Conclusion
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The purpose of this opening chapter was intentionally provocative: to show the ways in which 'atheism' - although not atheism in the normal sense - sits close to the heart of classical Christian theology and spirituality.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: To a certain degree, this helps us to see some of the points of constructive contact between faith and unbelief. For example, atheists' critiques can indeed aid us in purifying our ever-faulty notions of 'the God who is not a god'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: But it should also help us to guard against naive claims as to the alleged affinities between genuine atheism and authentic Christianity.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Eventually it is understood that Emmanuel, upon whom the villagers depend so thoroughly, no longer believes certain religious doctrines that have provided for his parishioners such peace and happiness.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Atheism Redding
"We are atheists but not with respect to the Most True God"
The atheist is described as having no gods to worship or any gods worth praying to.
"Only an atheist can be a good Christian, Only a *good* Christian can be a good atheist."
Atheism in Christianity
"God does not exist" is the theological starting point.
"Atheists are the only true believers"
Atheists ironically do good theology.
They reject misconceptions about God.
Atheists are on the 2nd-to-last step before true Theology.
Atheists are right in saying that God does not exist.
...because there is no evidence of God.

topics.review:
Kumusta! Let's break down this complex but fascinating topic of Christian "Atheism" for your Theology 11 final exam. Don't be intimidated by the name; it's a key concept for understanding how Christianity defines God, especially within SET3 on Images and Revelation.

The central and most provocative idea is that classical Christianity contains its own form of "atheism." This isn't atheism as we normally understand it (the rejection of God's existence), but rather a profound and necessary rejection of false gods, idols, and distorted images of God. The course material introduces this through the famous, paradoxical statement by Marxist philosopher Ernst Bloch: "Only an atheist can be a good Christian; only a Christian can be a good atheist." This sets the stage for our entire discussion.

To understand this, we must go back to the early days of Christianity. The reading from Stephen Bullivant provides a dramatic historical anchor. In the mid-second century Roman Empire, early Christians like Germanicus and the bishop Polycarp were publicly executed. The Roman crowd, watching them die, shouted, "Down with the atheists!" The irony is thick. Why would they call Christians atheists? Because Christians refused to worship the official Roman gods. They were seen as 'atheoi'—without gods. In a powerful moment, when asked to denounce his own, Polycarp gestures to the pagan crowd and retorts, "Down with the atheists!" Both sides see the other as godless infidels.

This is where St. Justin Martyr, a contemporary of Polycarp, provides the crucial theological key. Instead of simply rejecting the label, he qualifies it. He states, "we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort [the Greco-Roman ones] are concerned, but not with respect to the Most True God." This is the heart of Christian "atheism." It is a declared atheism *towards a certain kind of god*. The pagan gods of Greece and Rome were characterized as being like super-powered, egotistical humans. They were tied to natural phenomena (Zeus with thunder, Poseidon with the sea), were temperamental, and needed to be appeased with sacrifices. They were essentially powerful "beings" or "super-objects" within the world that could, in theory, be manipulated. The Christian argument, as articulated by thinkers like Bultmann and Herbert McCabe, is that the True God is fundamentally different. God is not bound by nature or ego because, as we learn in the related topic, "God is not a being." God isn't just one more thing, even the biggest thing, in the universe. To be a Christian, therefore, requires being an "atheist" to any god that is merely a controllable object or a projection of human ego.

This theological concept has a powerful modern application. The lecture notes point out that many reasonable atheists are right to reject the God they've been presented with. When someone, like the student in the anecdote whose friend Brad died, rejects a God who seems like a cruel or incompetent "Great Problem Solver," they are rejecting an idol. They are rejecting a distorted image of God. In this sense, as the Russian novelist Dostoevsky suggests through his character Bishop Tikhon, a "complete atheist stands on the next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith." Such an atheist has cleared the ground of all false gods, creating a space where a truer understanding might emerge. Atheists' critiques, by pointing out the lack of evidence for God-as-a-being, ironically perform a theological service by forcing believers to purify their concept of God.

Beyond this intellectual or theological "atheism," the course also explores "experiential atheism." This is not a rejection of God, but a feeling of God's absence or abandonment, even while holding an intellectual belief. It's the cry of the believer: "I'm doing all of these things for God, but how come I don't feel God?" This experience is powerfully captured by the classic spiritual concept of the "dark night of the soul," famously associated with mystics like St. John of the Cross and experienced even by modern saints like Mother Teresa. This spiritual emptiness or barrenness is not necessarily a sign of failing faith. In fact, it can be a period of profound spiritual growth, forcing the believer to move beyond a faith dependent on feelings and to widen their expectations of how God communicates and is present in the world.

In summary, the topic of Christian "Atheism" operates on two levels. Theologically, it is the essential Christian act of rejecting idols and any conception of God as a mere "being" among beings, as articulated by Justin Martyr. Experientially, it manifests as the "dark night of the soul," where the felt absence of God challenges a believer to mature beyond a faith based on emotion. Both forms push the individual away from distorted images of God and, paradoxically, can lead to a more authentic encounter with the "Most True God" who is always beyond our grasp and control. This directly serves the goal of SET3, which is to purify our images of God in light of Revelation.
keyTakeaways: The term 'Christian "Atheism"' does not mean Christians who don't believe in God. It refers to the Christian rejection of false, pagan-style gods or idols.
Early Christians like St. Polycarp were called 'atheoi' (atheists) by the Romans because they refused to worship the Roman pantheon.
St. Justin Martyr clarified this by stating Christians are "atheists" concerning the manipulative, human-like pagan gods, but not towards the "Most True God."
The core distinction is that pagan gods are viewed as powerful "beings" or "super-objects" within the world, whereas the Christian God is not a being in that sense.
The critiques of modern atheists can be theologically useful, as they often attack distorted or idolatrous images of God (e.g., God as a 'Great Problem Solver'), thus helping to purify faith.
According to Dostoevsky's character Bishop Tikhon, a "complete atheist" who has rejected all false gods is on the "next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith."
"Experiential atheism" is the feeling of God's absence or abandonment despite intellectual belief, a state also known as the "dark night of the soul."
This experience of God's absence, while painful, can be a period of spiritual growth, leading to a more mature faith that isn't dependent on feelings.

glossary.definition: A theological position, articulated by figures like St. Justin Martyr, that professes 'atheism' only in the sense of rejecting false, pagan, or idolatrous conceptions of God as manipulable 'beings', while still affirming faith in the 'Most True God'.
The Greek term for 'atheists' or 'godless ones'. It was used as a term of abuse by the pagan Roman crowds against early Christians who refused to worship Roman gods.
The state of intellectually believing in God ('up here') but not feeling or experiencing God's presence in one's life. It is characterized by a feeling of divine abandonment or forsakenness.

A concept from Christian spirituality (associated with St. John of the Cross) describing a period of spiritual desolation and emptiness, where a believer feels abandoned by God. It is often considered a stage of spiritual purification and growth.

Deities, such as those of the Greco-Roman pantheon (e.g., Zeus, Poseidon), characterized in the lecture notes as being egotistical, temperamental, tied to nature, and requiring appeasement, functioning more like 'super-objects' than a transcendent creator.
A term for 20th-century 'death of God' theologies. The source material explicitly distinguishes this from the main topic of Christian 'atheism'.
A term used to describe nonbelievers (atheists or agnostics) who live exceptionally selfless and moral lives, such as the aid workers mentioned in the lecture notes.

A concept from Dostoevsky's novel 'Demons', where the character Bishop Tikhon describes a person who has rejected every single false god and idol. This person is said to be on the 'next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith'.
A person who actively rejects idols or false conceptions of God. The source material suggests that both a good Christian and a critical atheist can function as an 'anti-idolater par excellence'.
A term used to describe the pagan gods, implying they are merely powerful things or objects within the universe that can be controlled and manipulated, rather than being a transcendent creator.

studyTips: Pay close attention to the quotation marks. Create a two-column chart to contrast 'atheism' (the rejection of God's existence) with "atheism" (the Christian rejection of idols).
Trace the historical argument. Write a short paragraph explaining how the ideas of Polycarp and Justin Martyr are connected. Polycarp lived the paradox, and Justin Martyr explained it.

Connect the intellectual and the experiential. Draw a line between the theological concept of Christian "Atheism" (rejecting God-as-an-object) and the feeling of 'experiential atheism' (when God feels absent, like an object you can't find).

Use the anecdotes as memory hooks. The story of the friend 'Brad' illustrates rejecting God as a 'Problem Solver'. The 'secular saint' aid workers show that morality isn't exclusive to believers. The 'religiously' practicing atheist friend shows the intellectual rigor of atheism.

Rephrase the paradoxes in your own words. Try to explain "Only an atheist can be a good Christian" and "A complete atheist stands on the next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith" to a friend. This will test your understanding.
Create a concept map linking Christian "Atheism" to its related ideas: 'God is Not a Being', 'Pagan Gods', 'Idolatry', and 'Dark Night of the Soul'.

connections.relationship: This is the foundational concept that makes Christian "Atheism" possible. Christian "Atheism" is the rejection of God *as a being* in the way pagan gods were. The Christian God transcends the category of 'being' altogether.
connections.example: An atheist is right to say there's no evidence for a 'God' who is just one more existing thing in the universe. Christian theology agrees, stating God isn't a 'thing' or 'being' at all, but the ground of all being.

connections.relationship: Christian "Atheism" is the active process of identifying and rejecting distorted images of God. Atheists who reject God are often rejecting these very distortions.
connections.example: The image of God as a 'Great Problem Solver' who failed to prevent a tragedy (like the death of Brad) is a distorted image. Rejecting this image is a form of Christian "Atheism," clearing the way for a more mature concept of God who is not a cosmic vending machine.

connections.relationship: The experience of 'experiential atheism' can be triggered by a perfectionist approach to faith, where one demands certain feelings or tangible results as 'proof' of their connection to God.
connections.example: A student who prays, goes to church, and does service work might think, 'I'm doing all the right things, so I should feel God's presence.' When they feel spiritual barrenness instead, it challenges their perfectionist framework and can lead to a 'dark night of the soul'.

connections.relationship: Experiential atheism and the 'dark night of the soul' are profound tests of faith. They force faith to move beyond intellectual agreement or emotional high into a deeper trust in God's presence even when it is not felt.
connections.example: Mother Teresa continued her work for decades while feeling a profound absence of God. Her faith was not based on feeling but on a committed choice and trust, which was deepened, not destroyed, by her experience of God's forsakenness.

connections.relationship: Atheism often arises when God is framed as the answer to a 'Problem' (like suffering) and the answer fails. Christian faith reframes God's relationship to suffering as a 'Mystery' to be entered, not a problem to be solved. Rejecting the 'God-as-Problem-Solver' is an act of Christian "Atheism".
connections.example: The death of a friend is a 'Problem' that a problem-solving God should have prevented. When He doesn't, atheism is a logical response. Reframing this as a 'Mystery' means accepting that God's ways are not our ways, moving beyond the simplistic, distorted image.

connections.relationship: The 'dark night of the soul' forces a person to widen their understanding of Revelation. If God is not revealing Himself through feelings of consolation or direct intervention, one must look for Him in other places—in silence, in community, in the face of the poor, or in the continuation of one's commitments.
connections.example: A person feeling abandoned by God might stop looking for an emotional 'zap' in prayer and start seeing God's revelation in the steadfast love of their family or the resilience of nature.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain St. Justin Martyr's statement, 'we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort are concerned, but not with respect to the Most True God.' What is the significance of this statement for Christian theology?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: St. Justin Martyr's statement is a defense against the Roman charge that Christians were 'atheoi' (atheists). Instead of denying it, he re-contextualizes it. The 'gods of this sort' he refers to are the pagan gods of Rome—deities who were seen as temperamental, human-like 'super-objects' tied to nature that could be appeased and manipulated. Justin is saying that yes, Christians are 'atheists' towards *these kinds* of gods. They reject idolatry. However, they are not atheists with respect to the 'Most True God,' who is transcendent, not a being among other beings, and cannot be manipulated. The significance is that it defines Christianity through a form of anti-idolatry. It establishes early on that the Christian God is of a completely different order than the pagan gods, laying the groundwork for the theological concept that 'God is not a being'.

examPrepQuestions.question: Differentiate between 'Christian "Atheism"', 'experiential atheism', and atheism proper, using examples from the course materials.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Atheism proper is the straightforward belief that there is no God or gods. The student who, after his friend Brad's death, 'decided not to believe in a God who would act so cruelly' is an example of someone embracing atheism proper. 'Christian "Atheism"' is a theological concept, not a lack of belief. It is the necessary rejection of false idols and distorted images of God, like the pagan gods who are just 'super-objects'. St. Justin Martyr exemplifies this by claiming to be an 'atheist' only to those false gods. 'Experiential atheism' is a spiritual state experienced by a believer. It's the feeling of God's absence despite intellectual faith, as when one says, 'I'm doing all these things for God, but how come I don't feel God?'. This is also known as the 'dark night of the soul,' experienced by figures like Mother Teresa.

examPrepQuestions.question: According to the course notes, how can a modern atheist be described as being 'on the next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith'?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: This idea, from Dostoevsky's character Bishop Tikhon, suggests that a 'complete atheist' performs a crucial theological task: iconoclasm, or the smashing of idols. Many atheists reject God not out of malice, but because the image of God they are presented with is intellectually or morally wanting—for example, a 'Great Problem Solver' who allows immense suffering. By rejecting this flawed, simplistic, or idolatrous concept of God, the atheist has, in a sense, cleared the ground of false notions. They have rejected everything that is *not* the true God. From this purified, empty position—devoid of false gods—they are only one step away from a potential encounter with the 'Most True God' who transcends all those rejected images. Their atheism is a purer state than an 'incomplete' faith that worships a distorted image.

quizQuestions.question: Why were early Christians like St. Polycarp called 'atheoi' by the Roman crowds?
choices: A) Because they refused to worship the Roman emperor and pagan gods.
B) Because they taught that God was an impersonal force.
C) Because their leader, Jesus, had been executed.
D) Because they met in secret and were suspected of dark rituals.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source material explicitly states the crowd called them 'atheists' because they were seen as a 'destroyer of our gods, who is teaching whole multitudes to abstain from sacrificing to them or worshipping them'.

quizQuestions.question: What is the key characteristic of the 'pagan gods' that Christian "Atheism" rejects?
choices: A) They are all-powerful and all-knowing.
B) They are merciful and loving.
C) They are egotistical, manipulable 'beings' or 'super-objects'.
D) They are abstract philosophical concepts.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes describe the pagan gods as 'egotistical, temperamental, human-like,' and as 'super-objects' that can be controlled. This is the concept of God that Christianity rejects.

quizQuestions.question: The feeling of abandonment by God, even while intellectually believing, is called:
choices: A) Christian 'Atheism'
B) Experiential atheism
C) Scientific atheism
D) Paganism
quizQuestions.explanation: The glossary and lecture notes define experiential atheism as 'that feeling of abandonment and God's forsakenness... when you believe in God intellectually ('up here'), but you don't experience God.'

quizQuestions.question: According to the notes, the 'dark night of the soul' can lead to:
choices: A) A definite loss of faith.
B) A deepening of faith and spiritual strength.
C) A desire to appease the gods with offerings.
D) A belief in scientific atheism.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes state, 'And yet somehow that experience of God forsakenness didn't deter their faith. It might have even deepened their strength.'

quizQuestions.question: Who famously said, 'Only an atheist can be a good Christian; only a Christian can be a good atheist'?
choices: A) St. Justin Martyr
B) Stephen Bullivant
C) Fyodor Dostoevsky
D) Ernst Bloch
quizQuestions.explanation: The reading attributes this famous observation to the Marxist philosopher Ernst Bloch in his book 'Atheism in Christianity'.

quizQuestions.question: According to Bishop Tikhon in Dostoevsky's novel, who 'stands on the next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith'?
choices: A) The devout monk
B) The complete atheist
C) The philosopher
D) The suffering servant
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes quote Tikhon telling the unbeliever Stavrogin, 'A complete atheist stands on the next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith,' because they have rejected all false gods.

quizQuestions.question: The core theological idea that underpins Christian "Atheism" is that:
choices: A) God created the universe and then left it alone.
B) God is not a being or an object within the universe.
C) God's existence can be proven through logic.
D) Jesus was a great moral teacher but not divine.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes state, 'The heart of Christian atheism is this whole idea that when Christians talk about God, God is not bound by nature. God is not bound by ego. Why? Because God is not a being.'

quizQuestions.question: In the anecdote about the student whose friend Brad was killed, his newfound atheism was a rejection of what image of God?
choices: A) God as a distant, uncaring creator.
B) God as an impersonal force of nature.
C) God as the 'Great Problem Solver'.
D) God as a mystery.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text explicitly says, 'The Great Problem Solver wasn't solving problems but creating them,' showing this was the specific, distorted image of God he rejected.

quizQuestions.question: St. Justin Martyr's approach to the charge of 'atheism' was to:
choices: A) Completely deny it and condemn the accusers.
B) Ignore it as a baseless insult.
C) Agree with it in a qualified sense, distinguishing between false gods and the True God.
D) Demand a public debate with pagan philosophers.
quizQuestions.explanation: Justin's novel approach was to 'side with both Polycarp and the crowd,' by confessing to be an atheist 'so far as gods of this sort are concerned'.

quizQuestions.question: The phrase 'destroyer of our gods' was used by the Roman crowd to describe whom?
choices: A) St. Justin Martyr
B) Zeus
C) St. Polycarp
D) Ernst Bloch
quizQuestions.explanation: The reading from the 'Martyrdom of Polycarp' quotes the arena crowd crying out that Polycarp is a 'destroyer of our gods'.

topics.title: God Is Not a Being

glossaryTerms.definition: A Latin term for an existing thing or object that occupies time and space. It refers to any specific entity within the universe, from a person to a planet. In this topic, it's what God is NOT.
A Latin term for the very act of 'to be.' It is understood as a verb, representing the dynamic and active nature of existence itself. For Aquinas, God is pure 'esse'.

A Latin scholastic phrase meaning 'the subsistent act of being itself.' It is Thomas Aquinas's primary definition for God, signifying that God's essence is to exist and that God is the uncreated source of all existence.
The 'what-ness' of a thing. It defines what something is, its nature, and its form. In all created things, essence limits existence.
The 'that-ness' of a thing; the act by which a being is present and manifest in the universe. In all created things, existence is distinct from essence.

Also known as negative theology, this is a theological approach that emphasizes the inadequacy of human language to describe God. It asserts that we can speak more accurately about what God is *not* than what God *is*.
The quality of being beyond or outside the limits of ordinary human experience, perception, and categories. It describes God as wholly 'other' and incomprehensible.
The quality of being within or inherent in the created world. It describes God as intimately and continuously present in all of creation.

One of Aristotle's Four Causes, it answers the question, 'What or who made this object?' It refers to the agent that brings something into being. The concept of an 'Uncaused First Cause' is central to this topic.
A Latin phrase meaning 'of its own kind' or 'unique in its characteristics.' It is used to describe God's nature as being in a class of its own, unlike any created thing.

entries.content: I watched this video where this guy explains how all of us have, like, some sort of causation loop... you keep asking, like, why am I here? And then you ask another why on top of that. And then at some point, it will lead to, like, a first cause of everything, which is apparently God.

God is the object of study in theology insofar as God is the focus of our discovery... But at the same time, God is also not an object. Because when you talk about an object, you're really referring to something that is literally right in front of you, something that you can define.

What's complicated about God is that we can't think with God precisely and say this, this, only this I'm going to study. Because when you're talking to a God, you're really talking about that ultimate reality.
Looking for God is not like a fish looking for the greatest, most powerful fish ever. It's more like the fish becoming aware of the water it is in.
God is not in the same category as the things a fish understands. God is somewhere outside of the way that this fish thinks.
If we're trying to understand what we mean by God, we have to get out of thinking that God is just some grand object existing in time and space.
God is not a being.
A 'being' (Latin: 'ens') refers to an existing thing or object in the universe that occupies time and space.
When Christians talk about God, we're not talking about a thing, because God is not a being.
Revelation. Faith everybody. Where we are currently at this point where we're discussing that God is not a being.
By being we mean that God is not an existing object in the universe that occupies time and space.
God is not like a chair, not like myself, not like anything that occupies any point in time and space in the universe.
Unlike the Greek gods, of course, who are representations of the volatility of the universe.
Bullivant makes clear in the reading that the most true God transcends natural abilities and does not require a beast.
Does God exist? The answer has to be properly nuanced. You have to clarify in what sense God does not exist, and in what sense God does exist.
Atheists are right in saying that God does not exist because there's no evidence of God. It's not as if you can pick up God's DNA.
The problem is that they often refer to God as just one more being (small letter 'b') in the universe.
God doesn't even belong to the genus of being. God doesn't belong to a category, a genus or a species.
God is not even the greatest of existing things or the greatest of all beings.

If you say that God is the greatest of all beings, he's still in the category of being. For example, if we say that Boris is the greatest student, that means Boris is part of that category of student. God isn't even in the category of existing things.
God exists, but not as one being among many. God doesn't belong to a category.
We can explain God's existence in two ways: 1) God enables existence to take place without being a being, and 2) God is both beyond and within our experience.
According to Aristotle, in order for you to know something, you have to be able to identify its four causes.
The material cause answers the question, 'What is it made of?' For example, a statue is made of stone.
The formal cause answers the question, 'How is it arranged?' What is its form? For example, arranging rock into a sculpture, distinct from a bench or a wall.
The efficient cause answers the question, 'What or who made this object?' For example, the sculptor is the efficient cause of the statue.
The final cause answers the question, 'What is its purpose?' or 'What is the telos?' For example, the purpose of a statue is to memorialize an event.
The principle is that every being has an efficient cause or a source from which it can account for its existence.
For Aristotle, you cannot create an entire chain of causes ad infinitum. At some point, you need to have a first cause that itself has no cause: an uncaused first cause.
Thomas Aquinas took Aristotle's philosophy and said that this first cause, the uncaused first cause, is what we call God.
The way that Aquinas puts it is that God is ipsum esse subsistens, the very act of being itself.
When 'Being' is capitalized, it is a verb. It is not 'ens' (a being, a noun) but 'esse' (the act of being, a verb). For Aquinas, existence is not a passive state, it's active.
Essence refers to what makes something what it is, its 'whatness'. It is related to the formal cause.
Existence refers to the act of a being being present; how an object manifests itself into the universe.
For Thomas Aquinas, what's interesting about God is that God's essence is existence.
When you talk about the essence of something, you say what it is and you say what it is not. Essence defines and therefore limits.
When you say that the essence of something is existence itself, that's unlimited. For Aquinas, God is the very act of being itself.
Everything exists as something, which implies a scope and a limit. Because you exist as this, you cannot exist as that.
What makes God unique (sui generis) is that because God is pure existence, God's nature is not limited. God doesn't exist *as* something; God just exists.
Asserting that God's essence is existence emphasizes God's non-competitive nature.

Beings tend to clash with each other; when one being asserts its power, the weaker is diminished. In contrast, when God asserts God's power, being isn't diminished, it flourishes. Creation takes place.
God's power isn't a threat to humanity. To think so (like some existentialist philosophers) is to hold an egoistic view of God, as a projection of the worst of humanity.
St. Augustine presents a tension in understanding God: God is both transcendental and immanent.
Transcendent means beyond our experience, outside of our categories, outside of our perception. God is completely other.
Islam and Judaism emphasize God's transcendence. In Islam, God is never depicted in art. For Jews, God's name (Yahweh) is not spoken.
To name something is to have some sort of control over it. The reluctance to name God respects his otherness.
Immanent means very much within our experience.
Pantheism is the belief that the sum total of every existing thing is God.
Christianity holds both transcendence and immanence. The Christian claim of the Incarnation (God entering the human experience) emphasizes God's immanence.

Analogy for transcendence/immanence: We may have never met artist Fernando Amorsolo (he is beyond our direct experience), but through his paintings, we can feel his presence (he is within our experience).
Being created in God's image should not be taken literally. It means we are able to create and relate like God.
Receiving Jesus' body as communion bread is an experience of God's immanence. Augustine says, 'You become what you receive.' It is communion, not consumption.
Augustine wrote of God: 'interior intimo meo et superior summo meo'—'More inward than my innermost self and higher than my highest thought.'
God can coexist in us because of God's non-competitive nature. If God is existence itself, and we share in that act of existence (in a limited way), then we are an offshoot of existence itself.
God is always loving. The proof is that the fact we still exist shows that God continues to share the gift of existence.

Not until I entered the Jesuits and began hearing about a different kind of God—a God who was with you in your suffering, a God who took a personal interest in your life, even if you didn't feel that all your problems were solved—did life started to make more sense.
