entries.content: God, falsely so-called
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: A converse case can be made for arguing that it is the Judeo-Christian God who is falsely, or at least misleadingly, so-called. This is because Hellenized Jews stole the already existing Greek descriptive noun 'theos' as a name for Yahweh.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This is a point that the twentieth-century Anglo-Irish Dominican theologian Herbert McCabe was at pains to stress.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: We do not know what we are talking about when we use the word 'God' ... When Jews and Christians came to use the word 'god' it was already lying around meaning something else - I mean it meant something that God certainly could not be, a god. Whatever we are referring to when we use the word 'God' it can no more be a god than it can be a model aeroplane or half-past eleven. (Herbert McCabe, God Still Matters)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 'God', 'Theos', 'Deus' is of course a name borrowed from paganism; we take it out of its proper context, where it is used for talking about the gods, and use it for our own purposes. This is quite a legitimate piece of borrowing and quite safe as long as it does not mislead us into thinking that the God we worship (or don't) is a god . . . He is always dressed verbally in second-hand clothes that don't fit him very well. (Herbert McCabe, God Still Matters)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: What McCabe is doing here is effectively turning Pseudo-Justin's argument on its head. He points out that, in its original context, 'god' (theos) denotes a class of personal, superhuman, supernatural beings, who collectively possess a wide range of impressive, though limited, super powers.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: But by choosing to refer to this One with the word 'god', Greek-speaking Jews - including, most influentially, those translating the Hebrew Scriptures - were using it as a name, rather than as a literal description. Transferred to this new context, the proper noun God became a kind of metaphor.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: There is a sense in which God is like a 'god' (he too is powerful, for example, albeit both far more so, and in a qualitatively different way), just as there is a sense in which God is like 'a mighty fortress' (he is steadfast, and protects those who 'dwell within' him). But the Judeo-Christian God cannot be an actual mighty fortress. And he cannot be, as McCabe reminds us, an actual 'god'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This point may perhaps be more easily grasped if one considers a couple giving their daughter the name of 'Poppy'. They may have many reasons for doing so... Feasibly, however, there is something about actual poppies that makes them think it would be a fitting name for a daughter: their beauty, or gracefulness, for example.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: They are not, of course, using this word as a straightforward description: she is not, and will not be, an actual member of the botanical family Papaveraceae. Instead, the word functions as a kind of metaphor or analogy: by naming her thus, her parents are intimating that they think (or hope) she will be like a poppy in some way.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: And this is, moreover, a point that applies far more widely... a strong case can be made that all human words about the Most True God are condemned to be egregiously lacking.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: St Augustine's comment on our use of the word 'person'... as a description for the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit: 'Because the Father is not the Son... then certainly there are three... But when it is asked "Three what?" then the great poverty from which our language suffers becomes apparent. But the formula "three persons" has been coined, not in order to give a complete explanation by means of it, but in order that we might not be obliged to remain silent.' (On the Trinity, 5.10)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: On arguably similar lines, the thirteenth-century German Dominican theologian Meister Eckhart writes in one of his vernacular sermons: 'let us pray to God that we may be free of God that we may gain the truth and enjoy it eternally.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: One plausible interpretation... is that Eckhart is drawing a distinction here between the Most True God ('let us pray to God'), and our flawed human conceptions of who that God actually is ('that we may be free of "god"'), in the interests both of truth and salvation ('that we may gain the truth and enjoy it eternally').
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: All human ideas about God must necessarily fall short of him. This is, as we have seen, the insight of such exemplary Christians thinkers as Augustine and Eckhart. This strand of Christian thought is sometimes called negative or apophatic theology.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In its strongest expressions, as it may be found in the writings of Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, it asserts that whatever it is that Christians call God, 'It falls neither within the predicate of nonbeing nor of being... It is beyond assertion and denial.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Challenging the adequacy of symbols
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Most, if not all, language about God is symbolic or analogical or mythological. Religious language is perhaps the language of myth and symbols par excellence.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Tillich claims that 'man's ultimate concern must be expressed syınbolically, because symbolic language alone is able to express the ultimate.'
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Tillich argues that 'the true ultimate transcends the realm of finite reality infinitely. Therefore, no finite reality can express it directly and properly.' At best, the finite can only point to the infinite.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: If one grants that religious language never perfectly reflects the ultimate, one is obliged to doubt the full adequacy of the symbols and myths employed, and be motivated to develop more adequate language.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

topics.review:
### The Big Idea: God as Being Itself, Not a Being Among Others

This topic, "God Is Not a Being," is one of the most intellectually challenging and spiritually profound concepts in Christian theology. At its core, it addresses a fundamental misunderstanding about God's nature. Often, when we think of God, we imagine a super-powerful entity—the biggest, oldest, and strongest *thing* in the universe. We might picture an old man on a throne or a cosmic force. This topic argues that this entire way of thinking is flawed. The Christian theological tradition, particularly as articulated by thinkers like Thomas Aquinas, insists that God is not the supreme 'being' in the category of existing things; rather, God is the very *act of Being itself*. This distinction is crucial because it moves us away from distorted, mythological images of God and toward a more mature understanding that can withstand philosophical and existential scrutiny. It helps explain God's relationship to the world, not as a competing entity, but as the ground and source of all reality.

### The Philosophical Foundation: From Aristotle to Aquinas

To grasp this concept, we must first turn to ancient Greek philosophy, specifically to Aristotle and his theory of the Four Causes. Aristotle argued that to fully understand any object, one must know its: 
1.  **Material Cause:** What it is made of (e.g., the stone of a statue).
2.  **Formal Cause:** Its form or structure (e.g., the shape of the statue).
3.  **Efficient Cause:** What or who brought it into being (e.g., the sculptor).
4.  **Final Cause (Telos):** Its purpose or end (e.g., to memorialize an event).

Aristotle observed that everything in the universe has an efficient cause; something must have caused it to exist. However, this chain of causation cannot go on infinitely backward (ad infinitum). Logically, there must be a starting point: a **First Cause** that is itself **uncaused**. This Uncaused Cause is the ultimate source of all other existence.

Centuries later, the Christian theologian St. Thomas Aquinas integrated this Aristotelian philosophy into theology. He identified this Uncaused First Cause with the God of Christian faith. But Aquinas went further. He wasn't just saying God was the first domino in a chain. He described God with the Latin phrase **_ipsum esse subsistens_**, which translates to "the very act of being itself." 

### Essence vs. Existence: What Makes God Unique

To understand _ipsum esse subsistens_, we must understand the scholastic distinction between *essence* and *existence*. 
*   **Essence** is the 'what-ness' of a thing. It defines what something is and, by defining it, also limits it. The essence of a chair is 'chair-ness,' which means it is not a table or a person. 
*   **Existence** is the 'that-ness' of a thing—the simple fact *that it is*. 

In every created thing, from a person to a star to a thought, essence and existence are separate. You exist *as* something, and that 'something' (your essence) defines and limits you. 

For Aquinas, God is unique (**_sui generis_**) because in God alone, **essence IS existence**. God doesn't exist *as* a particular type of thing; God's very nature is 'to be'. God is not a noun (_ens_, a being) but a verb (_esse_, the act of being). This means God is unlimited. You cannot ask 'what' God is in the same way you ask about other things, because God's 'what-ness' is pure 'is-ness'. This is why theologians say God doesn't belong to any category, genus, or species. To say God is the 'greatest of all beings' is still a mistake, as it places God within the category of 'beings,' even at the top. It's like saying the author of a book is the most important character; the truth is, the author is on a completely different level of reality from the characters.

### Implications of God as _Esse_

This understanding has profound implications. First, it leads to the concept of **God's non-competitive nature**. Beings in the universe often compete for resources, power, and even existence itself. When one being asserts its power, another may be diminished. But if God is Being-itself, then God's power does not threaten or diminish creation. Instead, God's power *enables* creation to be. The more God acts, the more created things flourish and become what they were meant to be. To see God as a rival to human freedom is a distorted image, projecting our own competitive nature onto God.

Second, it helps us understand the paradox of God's **transcendence and immanence**, a tension articulated beautifully by St. Augustine. God is **transcendent**: beyond our experience, categories, and comprehension ('higher than my highest thought'). This is the 'otherness' of God, respected in traditions like Judaism and Islam that forbid depicting God or speaking God's name. As the saying goes, 'If you understand, it's not God.' At the same time, God is radically **immanent**: intimately present within all of creation ('more inward than my innermost self'). The analogy of the fish in the water is apt: the fish is so completely surrounded by water that it is unaware of it. God isn't the biggest fish; God is the water. The Christian doctrine of the Incarnation—God becoming human in Jesus—is the ultimate statement of God's immanence.

### The Poverty of Language: Apophatic Theology

If God is beyond all our categories, how can we even speak about God? Thinkers like Herbert McCabe, Paul Tillich, and Meister Eckhart highlight the severe limitations of our language. McCabe points out that the word 'God' (_theos_) itself was borrowed from paganism, where it referred to a class of powerful but limited beings. When Judeo-Christian thought uses this word, it's used as a metaphor, like 'a mighty fortress,' not a literal description. Our words for God are, as McCabe says, 'second-hand clothes that don't fit him very well.' 

This leads to **apophatic (or negative) theology**, which emphasizes speaking of God in terms of what God is *not*. Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, a key figure in this tradition, stated that God is 'beyond assertion and denial.' Meister Eckhart's radical prayer—'let us pray to God that we may be free of God'—is a plea to be freed from our own limited, idolatrous images of God to encounter the true God. This is not about emptying our minds of God, but about acknowledging that our finite concepts can never contain the infinite reality of God, who is Being itself.
keyTakeaways: God is not a 'being' (Latin: ens), which is an existing object in the universe, but is the very act of Being itself (Latin: esse).
Thomas Aquinas defined God as 'ipsum esse subsistens,' meaning in God alone, essence (what something is) and existence (that it is) are identical.
Because God's essence is existence, God is unlimited and does not belong to any category of things, not even 'the greatest of all beings.'
This understanding leads to a 'non-competitive' view of God, where God's power enables creation to flourish rather than diminishing it.

God is simultaneously transcendent (beyond our concepts and categories) and immanent (intimately present within all of reality), as illustrated by the analogy of a fish being unaware of the water it is in.
The 'Uncaused First Cause' from Aristotle's philosophy is the philosophical starting point that Aquinas identifies with the Christian God.
All human language about God is metaphorical and inadequate. Apophatic (negative) theology stresses that we can more accurately say what God is *not* than what God *is*.
Atheists who deny God's existence are often correctly denying the existence of a mythological 'being' in the sky, a concept that classical theology also rejects.

glossary.definition: A Latin term for an existing thing or object that occupies time and space. It refers to any specific entity within the universe, from a person to a planet. In this topic, it's what God is NOT.
A Latin term for the very act of 'to be.' It is understood as a verb, representing the dynamic and active nature of existence itself. For Aquinas, God is pure 'esse'.

A Latin scholastic phrase meaning 'the subsistent act of being itself.' It is Thomas Aquinas's primary definition for God, signifying that God's essence is to exist and that God is the uncreated source of all existence.
The 'what-ness' of a thing. It defines what something is, its nature, and its form. In all created things, essence limits existence.
The 'that-ness' of a thing; the act by which a being is present and manifest in the universe. In all created things, existence is distinct from essence.

Also known as negative theology, this is a theological approach that emphasizes the inadequacy of human language to describe God. It asserts that we can speak more accurately about what God is *not* than what God *is*.
The quality of being beyond or outside the limits of ordinary human experience, perception, and categories. It describes God as wholly 'other' and incomprehensible.
The quality of being within or inherent in the created world. It describes God as intimately and continuously present in all of creation.

One of Aristotle's Four Causes, it answers the question, 'What or who made this object?' It refers to the agent that brings something into being. The concept of an 'Uncaused First Cause' is central to this topic.
A Latin phrase meaning 'of its own kind' or 'unique in its characteristics.' It is used to describe God's nature as being in a class of its own, unlike any created thing.

studyTips: Focus on the core distinction: 'being' (ens) as a noun vs. 'Being' (esse) as a verb. Write it down and explain it in your own words. This is the central pivot of the entire topic.
Create a simple chart connecting Aristotle's 'Uncaused First Cause' to Aquinas's 'ipsum esse subsistens.' This shows the philosophical-theological lineage of the idea.
The 'fish in the water' analogy is key. Be able to explain how it illustrates God's immanence and why looking for God as 'the biggest fish' is a mistake.

Don't be afraid of the paradox. The idea that God is both transcendent AND immanent is a core tenet. Use Augustine's quote 'More inward than my innermost self and higher than my highest thought' as a memory aid.
When you see the names McCabe, Eckhart, or Pseudo-Dionysius, think 'language is not enough.' Connect them to apophatic theology and the idea that our concepts of God are always incomplete.

Understand that from this theological perspective, a certain kind of atheism is correct. The argument isn't with someone who says 'I don't believe in a bearded man in the sky,' but with someone who denies the ground of Being itself.

connections.relationship: This topic provides the direct theological and philosophical antidote to distorted images. By defining God as 'Being itself' (esse) rather than 'a being' (ens), it corrects images of God as a cosmic rival, a divine puppeteer, or a fickle Olympian god.
connections.example: If you hold a distorted image of God as a perfectionist judge who is constantly disappointed, understanding God as non-competitive Being-itself, whose power enables you to flourish, completely reframes that image.

connections.relationship: The concept of God as 'ipsum esse subsistens' is not considered a purely human invention but a truth that God reveals about God's own nature. It is understood through a combination of faith and reason, a key aspect of how Revelation works.
connections.example: The biblical revelation of God's name to Moses as 'I AM WHO I AM' (Exodus 3:14) has been theologically interpreted for centuries as a hint toward this very idea: God's nature is simply 'to be'.

connections.relationship: The human search for meaning, the endless loop of asking 'Why?', finds its ultimate resting point here. The desire for an ultimate cause is fulfilled not by a first object, but by the source of all existence.
connections.example: The student's initial thought about a 'causation loop' leading to a 'first cause' is the exact starting point that Aristotle and Aquinas used. This topic provides the sophisticated theological answer to that initial human wonder.

connections.relationship: Defining God as a 'being' reduces God to a problem to be solved or an object to be analyzed. Defining God as 'Being itself' elevates God to the level of a Mystery to be participated in, which can never be fully grasped by the mind.
connections.example: Trying to prove the existence of 'a being' called God with empirical evidence is treating God as a problem. Contemplating the reality of 'Being itself' in which we 'live and move and have our being' is to engage with Mystery.

connections.relationship: Accepting that God is not an object we can empirically verify but is the very ground of existence requires a move beyond pure reason into the realm of faith. Faith is the trust in this reality that is both beyond us (transcendent) and within us (immanent).
connections.example: You cannot find 'God's DNA' because God is not a being with DNA. Faith is the personal assent to the reality of God as the 'esse' that makes DNA, and everything else, possible.

connections.relationship: This concept directly connects to Meister Eckhart's prayer 'to be free of God.' Christian 'atheism' is the necessary rejection of our own flawed, finite, and idolatrous concepts of 'gods' (as beings) in order to be open to the true, incomprehensible God (as Being itself).
connections.example: When Eckhart asks to be 'free of God,' he is engaging in Christian atheism. He is not rejecting the ultimate reality, but the inadequate mental idol he has constructed of that reality, which is exactly what the 'God is not a being' argument encourages.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain the theological distinction between God as a 'being' (ens) and God as 'Being' (esse). Why is this distinction important?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: A 'being' (ens) is a specific, existing object within the universe, defined and limited by its nature. In contrast, 'Being' (esse) is the pure and unlimited act of existence itself. This distinction is crucial because it moves God from being the 'biggest thing' in the universe to being the very ground of reality, avoiding mythological and competitive images of God.

examPrepQuestions.question: How did Thomas Aquinas use Aristotle's philosophy to develop his concept of God as 'ipsum esse subsistens'?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Aquinas took Aristotle's logical conclusion that there must be an 'Uncaused First Cause' to avoid an infinite regress of causation. He then identified this First Cause with the Christian God and further defined it as 'ipsum esse subsistens' (the act of being itself), meaning a reality whose very essence is to exist, making it the source of existence for all created things.

examPrepQuestions.question: Briefly explain God's 'non-competitive' nature and how it follows from the idea that God's essence is existence.
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Because God is the act of Being itself, God's power does not compete with creation's power. Unlike finite beings that can diminish each other, God's power is enabling. The more God acts, the more creation flourishes and becomes itself, because it is receiving a greater share in the very existence that God is.

quizQuestions.question: According to Thomas Aquinas, what is the primary definition of God?
choices: A) The greatest of all beings
B) The Unmoved Mover
C) Ipsum esse subsistens (the act of being itself)
D) The sum total of all existing things
quizQuestions.explanation: C is correct. Aquinas defined God as 'ipsum esse subsistens,' meaning His essence is existence. 'The greatest of all beings' (A) is incorrect because it still places God in the category of 'beings.' 'The Unmoved Mover' (B) is one of Aquinas's arguments for God but not his core definition. 'The sum total of all things' (D) describes pantheism, not classical Christian theology.

quizQuestions.question: The analogy of a 'fish becoming aware of the water' is used to illustrate which theological concept?
choices: A) God's transcendence
B) God's immanence
C) Apophatic theology
D) The Final Cause
quizQuestions.explanation: B is correct. The analogy illustrates God's immanence—His intimate, all-encompassing presence that we are often unaware of. Transcendence (A) refers to God being beyond us. Apophatic theology (C) is about the limits of language. The Final Cause (D) is a concept from Aristotle about purpose.

quizQuestions.question: Saying that 'God's essence is existence' implies that God is:
choices: A) Limited by His nature
B) A complex and defined being
C) The first being in a long chain of beings
D) Unlimited and not part of any category
quizQuestions.explanation: D is correct. For all other things, essence limits existence. Because God's essence IS existence, God is by definition unlimited and cannot be categorized alongside created things.

quizQuestions.question: Which thinker emphasized the 'poverty' of our language for God, noting that even the word 'God' was borrowed from paganism and used as a metaphor?
choices: A) St. Augustine
B) Thomas Aquinas
C) Meister Eckhart
D) Herbert McCabe
quizQuestions.explanation: D is correct. Herbert McCabe is cited in the notes for making this specific point, describing our words for God as 'second-hand clothes that don't fit him very well.' While the others also discussed the limits of language, McCabe is known for this particular argument about the word 'theos'.

quizQuestions.question: The theological term for describing God by what He is NOT is:
choices: A) Kataphatic theology
B) Apophatic theology
C) Pantheistic theology
D) Scholastic theology
quizQuestions.explanation: B is correct. Apophatic, or negative, theology focuses on what cannot be said about God due to His transcendence and the limits of human language. Kataphatic theology (A) is its opposite, making positive assertions about God.

quizQuestions.question: In Aristotelian terms, the sculptor who carves a statue is its:
choices: A) Material Cause
B) Formal Cause
C) Efficient Cause
D) Final Cause
quizQuestions.explanation: C is correct. The Efficient Cause is the agent that brings something into being. The Material Cause is the marble (A), the Formal Cause is the statue's shape (B), and the Final Cause is its purpose (D).

quizQuestions.question: The concept of God's 'non-competitive' nature means that:
choices: A) God does not interfere in human affairs.
B) God's power enables creation to flourish, not be diminished.
C) God is equal to other spiritual beings.
D) Humans should not try to compete with God.
quizQuestions.explanation: B is correct. Because God is Being-itself, His power is not a zero-sum game. When God acts, creation becomes 'more,' not 'less.' This refutes the idea of God as a rival to humanity.

quizQuestions.question: Augustine's phrase 'More inward than my innermost self and higher than my highest thought' captures the paradox of God's:
choices: A) Essence and Existence
B) Goodness and Justice
C) Immanence and Transcendence
D) Past and Future
quizQuestions.explanation: C is correct. 'More inward than my innermost self' points to God's radical immanence, while 'higher than my highest thought' points to His ultimate transcendence.

quizQuestions.question: According to the notes, why might an atheist be 'right' in saying God does not exist?
choices: A) Because God is a purely subjective feeling.
B) Because classical theology agrees that God does not exist as one more being or object in the universe.
C) Because there is no philosophical argument for God's existence.
D) Because faith has been disproven by science.
quizQuestions.explanation: B is correct. The notes suggest that atheists who deny the existence of a 'being' in the sky are rejecting an idol that classical theology also rejects. The issue is a misunderstanding of what 'God' refers to.

quizQuestions.question: What is the Latin term for a specific, existing thing, which God is said NOT to be?
choices: A) Esse
B) Telos
C) Ens
D) Deus
quizQuestions.explanation: C is correct. 'Ens' is the Latin word for a being or an existing thing. 'Esse' (A) is the act of being, which God IS. 'Telos' (B) is the Greek word for purpose. 'Deus' (D) is the Latin word for God.

topics.title: Revelation

glossaryTerms.definition: God's personal self-communication to humanity, inviting people into a relationship. It is not merely the transmission of information but the sharing of God's own self.
The study of God, derived from the Greek words 'theos' (God) and 'logos' (word, study, meaningful discussion). It is the discipline of making sense of God's revelation.

A rich Greek word meaning 'word, study, talk, order, or meaning.' In theology, it refers to a meaningful discussion or the rational principle of the universe, and in Christian theology, it identifies Jesus Christ.
The Greek word for God, and the root of words like 'theology' and 'theism'.
The Christian doctrine that God entered the human condition and became a human being in the person of Jesus Christ, who is described as the 'image of the invisible God'.

A Latin phrase by St. Augustine meaning, 'If you understand, then it is not God.' It signifies that God is an infinite mystery, and any attempt to fully comprehend or define God falls short of the reality of God.

A philosophical analogy used in the course to describe the human condition of being trapped by 'shadowy, ego-driven images of God,' and the educational/spiritual journey of moving toward the true source of light and reality.

The two complementary ways God communicates, identified in the notes as 'Deeds' (God's saving actions in history) and 'Words' (the prophetic speech or scripture that explains the meaning of those actions).
The human response of trust, surrender, and commitment to God's self-communication (Revelation). It is the act of making sense of and entering into the relationship offered by God.
In theology, not an unsolvable problem, but a reality so profound and deep that it is inexhaustible. God is a Mystery to be entered into, not a problem to be solved.

entries.content: We're now in the third module... the title of this module is Revelation: God Communicating God Himself and us making sense of that communication Revelation.
The word theology, as you would know, comes from two Greek words, the key of which is the word theos (God)... The logi part comes from the Greek word logos, which means study, talk, order or meaning.
The word logos is actually a very rich word in Greek, which refers to a meaningful discussion about a certain topic.
We can say that God really is the proper object of study in theology. God is the proper object of study.

We're going to talk about God in three ways. First, we're going to talk about God from an experiential angle... Then we're going to talk about God from a philosophical angle... And then finally... from a theological angle.
The objective of the midterm exam is for students to demonstrate their understanding of the first half of the course by making connections from one class concept to the other.
Module 3: Revelation and Faith, where we are studying who God is, what God communicates and how we are to make sense of this communication.

Plato's Allegory of the Cave describes people who have only known the world through shadowy images on a cave wall. The point of education is to move beyond these shadowy images to see the world as it really is.
The invitation is to consider that God is always beyond our shadowy, ego-driven images of Him.
Christianity claims God can be seen and imaged because of the Incarnation. It's not that we control God, but that God in his graciousness enters the human condition to become accessible.
St. Paul says that Jesus is the image of the invisible God.
Doctrines and beliefs are not pure speculation, but our imperfect attempts at articulating a mystery and making sense of God's revelation.

When something profound happens in their emotional lives, something that touches them deeply, they reject the possibility that it could be a sign of God's activity. Their intellect may become a wall that closes off their hearts to experiences of God's presence.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: They may also be unwilling to attribute to God anything that the believer might see as an obvious example of God's presence.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content:
It's like the story of the atheist caught in a flood. The fellow figures that the flood threatening his house is the chance to prove conclusively whether God exists. So he says to himself, If there is a God, I will ask him for help, and he will save me. When he hears a warning on the radio advising listeners to move to higher ground, he ignores it. If there is a God, he will save me, he thinks. Next, a firefighter knocks on his door to warn him to evacuate. “If there is a God, he will save me,” he says to the firefighter. When the floodwaters rise, the man climbs to the second floor. The coast guard boat motors by his window and offers him rescue. “If there is a God, he will save me,” he says and refuses help from the coast guard. Finally, he ends up on the roof, with the waters rising around him. A police helicopter hovers over the house and drops a rope to climb. “If there is a God, he will save me!" he shouts over the roar of the helicopter's blades. Suddenly a giant wave sweeps over him, and the man drowns and finds himself in heaven. When God comes to welcome him, the atheist is first surprised. And then furious. “Why didn't you save me?” he asks.

“What do you mean?” says God. “I sent the firefighter, the coast guard, and the police officer, and you still wouldn't listen!"

entries.content: Experiencing God also comes through personal interactions within the community. Sure, God communicates through private, intimate moments—as in prayer or reading of sacred texts— but God also enters into relationships with us through others in a faith community. Finding God often happens in the midst of a community—with a 'we' as often as an 'I.' For many people this community is a church, a synagogue, or a mosque. Or more broadly, religion.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: We try to be formed and held and kept by him, but instead he offers us freedom. And now when I try to know his will, his kindness floods me, his great love overwhelms me, and I hear him whisper, Surprise me.

revelation & faith
Revelation and faith can be approached from three angles: 1. from an experiential angle, 2. from a theological angle, and 3. from a philosophical angle.

topics.review:
Welcome to our review session for the topic of Revelation, which is a cornerstone of our third module, 'Images and Revelation'. This topic is crucial as it bridges our understanding of God with how we, as human beings, come to know and relate to the Divine. The very word 'theology'—from the Greek 'theos' (God) and 'logos' (meaningful talk, study, order)—sets the stage. We are engaged in a meaningful discussion about God, who is the proper object of our study.

At its core, Revelation is defined as God's self-communication. This is the single most important idea to grasp. Revelation is not primarily God handing down a list of rules, a set of abstract concepts, or a textbook of information. Instead, it is the personal, dynamic act of God communicating God's very self to humanity. A helpful analogy from our notes is the difference between 'knowing about' a person—possessing facts and data—and 'knowing them personally' through a relationship. Revelation is about the latter. It is an invitation into a relationship, much like the modern term 'situationship' was used to describe a form of communication that 'speaks without words,' highlighting the relational and experiential nature of this divine encounter. The handwritten note to 'meditate' on Revelation underscores this point; it’s something to be experienced, not just memorized.

The course approaches this topic from three angles: experiential, philosophical, and theological. Experientially, God's revelation is not confined to grand, supernatural events. The powerful story of the atheist in the flood serves as a key illustration. The man waits for a dramatic, undeniable rescue from God, all while rejecting the real, tangible help offered to him: a radio warning, a firefighter, a coast guard boat, and a police helicopter. God's response in the story, 'I sent the firefighter, the coast guard, and the police officer, and you still wouldn't listen!', teaches a profound lesson. Revelation is often mediated through the ordinary, through other people, and through the events of our daily lives. God enters into relationship with us through others in our community—a 'we' as much as an 'I'.

From a philosophical perspective, Plato's Allegory of the Cave provides a powerful framework for understanding our starting point. Like the prisoners who mistake shadows for reality, we often begin with 'shadowy, ego-driven images' of God. These images can be shaped by our fears, our desires for control, or our limited understanding. Revelation is the process of being led out of the cave, away from these distorted shadows, toward a more authentic encounter with the Divine. This journey requires humility and an openness to having our preconceptions challenged. The Latin phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus'—'If you understand, then it is not God'—captures this perfectly. If we believe we have fully captured or defined God, we have merely created an idol, a shadow on the cave wall. The true God is always a Mystery, always beyond our complete grasp.

This leads us to the theological angle, which finds its ultimate expression in the Christian claim of the Incarnation. How can the invisible God be seen? St. Paul states that Jesus is 'the image of the invisible God.' Christianity asserts that God, in His grace, does not remain distant but enters the human condition to become accessible. This is the climax of God's self-revelation. Through Jesus, God communicates not just through actions and words, but through a person. The two complementary modes of Revelation—'Deeds' (demonstrations of God's power and love in history) and 'Words' (the prophetic or scriptural expressions that interpret those deeds)—find their perfect unity in the life, death, and resurrection of Christ. Consequently, our doctrines and beliefs are not mere human speculation; they are our 'imperfect attempts at articulating a mystery' and making sense of this profound revelation.

However, receiving this revelation is not automatic. There are significant obstacles. Our own intellect can become a 'wall that closes off our hearts' if we refuse to attribute any deep emotional or personal experience to God's activity. We may rationalize away any sense of the sacred. This ties into the concept of 'Christian Atheism,' where one might reject the simplistic, controlling, or punishing images of God they were taught, which is a necessary step toward discovering the true God of revelation. The God revealed is not a cosmic puppeteer whose will we must rigidly follow, but a God of love who offers freedom and relationship. As one poignant reflection in our notes concludes, when seeking God's will, the overwhelming response is love and a whisper: 'Surprise me.' This captures the essence of a faith responding to revelation—it is not about control and certainty, but about a loving, dynamic, and surprising relationship with the God who has first reached out to us.
keyTakeaways: Revelation is not God communicating information, but God's personal self-communication, inviting us into a relationship.
God's revelation is often mediated through ordinary means, people, and community, as illustrated by the story of the man in the flood ignoring the firefighter and coast guard.
Plato's Allegory of the Cave is an analogy for moving beyond our 'shadowy, ego-driven images' of God to encounter the truer, more mysterious reality of the Divine.

The Latin phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' ('If you understand, it is not God') teaches that God is an inexhaustible Mystery, and if we think we have fully grasped God, we have settled for an idol.
Revelation occurs through two complementary modes: 'Deeds' (God's actions in history) and 'Words' (expressions that interpret the meaning of those actions).
For Christians, the Incarnation is the ultimate form of Revelation, where Jesus Christ is 'the image of the invisible God' (St. Paul), making the divine fully accessible in a person.
Our intellect can be an obstacle to receiving revelation if it becomes a 'wall' that dismisses profound personal or emotional experiences as potential encounters with God.
Doctrines and beliefs are not perfect containers of God, but are our 'imperfect attempts at articulating a mystery' and making sense of God's self-communication.

glossary.definition: God's personal self-communication to humanity, inviting people into a relationship. It is not merely the transmission of information but the sharing of God's own self.
The study of God, derived from the Greek words 'theos' (God) and 'logos' (word, study, meaningful discussion). It is the discipline of making sense of God's revelation.

A rich Greek word meaning 'word, study, talk, order, or meaning.' In theology, it refers to a meaningful discussion or the rational principle of the universe, and in Christian theology, it identifies Jesus Christ.
The Greek word for God, and the root of words like 'theology' and 'theism'.
The Christian doctrine that God entered the human condition and became a human being in the person of Jesus Christ, who is described as the 'image of the invisible God'.

A Latin phrase by St. Augustine meaning, 'If you understand, then it is not God.' It signifies that God is an infinite mystery, and any attempt to fully comprehend or define God falls short of the reality of God.

A philosophical analogy used in the course to describe the human condition of being trapped by 'shadowy, ego-driven images of God,' and the educational/spiritual journey of moving toward the true source of light and reality.

The two complementary ways God communicates, identified in the notes as 'Deeds' (God's saving actions in history) and 'Words' (the prophetic speech or scripture that explains the meaning of those actions).
The human response of trust, surrender, and commitment to God's self-communication (Revelation). It is the act of making sense of and entering into the relationship offered by God.
In theology, not an unsolvable problem, but a reality so profound and deep that it is inexhaustible. God is a Mystery to be entered into, not a problem to be solved.

studyTips: Re-tell the story of the man in the flood in your own words. Focus on identifying the ordinary means of salvation he rejects. Practice explaining how this illustrates God revealing Himself through everyday people and events, not just overt miracles.

Create a simple diagram of Plato's Cave. Label the prisoners, the shadows, the fire, and the path out of the cave. Next to each label, write its theological equivalent (e.g., Prisoners = Humanity, Shadows = Distorted Images of God, Path out = Revelation).

Memorize the phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus.' Practice explaining its meaning by connecting it to the topic of 'Mystery vs. Problem' from SET1. If God were a problem, you could solve it. As a Mystery, you can't, and that's the point.

Use the 'Deeds and Words' framework. Think of a biblical story (like the Exodus). Identify the 'Deed' (parting the Red Sea) and the 'Word' (God's command to Moses, the giving of the Law) that explains the event's meaning. This shows how they work together.

Reflect on the prompt 'Surprise me.' Write a short paragraph on how this idea challenges a transactional view of God (i.e., 'If I do X, God will do Y') and promotes a relational understanding of faith in response to Revelation.

Don't just define Revelation as 'communication'. Be more specific: 'God's SELF-communication'. Contrast this with 'communicating information'. Use the analogy of 'knowing about' a celebrity versus 'knowing' your best friend.

connections.relationship: Revelation presents God not as a problem to be solved but as a Mystery to be entered into. If God could be fully explained by revelation, God would be a problem; instead, revelation deepens our sense of God's inexhaustible Mystery.
connections.example: The quote 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' directly shows this. The goal of revelation isn't to give a final answer (solving a problem) but to invite us into a relationship with a reality that is beyond our full comprehension (a Mystery).

connections.relationship: Revelation is God's way of correcting our distorted, 'shadowy' images. It is the process of moving from our own flawed projections to the reality God reveals about Godself.
connections.example: Plato's Allegory of the Cave is the primary example used for this. The prisoners' 'distorted image' is the shadow on the wall. Revelation is the journey out of the cave to see the true forms, culminating in the sun (a metaphor for the Good, or God).

connections.relationship: Revelation and Faith are two sides of the same coin. Revelation is God's initiative—the act of self-communication. Faith is the human response—the act of receiving, trusting, and living according to that communication.
connections.example: In the story of the man in the flood, God's revelation came through the firefighter, the boat, etc. The man's lack of faith was his refusal to accept and act on that revelation.

connections.relationship: The idea that Revelation is God's 'self-communication' supports the concept that God is not just another being. A being could communicate facts about itself, but Being-itself communicates its very essence. Revelation is an encounter with the ground of all being, not just a powerful entity.
connections.example: When the notes say Revelation is 'not about concepts or images', it suggests we are encountering something other than a finite object we can categorize. This aligns with the idea that God is not one being among many.

connections.relationship: Christian 'atheism' is the rejection of false or idolatrous images of God. Revelation is the positive process that makes this possible, by providing a more authentic alternative to the idols we reject.
connections.example: A person might reject a 'vending machine' God who is supposed to grant wishes on demand. This 'atheistic' act opens them to the God of Revelation who whispers 'Surprise me,' offering freedom and relationship instead of transactions.

connections.relationship: A true encounter with God's Revelation should not lead to the arrogance of absolute certainty, but to a profound sense of Wonder. It opens us up to the magnitude and mystery of the Divine.
connections.example: Instead of feeling like they have God figured out, a person responding to revelation is more likely to feel 'overwhelmed' by God's 'great love,' as the notes describe. This feeling is a form of wonder.

connections.relationship: The human 'Desire' for truth, meaning, and transcendence is the starting point that God's Revelation addresses. God's self-communication is the divine response to this innate human longing.
connections.example: Our entire study of theology begins with this human desire (the 'Human Starting Point' of SET1). Revelation is the narrative of how God meets that desire not with a formula, but with Godself.

examPrepQuestions.question: Using Plato's Allegory of the Cave and the story of the man in the flood, explain how our personal perceptions can be obstacles to receiving God's revelation. How does God attempt to overcome these obstacles?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: Plato's Allegory of the Cave illustrates that we are often like prisoners who mistake 'shadowy, ego-driven images' for the reality of God. Our perceptions are limited and distorted. The story of the man in the flood provides a narrative example of this: the man has a fixed, preconceived notion of how God *should* act (a dramatic, supernatural intervention) which becomes an obstacle, making him blind to the real, ordinary ways God is trying to communicate and save him (radio, firefighter, boat, helicopter). God attempts to overcome these obstacles not by force, but by persistently communicating through various means. The ultimate Christian answer is the Incarnation, where God enters our 'cave' in the person of Jesus, becoming an accessible 'image of the invisible God' to lead us out toward the true light.

examPrepQuestions.question: Explain the statement 'Revelation is not about concepts, but God communicating Godself.' In your answer, incorporate the two complementary modes of revelation ('Deeds' and 'Words').
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: This statement means that the primary purpose of revelation is not to give us a list of facts or doctrines, but to invite us into a personal relationship with God. It's the difference between 'knowing about' someone and 'knowing' them personally. This divine self-communication happens in two complementary modes. 'Deeds' are God's saving actions in human history (e.g., the Exodus from Egypt). These are powerful demonstrations of God's character and love. However, without interpretation, their meaning might be lost. So, 'Words'—prophetic speech, scripture, and teachings—accompany the deeds to explain their significance and reveal the God who is acting. Both deeds and words work together, culminating in Jesus Christ, who is both God's ultimate 'Deed' (the act of Incarnation) and God's ultimate 'Word' (the fullness of what God has to say).

examPrepQuestions.question: What does the Latin phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' mean, and how does it relate to the course's understanding of Revelation and Mystery?
examPrepQuestions.modelAnswer: 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' translates to 'If you understand, it is not God.' This means that if we think we have fully grasped, defined, or contained God in our minds or doctrines, what we are holding onto is no longer the true, living God, but a smaller, finite idol of our own making. This relates directly to the distinction between a 'Problem' and a 'Mystery'. A problem can be solved and fully understood. A mystery, in the theological sense, is a reality so deep and rich that it is inexhaustible; we can know it more and more, but never exhaust it. Revelation, therefore, does not 'solve' the problem of God. Instead, it draws us deeper into the Mystery of God, continually expanding our understanding while reminding us that God is always greater than our concepts.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, the primary definition of Revelation is:
choices: A) A set of divine laws and doctrines.
B) A series of supernatural events and miracles.
C) God's personal self-communication to humanity.
D) A book of secrets about the end of the world.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core concept emphasized is that Revelation is God communicating God's own self, not just information or rules.

quizQuestions.question: Plato's Allegory of the Cave is used as an analogy to illustrate:
choices: A) The historical accuracy of the Bible.
B) The need to move beyond our 'shadowy, ego-driven images' of God.
C) The superiority of philosophical reason over faith.
D) That God is ultimately unknowable and distant.
quizQuestions.explanation: The allegory describes moving from shadows (distorted images) to reality, which is used to explain the journey from our flawed ideas of God to a more authentic encounter through revelation.

quizQuestions.question: In the story of the atheist in the flood, what is the main point about how God communicates?
choices: A) God only communicates through direct, miraculous intervention.
B) God communicates through ordinary means and people, which we can easily miss.
C) God does not communicate with non-believers.
D) One must pray for a specific kind of help for God to answer.
quizQuestions.explanation: The story's punchline is that God *did* send help via a firefighter, coast guard, and police, but the man's narrow expectations caused him to reject it. God often works through the mundane.

quizQuestions.question: The Latin phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' warns against what?
choices: A) The danger of believing you have fully understood or defined God.
B) The futility of studying theology.
C) The sin of not attending church.
D) The mistake of translating sacred texts.
quizQuestions.explanation: It means 'If you understand, it is not God,' cautioning that God is an infinite mystery, and our finite minds cannot contain Him. To think so is to create an idol.

quizQuestions.question: The two complementary modes of Revelation mentioned in the notes are:
choices: A) Prayer and Meditation.
B) Old Testament and New Testament.
C) Deeds and Words.
D) Individual Experience and Community Worship.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes explicitly state that Revelation occurs through 'Deeds (demonstration) and Words (expression),' which are complementary.

quizQuestions.question: According to St. Paul, the ultimate 'image of the invisible God' is:
choices: A) The created world.
B) The human soul.
C) The Holy Scripture.
D) Jesus Christ.
quizQuestions.explanation: The lecture notes quote St. Paul, stating, 'Jesus is the image of the invisible God,' highlighting the centrality of the Incarnation in Christian revelation.

quizQuestions.question: The notes suggest that one's intellect can become a 'wall' that blocks revelation when it:
choices: A) Engages in rigorous philosophical inquiry.
B) Studies the original languages of the Bible.
C) Learns about other world religions.
D) Dismisses deep emotional experiences as signs of God's activity.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source material mentions that some people's intellect 'closes off their hearts' by rejecting the possibility that profound emotional moments could be encounters with God.

quizQuestions.question: The relationship between Revelation and Faith is best described as:
choices: A) Revelation is God's initiative, and Faith is the human response.
B) Faith is a prerequisite for God to grant Revelation.
C) Revelation and Faith are identical concepts.
D) Revelation is for the community, while Faith is for the individual.
quizQuestions.explanation: The module title is 'Revelation and Faith,' and the dynamic is that God initiates communication (Revelation) and humans respond in trust and commitment (Faith).

quizQuestions.question: According to the notes, theological doctrines and beliefs are considered:
choices: A) Pure human speculation with no divine basis.
B) Perfect and infallible containers of divine truth.
C) Imperfect but necessary attempts to articulate the mystery of revelation.
D) Outdated concepts that should be discarded.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes state that doctrines are our 'imperfect attempts at articulating a mystery and making sense of God's revelation.'

quizQuestions.question: The final reflection ending with the whisper 'Surprise me' suggests that the relationship with the God of revelation is one of:
choices: A) Strict obedience to a detailed plan.
B) Fear and trembling before a powerful judge.
C) Freedom, love, and unexpectedness.
D) Intellectual certainty and absolute clarity.
quizQuestions.explanation: The quote contrasts a rigid attempt to 'know his will' with being 'overwhelmed' by love and offered freedom. 'Surprise me' implies a dynamic, loving relationship, not a controlled or fearful one.
